Executive Summary

Situation: 

The Victorian Department of Health oversees 77 public health services operating under maintenance models ranging from fully in-house to fully outsourced. This diversity has produced inconsistent asset performance, fragmented reporting, and limited ability to benchmark services, allocate capital effectively, or identify where clinical and safety risks are emerging.

Without standardised maintenance KPIs, the Department cannot systematically determine where building stock and facilities are deteriorating, where essential services infrastructure — HVAC, medical gases, backup power and nurse call systems — is at risk, or where maintenance teams lack adequate resources to maintain safe, reliable operations.

To address this, the Department has developed a comprehensive KPI framework comprising 12 KPIs across two implementation phases, supported by a 7-level maturity model (Level 0: Ad Hoc through Level 7: Transforming), as detailed in the September 2025 Maintenance Standards Guidelines Performance Monitoring document.

Complication: 

To realise the benefit of the Department’s KPI Framework, it must be ‘operationalised’ - from policy into operational practice across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity, building ages, service profiles, and competing clinical priorities.

Challenge: How can the Department implement its KPI framework in a way that is practical, scalable, and enduring — achieving widespread adoption and value realisation for the services and the Department.   How can this benefit be realised while minimising the additional reporting burden on health service maintenance teams that are already operating under resource constraints?

The CBS Group Approach:

CBS Group brings a senior team drawing upon the practical and pragmatic application of KPIs, ensuring asset performance is actively managed in the context of service delivery and of maintenance constraints.   

Our team understands the challenges in the field; the data limitations, the service delivery pressure, and the bandwidth challenges these teams face.

At the other end, we understand the Department’s KPI framework, the problems it is designed to solve, and are well placed to ensure that these ambitions are met.

CBS brings unrivalled professional asset management expertise to this engagement, coupled with deep experience in health services delivery and facility management.   

Equally importantly, we bring change management to this engagement, acknowledging how important it is to communicate clearly, understand constraints, and demonstrate value to this complex stakeholder group. In similar engagements, we have drawn upon our Collaboration Practice to apply the ISO44001 practices to ensure trust and transparency in contexts like this one.

Our proprietary CAPITAL framework has been successfully deployed to implement performance metrics across diverse organisations with varying maturity levels, delivering substantial validated savings while sustaining or improving asset performance and service delivery.

Our Melbourne-based team of senior asset management, systems engineering, and change management specialists will apply a proven methodology to the Department's health infrastructure context through three core commitments:

segmenting health services by operational maturity — not size or geography — to ensure proportionate, relevant support

co-designing implementation pathways with health services so the framework is owned, not imposed

integrating KPI data capture with existing maintenance systems to minimise administrative burden on operational teams

Our team brings direct experience from Australia's leading health FM organisations — Broadspectrum (now Ventia), Downer, and Cushman Wakefield. Nicole Caranguian designed and built asset management systems for the Royal Adelaide Hospital and Women's and Children's Hospital; Kate Heath developed KPI frameworks for competitive health FM contracts at Broadspectrum. This is not transferred methodology — it is direct health infrastructure experience.

Section 3: Service Provider Proposal and Response 

Organisational Details

Contact Details

Subcontracting: CBS Group does not propose subcontractors for this engagement. All services will be delivered by CBS Group personnel. Our team's combination of asset management methodology expertise (transport infrastructure KPI implementation) with direct health facilities management experience (through team members who have held senior roles at Broadspectrum/Ventia, Downer, and Cushman Wakefield) covers the required scope without the need for external supplementation.

Experience and Capability

Direct Experience and Knowledge

Client Testimonial

"CBS Group's strategic asset management advice has been instrumental in achieving value optimisation across our tunnel portfolio. Their CAPITAL Framework methodology delivered outcomes that exceeded our expectations, and their team's deep understanding of our governance requirements ensures seamless collaboration."

– Transport for NSW Senior Executive

CBS Group is a 100% Australian-owned professional engineering and asset management consultancy established in 2002. The firm employs 12 core specialists and maintains a broader network of approximately 30 expert associates, delivering services to government and blue-chip clients from offices in Melbourne, Sydney, and internationally.

CBS Group brings extensive, directly relevant experience in designing, implementing, and embedding performance-based KPI frameworks across complex public sector asset portfolios. The methodology that has been core to our work — phased KPI rollout across portfolios of assets managed by diverse organisations at varying maturity levels, with the objective of driving performance improvement rather than compliance activity — is what this engagement requires. 

Importantly, our team includes professionals who have held senior roles within Australia's leading health and social infrastructure facilities management organisations — Broadspectrum (now Ventia), Downer, and Cushman Wakefield — who bring direct understanding of how hospital and health facility maintenance is contracted, delivered, measured, and reported at the operational level.

At the heart of our service delivery is the award-winning CAPITAL Framework, our signature methodology for strategic asset management advisory services. With 15 years of refinement through infrastructure project delivery, CAPITAL has achieved $1.049B in validated savings across a $10b asset portfolio – delivering an impressive 31% average optimisation of contract values.

The proven CAPITAL Framework powers our comprehensive delivery across diverse service areas, bringing the same exceptional results we've achieved for Transport for NSW to your entire portfolio.

This represents proven operational capability, not theoretical promise. The CAPITAL Framework combines CBS’s unique methodology, sophisticated tools and specialised team expertise to deliver quantified, measurable value across Victoria’s health services.

CAPITAL Framework — Health Infrastructure Application

The CAPITAL Framework's seven core capabilities, originally developed for transport infrastructure, will be applied directly to the Department's KPI implementation challenge:

Each service within the Department will benefit from our proven methodology, established systems integration, and deep institutional knowledge, ensuring consistent excellence and measurable value delivery.

Similar Case Study: Transport Infrastructure

CASE STUDY: CAPITAL Framework KPI Implementation — Transport for NSW Tunnel Portfolio

CBS Group designed and implemented a performance monitoring framework across Transport for NSW's complex asset portfolio.  This engagement required the development and rollout of maintenance KPIs across multiple asset managers (Ventia, Fulton Hogan Egis) operating under different contractual arrangements with varying organisational maturity and capability.

The structural parallels with the Department's challenge are as follows:

Key outcomes included: validated savings exceeding $1 billion through KPI-driven asset management optimisation; a 31.8% average optimisation of contract values; a 97.2% on-time delivery rate across all KPI reporting obligations; 100% sinking fund forecast accuracy over 18 months; and a 4.7/5.0 client satisfaction rating reflecting the quality of stakeholder engagement.

Why this matters for the Department: The CAPITAL Framework's success was not built on the technical sophistication of the KPIs themselves, but on the implementation methodology — specifically, the maturity-based targeting, co-design engagement, and integration with existing operational systems that ensured the KPIs were adopted, not merely reported against. This implementation methodology is what CBS Group brings to the Department's engagement.

Experience and Understanding of the KPI Implementation Services Pilot

CBS understands the challenge - the Victorian Department of Health oversees 77 public health services operating under diverse maintenance models — from fully in-house teams to fully outsourced arrangements. 

This diversity has produced inconsistent asset management methodologies, performance outcomes and fragmented reporting, limiting the Department's ability to benchmark performance, allocate capital effectively, intervene where clinical and safety risks are emerging, or hold health services accountable against common standards.

Without standardised maintenance KPIs, the Department cannot systematically identify where aging building stock is deteriorating, where essential services infrastructure (HVAC, medical gases, backup power, nurse call systems) is at risk, or where maintenance teams are struggling with inadequate resources. 

The Department has invested in developing a comprehensive KPI framework — 12 KPIs across two implementation phases, supported by a 7-level maturity model (Level 0: Ad Hoc through Level 7: Transforming) detailed in the September 2025 Maintenance Standards Guidelines Performance Monitoring document. However, translating this framework across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity, building ages, service profiles, and competing clinical priorities — is a real implementation challenge.

Implementing the Department’s KPI framework from policy into operational practice in a way that is practical, scalable, and enduring — achieving adoption through benefits realisation, rather than compliance paperwork, and embedding a continual improvement mindset across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity, building ages, service profiles, and competing clinical priorities — is a real implementation challenge.

Our approach is designed around three commitments: segmenting health services by actual maturity (not size, service type or geography) to ensure support is proportionate and relevant; co-designing implementation pathways with the selected pilot health services so they own the framework rather than having it imposed; and integrating KPI data capture with existing maintenance systems and workflows to minimise administrative burden on operational teams.

Worked Example — MTTR Implementation in a Regional Health Service

The following worked example illustrates how KPI 1 (Mean Time to Repair) would be implemented end-to-end in a hypothetical Level 2 maturity regional health service, showing CBS Group's practical approach.

Facility Profile: Goulburn Valley District Hospital (hypothetical). A 120-bed regional health service with a maintenance team of 3 FTE (1 supervisor, 2 technicians), supplemented by external contractors for specialist trades. The facility is approximately 45 years old with two major refurbishments. The maintenance team currently uses MEX (basic configuration) for work order management, with inconsistent data entry practices. Current maturity assessment: Level 2 (Emerging — some formal processes established but inconsistently applied).

Phase 1: Discovery (What We Find): During the site assessment, CBS identifies the following: MEX is installed but work orders are not consistently closed out with completion timestamps; approximately 60% of reactive work orders have both a "reported" and "completed" timestamp, while 40% are missing one or both; no distinction is made between asset criticality levels in work order priority; and the maintenance supervisor tracks urgent clinical equipment failures mentally but does not record response times systematically.

Phase 2: Data Analysis (What We Learn): From the 60% of work orders with usable timestamps, CBS extracts 12 months of MTTR data and calculates: overall MTTR across all assets of approximately 14.2 hours; however, this figure masks significant variation — general building maintenance MTTR is approximately 22 hours (acceptable), while essential services MTTR is approximately 4.8 hours (needs validation against AS 1851 and clinical requirements). CBS also identifies that 35% of reactive work orders are repeat failures on the same assets, suggesting underlying reliability issues that Repeated Failures (KPI 4), Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate (KPI 5), and the ensuing root cause analyses will help address.

Phase 3: Co-Design (What We Agree): In the co-design workshop (attended with other Level 2 cohort services), CBS works with the maintenance supervisor to establish the following: MTTR will be measured in three tiers aligned to asset criticality — Critical (essential services: theatre ventilation, medical gases, backup power, fire systems, nurse call) with a target MTTR of less than 2 hours; High (clinical environment: ward HVAC, hot water, lifts, sterilisation) with an example target MTTR of less than 8 hours; and Standard (non-clinical: general lighting, office HVAC, grounds) with a target MTTR of less than 48 hours.

To capture the data, CBS configures two changes in MEX: a mandatory "Asset Criticality" field on the work order form (pre-populated from the asset register with a dropdown of Critical/High/Standard); and a workflow rule that prevents work order closure without both "Reported" and "Completed" timestamps. These changes require approximately 2 hours of MEX configuration and 30 minutes of training for each technician.

Phase 4: Pilot (What Happens): During the 8-week pilot: the data capture rate improves from 60% to 92% within 4 weeks (the mandatory fields make it easier to comply than to skip); the maintenance supervisor reports that the criticality tiering "makes sense" and reflects how the team already prioritises informally; the fortnightly check-in identifies that the "Completed" timestamp is being entered when the technician finishes work, not when the asset is returned to service — CBS adjusts the field label to "Asset Returned to Service" to capture the correct endpoint; and the additional data entry burden is measured at approximately 3 minutes per work order, equating to approximately 45 minutes per week — well within the 2-hour monthly target.

Phase 5: Outcomes: After the pilot, the health service has: a functioning MTTR measurement system integrated into existing MEX workflows; baseline MTTR data segmented by asset criticality, enabling meaningful performance tracking; visibility of the repeat-failure pattern (feeding into KPI 4: Repeated Failures), which the maintenance supervisor uses to build a business case for targeted asset replacement; and a practical, sustainable reporting process that adds minimal burden to the maintenance team.

The Department has: comparable MTTR data from this service that can be benchmarked against other Level 2 cohort services; visibility of essential services response performance against clinical risk thresholds; and evidence of improvement trajectory that informs both the service's maturity advancement and the Department's capital allocation decisions.

Worked Example — OEE Adaptation for Health Essential Services

Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a Phase 2 KPI that requires adaptation for health infrastructure. In manufacturing, OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. In a health context, CBS adapts this as Essential Services Effectiveness (ESE):

ESE = Availability × Performance × Compliance

Availability measures the proportion of time essential services systems are operational and available for clinical use — calculated as (Total scheduled hours minus unplanned downtime hours) divided by Total scheduled hours. For a hospital operating theatre ventilation system scheduled 24/7, 8 hours of unplanned downtime in a month (720 hours) yields an Availability of 98.9%.

Performance measures whether the system delivers to its design specification when operational — for example, whether the HVAC system maintains the required temperature and pressure differentials in a theatre environment, or whether the medical gas system delivers at the required pressure and flow rates. This is measured through periodic performance testing against AS 1851 (fire systems), AS 2896 (medical gas), or design specifications.

Compliance replaces the manufacturing "Quality" component and measures the proportion of scheduled maintenance tasks completed to specification and on time — directly linking to KPI 5 (Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate) and mandatory maintenance frequencies under the relevant Australian Standards.

Practical implementation: For a Level 3 (Developing) health service with basic CMMS capability, CBS would initially measure ESE for the top 5 essential services systems only (backup power, fire detection and suppression, medical gases, theatre ventilation, and nurse call). Data sources include: CMMS downtime logs for Availability, existing essential services testing records for Performance, and planned maintenance completion records for Compliance. This focused approach provides a meaningful ESE measure without requiring data across all building systems — the scope can be expanded as the health service matures.

Proposed Service and Deliverables

Description of the services provided against the requirements and deliverables

CBS Group will align to the five-phase delivery approach aligned to the Department's specified structure.  The service and value we provide is underpinned by the CAPITAL Framework methodology (described in 3.1) adapted for the health infrastructure context.

Phase 1: Discovery and Assessment (Weeks 1–4)

This phase establishes the evidence base for a targeted implementation. Our approach is designed to engage with, and rapidly build a clear, evidence-based understanding of the current maintenance environment across the 77 Victorian health services. 

CBS will conduct a systematic assessment of each health service's maintenance model, systems, processes, and data capabilities. The assessment is structured around four dimensions aligned to the Department's maturity model:

Leveraging the Department's stakeholder details to plan engagement, we will conduct targeted engagement with operational leads, engineering managers and asset management teams to validate findings and ensure the local context is fully understood.

The initial assessment will be conducted through a combination of structured interviews with facilities managers and maintenance leads (60–90 minutes, on-site or video), and a self-assessment questionnaire, followed up with system walkthroughs to understand data capture workflows, and document review (maintenance plans, asset registers, recent performance reports where available).

Key deliverables: Discovery Report documenting the current state across the 77 health services; Stakeholder Engagement& Communications Plan; Data Readiness Assessment for each pilot service; and Risk Register with mitigation strategies.

Phase 2: Data Analysis and Benchmarking (Weeks 3-8)

Overlapping with late Phase 1 to maintain momentum, this phase gathers, validates, and analyses maintenance data from participating health services. The overlapping timeline reflects that data collection can commence for early-assessed services while discovery continues for later services. Our team will undertake rigorous data validation and cleansing to ensure accuracy and comparability across health services.

CBS will benchmark current performance against the Department's six (6) Foundational KPIs and relevant standards, including alignment with ISO 55001 (Asset Management) and ISO 41001 (Facility Management) where applicable. This analytical rigour ensures that KPI targets are grounded in engineering evidence, not arbitrary benchmarks. A critical output is the maturity segmentation — classifying health services into cohorts using assessment criteria mapped to the Department's 7-level Asset Management Maturity model. This segmentation, combined with the organisational and operational readiness information gathered in Phase 1 will provide the Department meaningful information to determine the ongoing pilot group composition, ensuring the pilot group is representative, scalable and positioned to generate meaningful insights for sector wide implementation.

For health services with limited data, we will establish minimum viable baselines using available information supplemented by structured estimation where necessary. This ensures every health service has a starting point for measurement, even if initial data is imperfect — recognising that perfect data is not a prerequisite for beginning to improve.

A critical challenge in benchmarking across the 77 health services is the diversity of building stock — from recently constructed facilities to buildings over 100 years old, with varying configurations, service profiles, and asset condition. CBS will normalise benchmarking data using a peer group methodology based on three dimensions: facility complexity (determined by service profile — tertiary/specialist, general acute, subacute/rehabilitation, community health), asset age band (grouped into post-2010, 1980–2010, pre-1980 cohorts reflecting distinct construction standards and asset lifecycles), and maintenance model (in-house, partially outsourced, fully outsourced). This segmentation produces meaningful peer groups where like is compared with like, preventing misleading benchmarks that penalise older facilities or reward lower-complexity services. Where a health service spans multiple building ages or configurations, we will benchmark at the building or precinct level where data permits, aggregating to the health service level for portfolio reporting.

Key deliverables: Benchmarking Report with performance baselines and peer group normalisation methodology; Maturity Segmentation Model mapping each pilot health service to the Department's 7-level maturity model; pilot group definition and selection rationale (proposing 15 health services spanning maturity, facility types and, where possible, equitable metro/regional representation); and Gap Analysis identifying priority improvement areas for each cohort – Foundational, Established and Advanced.

Phase 3: Engagement and Co-Design (Weeks 6-12)

This phase is the critical bridge between analysis and implementation, focusing on direct engagement and collaborative design to ensure the KPI framework is practical, adoptable and aligned with operational realities. Our codesign approach ensures that health services feel ownership of the solution, that implementation is grounded in real world practice, will provide enhanced visibility and accountability, provide data driven decision making and add ongoing value to their service through the benefits realisation.

CBS will facilitate stakeholder engagement and co-design through the following structured mechanisms:

Codesign workshops (2-2.5 hours) –with each service including executive stakeholders, asset / facility managers, key engineering and maintenance team members and other key stakeholders, structured around the practical question: "How will these KPIs work in your facility?".  The workshops will incorporate:

Context setting: overview of the KPI framework purpose, what the Department needs, and how KPIs will support investment decisions and operational improvement. Facilitated discussion on current maintenance challenges ie local barriers and innovations — building rapport and identifying shared pain points within the team.

KPI walk-through: practical review of Phase 1 KPIs using de-identified data examples from Phase 2 analysis. For each KPI: what it measures, what data is needed, where that data sits in their current systems, and what "good" looks like at their maturity level. Participants work through a sample KPI calculation using their own facility context.

Data and systems: hands-on exercise identifying data sources and collection methods within participants' current systems (CMMS, spreadsheets, or manual). CBS presents draft reporting templates for group feedback and refinement. Focus on minimising additional workload — what can be automated versus what requires manual input.

Target setting and action planning: co-design of realistic KPI targets for each maturity cohort. Participants identify their top three implementation priorities and potential barriers. CBS documents agreed actions, responsibilities, and timelines for each participant.

Site visits: Targeted one-on-one engagement with asset/facilities managers who have specific implementation challenges or complexities requiring additional investigation and understanding.

Follow up virtual / telephone calls:  for clarification or where additional information may be required.

Key deliverables: Co-Design Outcomes Report; cohort-specific Implementation Action Plans (including KPI targets, data collection methods, reporting templates, training plans, and timelines); tailored KPI reporting templates and data collection guides for each maturity cohort; and Training Needs Analysis.

Phase 4: Pilot Implementation (Weeks 10–18)

Drawing on the insights gained in Phase 3, CBS will codesign tailored support options, implementation pathways, and action plans for each pilot service. This includes identifying capability building needs, data improvement strategies, and operational adjustments required to embed the KPIs effectively. -design tailored support options, implementation pathways, and action plans for each -building needs, data improvement strategies, and operational adjustments required to embed the KPIs effectively.

These pilot implementations will be structured to test every element of the implementation approach of the foundational KPIs in a supported environment before sector-wide deployment.

Pilot support includes: on-site or virtual coaching sessions with each pilot service to configure data collection, test reporting, and troubleshoot issues; fortnightly check-in calls with pilot service maintenance managers; a shared online workspace for pilot participants to exchange experiences and solutions; and CBS team availability for ad-hoc support as issues arise.

During this Phase, ensuring CBS is adding value above pure KPI implementation, where data permits, we will apply Reliability-Centred Maintenance (RCM) analysis principles — drawing on Jim Ellwood's and Gethin Perry’s qualifications and expertise in Maintenance and Reliability Engineering and extensive experience delivering FMECA and RCM programmes across infrastructure, transport, defence, and utilities sectors —validating current maintenance task frequencies and strategies suitability against the health service's asset criticality profile.

To assess the effectiveness of the tailored implementation framework in real-world conditions, the pilot is monitored against defined success criteria including: ability to produce Phase 1 KPI reports using existing systems and configured processes; data quality and completeness levels; time investment required from maintenance staff (targeting less than 2 hours per month of additional effort); and maintenance team satisfaction with the implementation approach.  We will document lessons learned and identify barriers and enablers to refine tools, templates and processes based on the feedback received.

Key deliverables: Pilot Implementation Plan; pilot monitoring reports (fortnightly); Pilot Evaluation Report with quantitative and qualitative outcomes and lessons learned, and pilot feedback.

Phase 5: Full Implementation and Continuous Improvement (Weeks 16–22)

Incorporating pilot feedback, CBS will finalise the implementation framework for sector-wide adoption. This phase addresses the critical transition from supported pilot to self-sustaining sector-wide operation.

Key activities include:

Finalised implementation framework — incorporating all pilot learnings, refined KPI implementation and reporting templates, data collection guides, training materials, and support resources packaged for deployment across all 77 health services.

Change management deployment — development and delivery of a communication and engagement strategy for sector-wide adoption (detailed in Section 3.5).

Self-sustaining support structures — establishment of a "KPI Champions" network (trained representatives from each health service or regional cluster who provide peer support and serve as first-line guidance for their colleagues); a resource library (hosted on the Department's existing platforms) containing all implementation tools, templates, guides, and FAQs; defined escalation pathways for issues that cannot be resolved at health service or peer network level; and a recommended ongoing review cycle (quarterly portfolio performance review, annual framework refresh).

Continuous improvement framework — mechanisms for ongoing monitoring, evaluation, and refinement including a defined process for updating KPI targets as health services mature, a feedback mechanism for health services to report implementation challenges and suggest improvements, and alignment with the Department's planned progression from Phase 1 to Phase 2 KPIs.

Key deliverables: Final Implementation Framework; Change Management and Communication Strategy; Continuous Improvement Framework; sector-wide KPI reporting templates, data collection guides, and training materials; KPI Champions training package and network establishment; and Project Close-Out Report with recommendations for sustained adoption.

Proposed Actions/Items to meet requirements against the specified timeline

Project Plan Summary

In Figure 1 below, CBS has provided a detailed map of key activities by phase, identifying activity flow, involvement and key deliverables.

Figure 1 Phased delivery activities designed to meet the Department’s objectives, and aligned to scope and evaluation criteria

Actions to ensure requirements and timelines are met

Dependency management and contingency. The most critical external dependency is the Department's provision of stakeholder details and introductions to pilot health services at the start of Phase 1. If introductions are delayed, CBS will mitigate by: commencing desktop analysis (document review, data collection scoping, assessment framework design) during Week 1 without requiring health service access; conducting initial stakeholder mapping using publicly available information and the Department's existing health service directory; and compressing the site assessment phase from 3 weeks to 2 weeks once introductions are made, with additional team resource temporarily assigned to maintain the overall timeline. A delay of up to 2 weeks can be absorbed within the existing programme structure. If delays exceed 2 weeks, CBS will propose a revised programme to the Department with adjusted milestones. Similarly, if Department review periods for critical deliverables (D6, D10, D17) extend beyond the 5-business-day review cycle, CBS will flag the schedule impact in the fortnightly progress report and propose mitigation.

Concurrent phase management. All phases overlap to run concurrently by design, enabling an efficient 22-week programme. CBS manages this through deliberate team allocation: during the Phase 2/3 overlap (Weeks 6–8), Perry and Harper lead the data and benchmarking workstream while Caranguian and Ellwood lead the engagement and co-design workstream — the two streams are distinct in personnel and activities, converging only at defined integration points (the maturity segmentation output from Phase 2 feeds directly into Phase 3 co-design workshop design). During the Phase 4/5 overlap (Weeks 16–18), Ellwood leads the final pilot monitoring while Caranguian begins full implementation planning — again, distinct personnel on distinct activities with defined integration points. Kate Heath manages cross-phase coordination through the fortnightly progress reporting cycle. Jeff Dusting's strategic oversight role includes monitoring phase interactions and resolving any resource conflicts.

Critical path. The critical path runs through: Department of Health (DH) stakeholder introductions (Week 1) → site assessments complete (Week 4) → Maturity Segmentation Model approved by DH (Week 6) → co-design workshops complete (Week 11) → Implementation Action Plans approved by DH (Week 12) → pilot commencement (Week 13) → Pilot Evaluation Report (Week 18) → Final Implementation Framework approved by DH (Week 21). The two items most likely to cause programme delay are DH approval of the Maturity Segmentation Model (which gates the co-design workshop content) and DH approval of the Implementation Action Plans (which gates pilot commencement). CBS will mitigate these by submitting draft deliverables 1 week before the formal submission date, enabling informal DH review and feedback before the formal review cycle begins.

Consolidated Deliverables Register

D = Core deliverable; V = Value-add deliverable

Quality Assured Processes

All deliverables follow a structured quality assurance process:

Internal review. Every deliverable undergoes peer review by at least one team member not directly responsible for its preparation, ensuring technical accuracy, methodological consistency, and alignment with the Department's requirements.

Strategic review. Jeff Dusting reviews all major deliverables (D1, D5, D6, D9, D10, D15, D17, D18) prior to submission, ensuring alignment with the CAPITAL Framework methodology and strategic consistency across the engagement.

Department review cycle. All deliverables are submitted in draft for Department review and comment, with a 5-business-day review period. CBS incorporates Department feedback and resubmits as final within 3 business days of receiving comments. Critical path deliverables

CBS Group’s approach and methodology in delivering the services

True partnership: Achieving excellence through collaboration

CBS Group's approach is built on a foundation of genuine partnership and mutual trust, not transactional service provision. We embed ourselves as an extension of the Departments Asset Planning Unit working collaboratively with other Department stakeholders. We will align our success metrics directly with your strategic objectives, and at the same time work hand in hand with the selected pilot services, using documented change management methodologies to engage, embed and progress services along the KPI maturity scale.

Our engagements with TfNSW on the Western Harbour Tunnel and M6, exemplify the power of true collaboration. The remarkable $1.049B in validated savings wasn't achieved by CBS Group alone – it emerged from the synergy between TfNSW's strategic vision, deep asset knowledge and innovative thinking with our methodologies and technical expertise. 

The Department’s willingness to challenge conventional approaches and engage in open dialogue and co-design is essential to realise success.

We understand that trust is earned through consistent delivery and open communication. Through a collaborative partnership model – where insights flow freely, challenges are addressed collectively and successes are shared – we don't just deliver services; we amplify the Health Services’ capabilities and co-create solutions that neither party could achieve independently. This is the foundation of lasting value: genuine partnership built on mutual respect, shared goals and proven trust.

Lessons learned that shape our proposed approach

Key lessons from the TfNSW implementation directly informed the methodology we propose for the Department:

Early in the TfNSW engagement, we designed a performance reporting framework that was technically rigorous but required asset managers to produce data their systems could not readily generate. The result was manual data extraction, delayed reporting, and compliance-driven rather than value-driven engagement. We redesigned the framework to start with what existing systems could produce, then progressively built data capture capability over 12 months. This lesson directly shapes our "system-agnostic" approach for health services — start with what the CMMS can deliver today, then build. 

We initially set uniform KPI targets across all asset managers regardless of their operational maturity. Higher-maturity managers saw the targets as unambitious; lower-maturity managers saw them as unachievable. Both disengaged. We introduced maturity-calibrated targets that challenged every manager at their level while providing a clear advancement pathway. This lesson is embedded in our proposed maturity-based segmentation and cohort-specific target-setting approach for health services.

We found that KPI data collection and reporting waned when staff could not see a direct line of sight between the KPI and improved operational outcomes.  Where outcomes were not linked, the measures were perceived as additional administrative burden rather than performance enablers. We refined the framework to explicitly link each KPI to tangible service, financial or risk outcomes, and where possible, to surface quick wins that demonstrated value early. We also identified owners for the outcomes, and therefore, the KPIs; ensuring the KPIs are “championed” and implementation is sustained over the long term. This lesson is embedded in our approach to KPI design and rollout for health services, ensuring that every measure is outcome-anchored, decision-useful and demonstrably beneficial to those accountable for delivery, thereby sustaining implementation beyond compliance.

Value-Add to Project Deliverables

Beyond the specified requirements, CBS Group offers the following value-add elements:

Maturity Advancement Roadmap. For each participating health service, a clear pathway from current maturity level to target state with defined milestones. This gives individual health services and the Department a shared view of the improvement trajectory and the support needed to achieve it.

KPI Interdependency Analysis. A mapping of how the 12 KPIs interact and influence each other — for example, how improving Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate (KPI 5) is likely to reduce Repeated Failures (KPI 4) and improve MTTR (KPI 1). This analysis enables health services to prioritise effort for maximum cross-KPI impact.

Portfolio Performance Dashboard Concept. A design specification (not a built system) for a Department-level dashboard enabling real-time visibility of KPI performance across the health service portfolio, segmented by cohort, region, facility type, and maturity level. This concept can be implemented using the Department's existing reporting platforms and provides a foundation for ongoing portfolio oversight.

Understanding of Key Issues and Risks

CBS Group has identified the following key issues and risks based on our analysis of the RFP, the Department's Performance Monitoring guidance document, and our experience implementing KPI frameworks across complex multi-stakeholder environments.

Data quality, availability, and system diversity. Health services at lower maturity levels are likely to have incomplete, inconsistent, or inaccessible maintenance data. Phase 1 KPIs (MTTR, Reactive Maintenance SLA Compliance, Maintenance Work Order Backlog, Repeated Failures, Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate, Maintenance Cost per Bed/sqm) assume a level of systematic data capture that may not exist uniformly. Some health services may use enterprise CMMS platforms (such as MEX, Maximo, or Pronto), others may use basic systems or spreadsheets, and some may rely on paper-based processes. 

Our approach addresses this through early data readiness assessment in Phase 1, pragmatic data collection workarounds for lower-maturity services, and a system-agnostic data requirements specification that defines what information is needed rather than prescribing which system must produce it.

Mitigation: Phase 1 Discovery includes a specific data readiness assessment for each pilot health service, cataloguing the CMMS platform in use and its current configuration. Phase 2 develops minimum viable data collection templates for services that cannot natively produce KPI data from existing systems. CBS will work directly with common CMMS platforms used across Victorian health services — including MEX (widely deployed in Australian health and government), IBM Maximo (used by larger health services and shared services arrangements), and Pronto Xi (common in regional health services) — to configure standard KPI extraction reports. 

For services using spreadsheet-based or manual systems, we will develop structured data collection workbooks with built-in validation and automated KPI calculation that can be progressively migrated to a CMMS as the service matures. Our approach does not require health services to change their systems — we configure reporting within whatever platform they currently use.

Organisational resistance and competing priorities. Health service maintenance teams are typically lean and focused on keeping facilities operational. KPI implementation may be perceived as an additional compliance burden imposed by head office, particularly where maintenance budgets have been under sustained pressure. Clinical priorities will always — and should always — take precedence over reporting activities.

Mitigation: Our co-design approach (Phase 3) involves health services in shaping how KPIs apply to their context, building ownership rather than imposing obligations. We design data capture to integrate with existing workflows rather than creating parallel processes. We frame KPIs as tools that strengthen the case for maintenance investment, not as surveillance mechanisms.

Normalisation and benchmarking validity. Meaningful benchmarking across 77 health services requires appropriate normalisation that accounts for facility type, age, complexity, service profile, and geographic context. Without sound normalisation, benchmarking will produce misleading comparisons that undermine confidence in the framework.

Mitigation: Phase 2 develops facility-type peer groups and normalisation methodologies that account for structural differences in cost drivers and performance profiles. Benchmarking is presented as a tool for identifying improvement opportunities, not as a league table.

Essential services and clinical risk. Health facility maintenance has direct patient safety implications that distinguish it from other infrastructure contexts. Asset failures in critical clinical systems (theatre ventilation, medical gases, backup power, sterilisation, nurse call) carry clinical risk that must be reflected in KPI prioritisation and escalation frameworks. 

Health services are also subject to specific Australian Standards governing essential services maintenance, including AS 1851 (Routine Service of Fire Protection Systems and Equipment), AS 2896 (Medical Gas Systems), and AS/NZS 3003 (Electrical Installations in Patient Care Areas), which impose mandatory maintenance frequencies and performance requirements that must be reflected in KPI measurement.

Mitigation: Our KPI implementation will align with essential services classifications, clinical risk categories, and applicable Australian Standards maintenance requirements. The Victorian Health Building Authority (VHBA), which plays a central role in health infrastructure planning and standards across the state, provides additional guidance on facility performance expectations that will inform our benchmarking and target-setting methodology. 

KPI measurement frameworks will be cross-referenced against mandatory maintenance obligations under AS 1851, AS 2896, and AS/NZS 3003 to ensure that essential services compliance is visible within the KPI reporting structure — enabling health services to demonstrate compliance performance alongside operational performance in a single framework.

Sustaining momentum beyond pilot. The transition from a supported pilot environment to self-sustaining sector-wide adoption is the highest-risk phase. Without ongoing support structures, early adoption gains are likely to decay.

Mitigation: Phase 5 establishes self-sustaining support mechanisms — peer networks, KPI champion training, resource libraries, and escalation pathways — designed to maintain momentum after the engagement concludes.

CBS Group’s Response against Evaluation Criteria

Evaluation Criteria 1

Demonstrated experience in large-scale asset management, KPI implementation, and stakeholder engagement within health or public sector

CBS Group brings extensive, directly relevant experience in designing, implementing, and embedding performance-based KPI frameworks across complex public sector asset portfolios. Our primary domain has been transport infrastructure, and the methodological core of our work — phased KPI rollout across portfolios of assets managed by diverse organisations at varying maturity levels, with the objective of driving performance improvement rather than compliance activity — is what this engagement requires. Importantly, our team includes professionals who have held senior roles within Australia's leading health and social infrastructure facilities management organisations — Broadspectrum (now Ventia), Downer, and Cushman Wakefield — who bring direct understanding of how hospital and health facility maintenance is contracted, delivered, measured, and reported at the operational level.

Proven KPI Implementation at Scale

Refer to CASE STUDY: CAPITAL Framework KPI Implementation — Transport for NSW Tunnel Portfolio in our response to Q3.2.

Victorian Public Sector and Government Experience

Austin Health – The CBS team lead the transition of facility management services to private provider (at the time Broadspectrum). Jim also managed and conducted a Reliability, Availability and Maintainability (RAM) analysis of candidate systems for the VICERS project for the Melbourne suburban train fleet. Both projects required working within Victorian Government procurement and governance frameworks. 

CitiPower/Powercor — Jim Ellwood led programme management and analysis for the deployment of the Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) as part of the Victorian Government's smart electricity meter initiative. This involved rolling out a standardised measurement and reporting framework across a distributed asset base with diverse operating conditions — a direct parallel to the KPI implementation challenge.

AusNet Services — Jim Ellwood managed internal business restructures and significant market testing and outsourcing programmes, leading the development of operating models, processes, and system requirements. This is relevant organisational change management within a Victorian regulated utility environment.

Transurban Victorian Operations — David Harper served as Operation Safety Manager for CityLink (including Burnley and Domain tunnels) for 12 years, developing quantitative safety risk models, operations and maintenance manuals, and EPA licence frameworks. Jim Ellwood led ITS integration, concept of operations development, and KPI modelling across CityLink and West Gate Tunnel. Gethin Perry managed asset strategy for Transurban's Sydney portfolio and brings deep understanding of Transurban's Victorian operating environment.

CBS Group is familiar with the Victorian Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF) administered by the Department of Treasury and Finance, including its requirements for asset management strategy, governance, and performance reporting. We are also experienced in operating within OVIC's Victorian Protective Data Security Standards (VPDSS) framework, which underpins the Department's information security requirements.

Facilities Management Experience

In addition to CBS Group’s experience detailed in the above sections, CBS Group's team includes professionals with extensive urban and social infrastructure facilities management (FM) experience gained through senior roles at Australia's leading FM providers — the same organisations that deliver maintenance services to public health services across Victoria and nationally.  Details of our teams’ expertise is located in Section 7.

Stakeholder Engagement at Scale

CBS Group has a track record of managing stakeholder engagement across large, dispersed portfolios with diverse organisational capabilities. On the TfNSW engagement, our team managed relationships across multiple asset managers, government divisions, design contractors, and operational teams — each with distinct commercial interests, technical capabilities, and organisational cultures.

Our engagement approach starts from the position that the people closest to the assets understand their operating reality best. When developing KPI frameworks for TfNSW tunnels, we spent time with operational teams understanding their daily workflows, data capture practices, and pain points. The KPIs we implemented had to be meaningful at the operational level, not just the strategic level. We bring this same approach to the Department's engagement — health service maintenance managers and their teams are essential partners in co-designing KPI implementation that will work in their facilities.

Our CBS team has extensive experience co-designing solutions and improved delivery methodologies across the asset management spectrum, engaging closely with stakeholders to build rapport and engagement in the codesign process to drive changes in attitude and outcomes. This approach is underpinned by our qualifications in ISO 44001 (Collaborative Business Relationship Management), making CBS Group uniquely qualified to support and implement collaborative leadership frameworks across complex, multi-stakeholder environments. We bring this same approach to the Department's engagement — health service maintenance managers and their teams are essential partners in co-designing KPI implementation that will actually work in their facilities.

Evaluation Criteria 2

Ability to deliver actionable insights, tailored support, and scalable solutions for diverse health service cohorts

CBS Group's approach is built on a fundamental principle proven through our infrastructure experience: KPIs drive value only when they are embedded in the operational reality of the organisations that must report against them.

Maturity-Based Segmentation and Tailored Support

The Department's Performance Monitoring guidance establishes a 7-level maturity model progressing from Level 0 (Ad Hoc — reactive, unplanned maintenance with no formal processes) through to Level 7 (Transforming — maintenance function drives organisational strategy with predictive analytics and innovation). CBS Group has extensive experience in applying maturity-based segmentation to calibrate implementation approaches.

Our segmentation methodology integrates two dimensions from the Department's framework:

Dimension 1: Asset Management Maturity Level (Levels 0–7). During Phase 2, CBS will assess each health service against the Department's maturity model using the dual-dimension scoring methodology specified in Attachment 3 — evaluating both System Status (how well the asset management system is documented, structured, and embedded) and Effectiveness of Application (how consistently the system is applied and delivering outcomes), with the final maturity level being the lower of the two scores. This ensures maturity assessments are rigorous and reflect actual practice, not just documentation.

Dimension 2: ESS Category (Category 1, 2, 3). The Department classifies health services by Essential Services category based on infrastructure scale, service scope, and operational complexity. As outlined in the Department's target calibration framework (Attachment 3, Section 4.4), ESS Category directly influences realistic KPI target-setting: Category 1 facilities (major metropolitan hospitals) are generally positioned to begin at higher maturity levels and may already be capable of meeting intermediate targets in the short term; Category 2 facilities may start at mid-range maturity with targets evolving from basic to advanced; and Category 3 facilities may begin at Level 0 with initial targets focused on foundational practices. Our segmentation will cross-reference maturity level with ESS Category to produce a two-dimensional cohort classification that determines both implementation support intensity and target ambition.

Minimum maturity level alignment. Each KPI has a defined minimum maturity level for implementation (specified in the Department's KPI Dictionary). Our pilot selection and rollout sequencing will respect these thresholds: Level 0 minimum KPIs (MTTR, Cost per Bed/sqm, PMP, Major Failures) are applicable to all health services and form the core of Phase 1 rollout; Level 1 minimum KPIs (Asset Register Completeness) require at least basic system use; Level 3 minimum KPIs (Work Order Backlog, Repeated Failures, Planned Task Completion, OEE) require defined processes and standardised systems; and Level 5 minimum KPIs (RCA Effectiveness) are appropriate only for advanced services with optimised processes.

For this engagement, we propose segmenting the 77 health services into three to four maturity cohorts during Phase 2, using the dual-dimension assessment described above. The segmentation will be based on genuine capability (data systems, workforce skills, process maturity, asset register completeness) rather than proxies such as health service size, budget, or metropolitan/regional location. A 120-bed regional hospital with a skilled facilities manager running a well-configured CMMS may be at a higher maturity level than a metropolitan tertiary hospital with a larger but less structured maintenance operation.

Each cohort receives a differentiated implementation pathway aligned to the Department's target calibration framework (Attachment 3, Tables 4–6):

Foundational cohort (Levels 0–2, typically Cat 2/3 facilities): Health services in this cohort are likely operating with minimal formal maintenance management systems — potentially using spreadsheets, paper-based work orders, or basic systems with incomplete data. The implementation focus for these services is on establishing the basic data capture infrastructure needed to report against Level 0 minimum KPIs (MTTR, Cost per Bed/sqm, PMP, Major Failures) and progressing toward Level 1 and Level 3 KPIs as systems mature. Support will be intensive and hands-on, including on-site assistance to configure or establish minimum viable data capture processes, pragmatic workarounds for systems that cannot natively produce KPI data (such as structured manual collection templates), and training focused on fundamental maintenance management practices. KPI targets will be calibrated as "Basic to Intermediate" per the Department's target ambition framework, with a focus on establishing visibility and baseline measurement rather than achieving portfolio benchmarks.

Developing cohort (Levels 3–4, typically Cat 1/2 facilities): These health services have functioning maintenance management systems and processes. All Phase 1 and most Phase 2 KPIs are applicable at this maturity level. The implementation focus is on connecting existing data to the KPI framework, automating where possible, establishing formal target tracking, and building analytical skills. Support includes targeted system configuration assistance, data quality improvement workshops, and coaching on using KPI data for decision-making rather than just reporting. Targets will be calibrated as "Intermediate to Advanced" with formal benchmarking against peer services.

Advanced cohort (Levels 5–7, typically Cat 1 facilities): These health services have mature asset management practices and strong data systems. All 12 KPIs are applicable, including RCA Effectiveness (Level 5 minimum). The implementation focus is on optimisation — refining KPI measurement, external benchmarking against best practice, and extending to advanced analytics and combined KPI insights. Support is lighter touch, focused on peer leadership (these services can mentor foundational cohort services), continuous improvement, and stretch targets calibrated as "Advanced to Transformational" per the Department's framework.

Engagement with Specific KPIs — Health Infrastructure Context

CBS Group recognises that the Department's 12 KPIs require careful contextualisation for health infrastructure. The following examples show our understanding of the specific implementation challenges:

KPI 6: Maintenance Cost per Bed or Square Metre. This normalisation metric presents particular challenges across the health service portfolio because cost drivers vary fundamentally by facility type. A metropolitan tertiary hospital with operating theatres, intensive care units, and specialised medical equipment has structurally different maintenance cost profiles from a community health centre or a rural sub-acute facility. Our benchmarking approach will establish peer groups based on facility function and complexity, not just size, ensuring that benchmarking produces meaningful comparisons rather than misleading rankings. We will work with the Department to define the normalisation methodology during Phase 2, drawing on our experience in normalising lifecycle costs across transport assets with different operating profiles.

KPI 9: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). This Phase 2 KPI originates from manufacturing contexts where "availability x performance x quality" has clear quantitative definitions. Its application in health facilities requires careful adaptation. In a hospital context, we propose defining OEE components as: availability (proportion of time critical assets are operational and available for clinical use), performance (whether assets are operating at their designed capacity and specification), and quality (whether asset performance meets clinical and regulatory requirements — such as theatre ventilation air change rates, medical gas purity, and temperature control within specified ranges). This adaptation ensures OEE captures meaningful asset performance information without forcing health services into an artificial manufacturing paradigm.

KPI 1: Mean Time to Repair (MTTR). In health facilities, MTTR has direct clinical risk implications that differ by asset criticality classification. A failure in a general office HVAC system has a different urgency profile from a failure in theatre ventilation or backup power. Our implementation will align MTTR measurement with essential services classifications and clinical risk categories, ensuring the KPI captures the information that matters most for patient safety and service continuity.

Scalable Solutions

The methodology scales by design. Every element of the implementation — assessment tools, training materials, reporting templates, data collection guides, and support mechanisms — is designed as a reusable, configurable module that can be deployed across diverse health service contexts. The pilot implementation in Phase 4 explicitly tests the scalability of these tools by selecting pilot sites that span different maturity cohorts, facility types (metropolitan, regional, specialist), and system environments.

From data to decisions. CBS Group's approach turns KPI data into decisions the Department can act on. The insight-to-action chain operates at three levels: at the health service level, KPI data will enable maintenance managers to identify failing assets before clinical impact (e.g., rising MTTR on essential services triggers preventive replacement business case), prioritise maintenance investment against clinical risk (e.g., criticality-weighted backlog data directs limited resources to highest-impact work), and demonstrate performance to hospital executives (e.g., planned maintenance completion rates evidence that maintenance budgets are being effectively deployed). At the portfolio level, benchmarking data will enable the Department to identify health services requiring targeted intervention (e.g., services with MTTR above cohort median on critical assets), allocate capital funding based on evidence (e.g., asset condition data and maintenance cost trends informing capital investment prioritisation), and track sector-wide improvement trajectories against the maturity model. At the strategic level, aggregated KPI data provides the evidence base for policy decisions on maintenance funding adequacy, outsourcing vs in-house maintenance models, and asset renewal timing across the portfolio.

Opportunities for Value Based Interventions

While not explicitly required in this engagement, but assigning our senior team to this engagement, the Department will be able to draw upon the CAPITAL framework to identify CBS Value Based interventions.   We expect that in the conduct of this engagement, opportunities for cost reduction and asset performance improvement will be identified.

These opportunities could include opportunities for:

Selected equipment retirement, 

Change of maintenance strategy for selected equipment

Refinement of the lifecycle strategy for high capital value items

CBS appreciates that many times these changes are complicated and has an established Value-based pricing regime that could be applied, that allows CBS to ‘take the risk’ on savings possible.  It is our experience that this results in a substantial acceleration in value creation for our clients.

Evaluation Criteria 3

Commitment to sector-wide collaboration, knowledge sharing, and capacity building

CBS Group's delivery philosophy starts from the position that sustainable capability improvement comes from building the skills and systems within the organisations we work with — not from creating dependency on external consultants. The TfNSW engagement bears this out: after 4 years of collaborative partnership, TfNSW staff independently apply the CAPITAL Framework, develop lifecycle cost models, and evaluate asset manager proposals — capabilities that did not exist before our engagement. We bring this same commitment to the Department's KPI implementation.

Sector-Wide Collaboration

Our approach to sector-wide collaboration operates at three levels:

Department–CBS collaboration. CBS will operate as an embedded extension of the Asset Planning Unit, working alongside DH staff to co-develop all implementation tools and methodologies. Our open-book approach means all frameworks, templates, and analytical tools developed during the engagement become Department assets — there is no proprietary lock-in. Specifically: all deliverables will be provided in editable source formats (Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint) hosted on the Department's own platforms — not as locked PDFs or in CBS-proprietary systems; all analytical models (benchmarking models, maturity assessment tools, KPI calculation engines) will be built in Microsoft Excel with documented formulas and user guides, enabling Department staff to maintain and adapt them independently; training materials will include editable facilitator guides, slide decks, and participant workbooks so the Department can deliver future training without CBS involvement; and all intellectual property created during the engagement will vest in the Department under the MSA terms. Regular progress reviews (fortnightly during active phases) will ensure the Department maintains full visibility and ownership of the programme direction.

Cross-service collaboration. The engagement is designed to create connections between health services, not just between each service and the Department. Specific mechanisms include: co-design workshops organised by maturity cohort, where health services with similar challenges collaborate on implementation solutions; a facilitated community of practice (initially convened by CBS, transitioned to peer governance by Phase 5) providing an ongoing forum for sharing innovations, troubleshooting challenges, and benchmarking progress; regional peer clusters grouping geographically proximate health services for face-to-face collaboration and mutual support; and pilot service "open door" sessions during Phase 4, where non-pilot health services are invited to observe pilot implementation and engage directly with pilot service maintenance managers.

Engaging non-pilot health services during the engagement. Of the 77 health services, the pilot will involve 15–20. The remaining services are actively engaged through: the portfolio-wide survey and initial data analysis in Phase 1 and Phase 2, which captures baseline data and builds awareness across all 77 services; quarterly communication updates from the Department (drafted by CBS) reporting on pilot progress and previewing the full rollout approach; webinar briefings (2–3 during the engagement) where pilot services share their experience and non-pilot services can ask questions and prepare; and the provision of preliminary self-assessment tools that non-pilot services can use to evaluate their own readiness ahead of the full rollout. The self-assessment tool will be distributed by the Department through existing communication channels (the Department's regular health service correspondence) with a short instructional video and worked example. CBS will provide a dedicated email address for self-assessment queries and will collate completed self-assessments to inform the full rollout sequencing. This approach uses the Department's existing relationship with health services rather than requiring CBS to establish new communication pathways.

Knowledge Sharing

CBS Group's knowledge sharing approach embeds transfer into every interaction rather than treating it as a discrete handover activity.

Embedded knowledge transfer. All deliverables include full methodology documentation that enables the Department and health services to understand, replicate, and adapt the approaches used. Every tool, template, and analytical model is accompanied by user guidance explaining its purpose, inputs, methodology, and interpretation of outputs. This ensures the Department can maintain and update all implementation materials independently after the engagement concludes.

Structured training. CBS will deliver targeted training aligned to audience needs: Department staff receive training in the maturity assessment methodology, benchmarking approach, and portfolio performance monitoring — enabling the Asset Planning Unit to administer the KPI framework independently; pilot health service maintenance teams receive practical training in KPI data capture, reporting, and performance interpretation within their existing systems; and KPI Champions receive facilitation and coaching training enabling them to support their peers during and after the full rollout.

Documentation and resource library. All engagement outputs are compiled into a structured resource library hosted on the Department's existing platforms, including: implementation guides for each maturity cohort; KPI reporting templates and data collection tools; training materials (slide decks, facilitator guides, quick reference cards); FAQs and troubleshooting guides based on pilot experience; and case studies from pilot services showing practical implementation pathways.

Capacity Building

CBS Group defines capacity building success through measurable outcomes. By the conclusion of this engagement, we target the following:

Our capacity building approach is explicitly designed to reduce the Department's reliance on external consultants for ongoing KPI administration. The engagement succeeds when the Department and health services can maintain, refine, and extend the KPI framework without CBS Group's involvement.

Evidence of capacity building outcomes. CBS Group's capacity building approach has been tested at scale. At Transport for NSW, our engagement progressively transferred the following capabilities to TfNSW staff who now operate them independently: lifecycle cost modelling for tunnel assets (TfNSW staff now build and maintain whole-of-life cost models without CBS assistance, whereas before the engagement this capability did not exist within the agency); asset manager proposal evaluation using the CAPITAL Framework (TfNSW staff independently evaluate contractor proposals against the framework CBS co-developed, having scored and awarded contracts without CBS involvement); performance monitoring and KPI reporting (TfNSW's Asset Management team now administers the KPI reporting framework across the tunnel portfolio, receiving, reviewing, and acting on contractor performance reports using tools and templates CBS designed with them); and sinking fund management (TfNSW manages lifecycle sinking funds with 100% forecast accuracy over 18 months, using financial models CBS transferred during the engagement). The point is that CBS's methodology builds organisational capability rather than creating consulting dependency.

Evaluation Criteria 4

Adherence to relevant government guidelines, standards, and compliance requirements

CBS Group has a strong track record of operating within government regulatory and compliance frameworks. Our approach to compliance is substantive rather than procedural — we integrate compliance requirements into our delivery methodology rather than treating them as separate obligations.

Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF). The Department's KPI framework operates within the Victorian AMAF administered by DTF. CBS Group's methodology is grounded in ISO 55001 (Asset Management), which shares foundational principles with the AMAF, including requirements for: an asset management policy and strategy linked to organisational objectives; defined roles, responsibilities, and accountabilities for asset management; performance evaluation and monitoring frameworks; and continual improvement mechanisms. Our implementation approach will ensure that KPI frameworks developed for health services are consistent with their AMAF obligations, particularly the requirements around asset management accountability, planning, and performance evaluation.

ISO Standards. Our approach draws on the following international standards: ISO 55001:2014 (Asset Management — Management Systems), which provides the overarching framework for asset management practice; and ISO 41001:2018 (Facility Management — Management Systems), which is the applicable standard for health facility maintenance and provides specific guidance on facility service delivery, performance measurement, and continual improvement. The Department's 12 KPIs align well with both standards' requirements for performance evaluation and monitoring.

Australian Standards for Essential Services. Our KPI implementation will be cross-referenced against mandatory maintenance standards applicable to health facilities, including: AS 1851:2012 (Routine Service of Fire Protection Systems and Equipment), which prescribes mandatory inspection, testing, and maintenance frequencies for fire detection, suppression, and evacuation systems; AS 2896:2021 (Medical Gas Systems — Installation and Testing), which governs the maintenance and performance verification of medical gas reticulation systems; and AS/NZS 3003:2018 (Electrical Installations — Patient Care Areas), which specifies maintenance requirements for electrical systems in clinical environments. Alignment with these standards ensures that KPI measurement captures essential services compliance as an integral component of maintenance performance.

Victorian Health Building Authority (VHBA). We acknowledge the VHBA's role in health infrastructure planning, design guidance, and performance standards across Victorian health services. Our benchmarking methodology and KPI target-setting will be informed by VHBA standards and guidelines where applicable, ensuring alignment with the broader health infrastructure governance framework.

Information Security. CBS Group commits to completing the Information Security Standards Requirements Assessment (ISSRA) in full. Our security practices are aligned with the Victorian Protective Data Security Standards (VPDSS) and the Australian Government's Essential Eight mitigation strategies. Specific commitments include: multi-factor authentication enforced across all CBS systems and personnel; application control and administrative privilege restriction; regular patching (critical patches within 48 hours, routine within 2 weeks); daily backups with offsite replication; encryption at rest and in transit for all Department data; no offshore data processing or storage; formal access provisioning and deprovisioning processes with access reviews; documented incident response plan, tested annually; and personnel security awareness training completed annually by all staff.

CBS Group holds Cyber Security insurance with an APRA-approved insurer. We will participate in Department security assessments and audits at engagement commencement and at intervals during the engagement as required.

Privacy and Data Handling. CBS Group will handle all Department and health service data in accordance with the Victorian Privacy and Data Protection Act 2014 and the Health Records Act 2001. We will apply appropriate protective markings to all documents and data in accordance with the Department's information classification framework. We will not use AI tools to process Department data without prior written approval from the Department.

Professional Standards. Our team members are registered professionals with Engineers Australia and hold relevant certifications including Chartered Engineer (CEng), Fellow of Engineers Australia (FIEAust), Certified Fellow of Asset Management (CFAM), and ISO 55001 certification.

Victorian Government Strategic Alignment. CBS Group acknowledges the Department's System Planning Division's responsibility for addressing climate threats and integrating broader government directions into infrastructure planning. Our KPI implementation approach supports the Victorian Government's sustainability and resilience objectives by: establishing asset condition visibility that enables proactive maintenance (reducing emergency interventions and associated environmental impact); supporting evidence-based capital investment planning that enables prioritisation of energy efficiency and climate resilience upgrades; and providing data foundations that can be extended to capture energy consumption and environmental performance metrics as the KPI framework matures.

Evaluation Criteria 5

Robust change management and communication planning

Implementing KPIs across 77 health services with diverse maturity levels is a change management challenge first and a technical on
