3.2.       Similar Case Study: Transport Infrastructure
CASE STUDY: CAPITAL Framework KPI Implementation — Transport for NSW Tunnel Portfolio
CBS Group designed and implemented a performance monitoring framework across Transport for NSW's complex asset
portfolio. This engagement required the development and rollout of maintenance KPIs across multiple asset managers
(Ventia, Fulton Hogan Egis) operating under different contractual arrangements with varying organisational maturity and
capability.




                                                                                                                           Page 4
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring


The structural parallels with the Department's challenge are as follows:

 Victoria Dept of Health Challenge              CBS Reference Experience
 77 health services with diverse                Multiple asset managers with different organisational structures and capabilities
 maintenance models
 Health services at different maturity levels   Asset managers at different stages of capability development, requiring calibrated
 (Level 0–7)                                    KPI targets
 Need for performance metrics that drive        KPIs designed to connect maintenance performance to whole-of-life outcomes and
 improvement, not just reporting                community impact (lane rental framework)
 Diverse systems and data capture               Different CMMS platforms, data quality levels, and reporting capabilities across
 capabilities                                   asset managers
 Risk that KPIs become a compliance             Performance framework designed to incentivise genuine asset stewardship
 burden                                         behaviour
 Need for phased rollout with pilot testing     Phased implementation across tunnels with progressive refinement based on
                                                operational feedback

Key outcomes included: validated savings exceeding $1 billion through KPI-driven asset management optimisation; a
31.8% average optimisation of contract values; a 97.2% on-time delivery rate across all KPI reporting obligations; 100%
sinking fund forecast accuracy over 18 months; and a 4.7/5.0 client satisfaction rating reflecting the quality of
stakeholder engagement.
Why this matters for the Department: The CAPITAL Framework's success was not built on the technical sophistication
of the KPIs themselves, but on the implementation methodology — specifically, the maturity-based targeting, co-design
engagement, and integration with existing operational systems that ensured the KPIs were adopted, not merely reported
against. This implementation methodology is what CBS Group brings to the Department's engagement.

3.3.        Experience and Understanding of the KPI Implementation Services
            Pilot
CBS understands the challenge - the Victorian Department of Health oversees 77 public health services operating under
diverse maintenance models — from fully in-house teams to fully outsourced arrangements.
This diversity has produced inconsistent asset management methodologies, performance outcomes and fragmented
reporting, limiting the Department's ability to benchmark performance, allocate capital effectively, intervene where
clinical and safety risks are emerging, or hold health services accountable against common standards.
Without standardised maintenance KPIs, the Department cannot systematically identify where aging building stock is
deteriorating, where essential services infrastructure (HVAC, medical gases, backup power, nurse call systems) is at
risk, or where maintenance teams are struggling with inadequate resources.
The Department has invested in developing a comprehensive KPI framework — 12 KPIs across two implementation
phases, supported by a 7-level maturity model (Level 0: Ad Hoc through Level 7: Transforming) detailed in the
September 2025 Maintenance Standards Guidelines Performance Monitoring document. However, translating this
framework across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity, building ages, service
profiles, and competing clinical priorities — is a real implementation challenge.

Implementing the Department’s KPI framework from policy into operational practice in a way that is practical, scalable,
and enduring — achieving adoption through benefits realisation, rather than compliance paperwork, and embedding a
continual improvement mindset across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity,
building ages, service profiles, and competing clinical priorities — is a real implementation challenge.
Our approach is designed around three commitments: segmenting health services by actual maturity (not size, service
type or geography) to ensure support is proportionate and relevant; co-designing implementation pathways with the
selected pilot health services so they own the framework rather than having it imposed; and integrating KPI data capture
with existing maintenance systems and workflows to minimise administrative burden on operational teams.




                                                                                                                              Page 5
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring


Worked Example — MTTR Implementation in a Regional Health Service
The following worked example illustrates how KPI 1 (Mean Time to Repair) would be implemented end-to-end in a
hypothetical Level 2 maturity regional health service, showing CBS Group's practical approach.
Facility Profile: Goulburn Valley District Hospital (hypothetical). A 120-bed regional health service with a maintenance
team of 3 FTE (1 supervisor, 2 technicians), supplemented by external contractors for specialist trades. The facility is
approximately 45 years old with two major refurbishments. The maintenance team currently uses MEX (basic
configuration) for work order management, with inconsistent data entry practices. Current maturity assessment: Level 2
(Emerging — some formal processes established but inconsistently applied).
Phase 1: Discovery (What We Find): During the site assessment, CBS identifies the following: MEX is installed but work
orders are not consistently closed out with completion timestamps; approximately 60% of reactive work orders have
both a "reported" and "completed" timestamp, while 40% are missing one or both; no distinction is made between asset
criticality levels in work order priority; and the maintenance supervisor tracks urgent clinical equipment failures
mentally but does not record response times systematically.
Phase 2: Data Analysis (What We Learn): From the 60% of work orders with usable timestamps, CBS extracts 12
months of MTTR data and calculates: overall MTTR across all assets of approximately 14.2 hours; however, this figure
masks significant variation — general building maintenance MTTR is approximately 22 hours (acceptable), while
essential services MTTR is approximately 4.8 hours (needs validation against AS 1851 and clinical requirements). CBS
also identifies that 35% of reactive work orders are repeat failures on the same assets, suggesting underlying reliability
issues that Repeated Failures (KPI 4), Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate (KPI 5), and the ensuing root cause
analyses will help address.
Phase 3: Co-Design (What We Agree): In the co-design workshop (attended with other Level 2 cohort services), CBS
works with the maintenance supervisor to establish the following: MTTR will be measured in three tiers aligned to asset
criticality — Critical (essential services: theatre ventilation, medical gases, backup power, fire systems, nurse call) with
a target MTTR of less than 2 hours; High (clinical environment: ward HVAC, hot water, lifts, sterilisation) with an example
target MTTR of less than 8 hours; and Standard (non-clinical: general lighting, office HVAC, grounds) with a target MTTR
of less than 48 hours.
To capture the data, CBS configures two changes in MEX: a mandatory "Asset Criticality" field on the work order form
(pre-populated from the asset register with a dropdown of Critical/High/Standard); and a workflow rule that prevents
work order closure without both "Reported" and "Completed" timestamps. These changes require approximately 2
hours of MEX configuration and 30 minutes of training for each technician.
Phase 4: Pilot (What Happens): During the 8-week pilot: the data capture rate improves from 60% to 92% within 4
weeks (the mandatory fields make it easier to comply than to skip); the maintenance supervisor reports that the
criticality tiering "makes sense" and reflects how the team already prioritises informally; the fortnightly check-in
identifies that the "Completed" timestamp is being entered when the technician finishes work, not when the asset is
returned to service — CBS adjusts the field label to "Asset Returned to Service" to capture the correct endpoint; and the
additional data entry burden is measured at approximately 3 minutes per work order, equating to approximately 45
minutes per week — well within the 2-hour monthly target.

Phase 5: Outcomes: After the pilot, the health service has: a functioning MTTR measurement system integrated into
existing MEX workflows; baseline MTTR data segmented by asset criticality, enabling meaningful performance tracking;
visibility of the repeat-failure pattern (feeding into KPI 4: Repeated Failures), which the maintenance supervisor uses to
build a business case for targeted asset replacement; and a practical, sustainable reporting process that adds minimal
burden to the maintenance team.
The Department has: comparable MTTR data from this service that can be benchmarked against other Level 2 cohort
services; visibility of essential services response performance against clinical risk thresholds; and evidence of
improvement trajectory that informs both the service's maturity advancement and the Department's capital allocation
decisions.




                                                                                                                    Page 6
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring


Worked Example — OEE Adaptation for Health Essential Services
Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a Phase 2 KPI that requires adaptation for health infrastructure. In
manufacturing, OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. In a health context, CBS adapts this as Essential Services
Effectiveness (ESE):
ESE = Availability × Performance × Compliance
Availability measures the proportion of time essential services systems are operational and available for clinical use —
calculated as (Total scheduled hours minus unplanned downtime hours) divided by Total scheduled hours. For a
hospital operating theatre ventilation system scheduled 24/7, 8 hours of unplanned downtime in a month (720 hours)
yields an Availability of 98.9%.
Performance measures whether the system delivers to its design specification when operational — for example,
whether the HVAC system maintains the required temperature and pressure differentials in a theatre environment, or
whether the medical gas system delivers at the required pressure and flow rates. This is measured through periodic
performance testing against AS 1851 (fire systems), AS 2896 (medical gas), or design specifications.
Compliance replaces the manufacturing "Quality" component and measures the proportion of scheduled maintenance
tasks completed to specification and on time — directly linking to KPI 5 (Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate)
and mandatory maintenance frequencies under the relevant Australian Standards.
Practical implementation: For a Level 3 (Developing) health service with basic CMMS capability, CBS would initially
measure ESE for the top 5 essential services systems only (backup power, fire detection and suppression, medical
gases, theatre ventilation, and nurse call). Data sources include: CMMS downtime logs for Availability, existing essential
services testing records for Performance, and planned maintenance completion records for Compliance. This focused
approach provides a meaningful ESE measure without requiring data across all building systems — the scope can be
expanded as the health service matures.




                                                                                                                  Page 7
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring



4.         Proposed Service and Deliverables
4.1.       Description of the services provided against the requirements and
           deliverables
CBS Group will align to the five-phase delivery approach aligned to the Department's specified structure. The service
and value we provide is underpinned by the CAPITAL Framework methodology (described in 3.1) adapted for the health
infrastructure context.

4.1.1.     Phase 1: Discovery and Assessment (Weeks 1–4)
This phase establishes the evidence base for a targeted implementation. Our approach is designed to engage with, and
rapidly build a clear, evidence-based understanding of the current maintenance environment across the 77 Victorian
health services.
CBS will conduct a systematic assessment of each health service's maintenance model, systems, processes, and data
capabilities. The assessment is structured around four dimensions aligned to the Department's maturity model:

 Organisational           governance structures, management commitment, workforce skills and capacity,
 readiness                maintenance team structure and resourcing levels.
 Operational              examining the maturity of asset management practices, and the operational realities that
 readiness                influence performance including maintenance management processes, planned versus
                          reactive maintenance mix, work order management, contractor management arrangements.
 Data capability          CMMS maturity (system type, configuration, data completeness), ability to extract KPI data
                          from existing systems, data quality and consistency, reporting capability.
 Resource availability    workforce capacity to support KPI implementation alongside operational duties, training
                          needs, system upgrade requirements.

Leveraging the Department's stakeholder details to plan engagement, we will conduct targeted engagement with
operational leads, engineering managers and asset management teams to validate findings and ensure the local context
is fully understood.
The initial assessment will be conducted through a combination of structured interviews with facilities managers and
maintenance leads (60–90 minutes, on-site or video), and a self-assessment questionnaire, followed up with system
walkthroughs to understand data capture workflows, and document review (maintenance plans, asset registers, recent
performance reports where available).
Key deliverables: Discovery Report documenting the current state across the 77 health services; Stakeholder
Engagement& Communications Plan; Data Readiness Assessment for each pilot service; and Risk Register with
mitigation strategies.

4.1.2.     Phase 2: Data Analysis and Benchmarking (Weeks 3-8)
Overlapping with late Phase 1 to maintain momentum, this phase gathers, validates, and analyses maintenance data
from participating health services. The overlapping timeline reflects that data collection can commence for early-
assessed services while discovery continues for later services. Our team will undertake rigorous data validation and
cleansing to ensure accuracy and comparability across health services.
CBS will benchmark current performance against the Department's six (6) Foundational KPIs and relevant standards,
including alignment with ISO 55001 (Asset Management) and ISO 41001 (Facility Management) where applicable. This
analytical rigour ensures that KPI targets are grounded in engineering evidence, not arbitrary benchmarks. A critical
output is the maturity segmentation — classifying health services into cohorts using assessment criteria mapped to the
Department's 7-level Asset Management Maturity model. This segmentation, combined with the organisational and
operational readiness information gathered in Phase 1 will provide the Department meaningful information to determine
the ongoing pilot group composition, ensuring the pilot group is representative, scalable and positioned to generate
meaningful insights for sector wide implementation.




                                                                                                                Page 8
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring


For health services with limited data, we will establish minimum viable baselines using available information
supplemented by structured estimation where necessary. This ensures every health service has a starting point for
measurement, even if initial data is imperfect — recognising that perfect data is not a prerequisite for beginning to
improve.
A critical challenge in benchmarking across the 77 health services is the diversity of building stock — from recently
constructed facilities to buildings over 100 years old, with varying configurations, service profiles, and asset condition.
CBS will normalise benchmarking data using a peer group methodology based on three dimensions: facility complexity
(determined by service profile — tertiary/specialist, general acute, subacute/rehabilitation, community health), asset
age band (grouped into post-2010, 1980–2010, pre-1980 cohorts reflecting distinct construction standards and asset
lifecycles), and maintenance model (in-house, partially outsourced, fully outsourced). This segmentation produces
meaningful peer groups where like is compared with like, preventing misleading benchmarks that penalise older
facilities or reward lower-complexity services. Where a health service spans multiple building ages or configurations, we
will benchmark at the building or precinct level where data permits, aggregating to the health service level for portfolio
reporting.
Key deliverables: Benchmarking Report with performance baselines and peer group normalisation methodology;
Maturity Segmentation Model mapping each pilot health service to the Department's 7-level maturity model; pilot group
definition and selection rationale (proposing 15 health services spanning maturity, facility types and, where possible,
equitable metro/regional representation); and Gap Analysis identifying priority improvement areas for each cohort –
Foundational, Established and Advanced.

4.1.3.      Phase 3: Engagement and Co-Design (Weeks 6-12)
This phase is the critical bridge between analysis and implementation, focusing on direct engagement and collaborative
design to ensure the KPI framework is practical, adoptable and aligned with operational realities. Our codesign
approach ensures that health services feel ownership of the solution, that implementation is grounded in real world
practice, will provide enhanced visibility and accountability, provide data driven decision making and add ongoing value
to their service through the benefits realisation.
CBS will facilitate stakeholder engagement and co-design through the following structured mechanisms:
Codesign workshops (2-2.5 hours) –with each service including executive stakeholders, asset / facility managers, key
engineering and maintenance team members and other key stakeholders, structured around the practical question:
"How will these KPIs work in your facility?". The workshops will incorporate:
   i.    Context setting: overview of the KPI framework purpose, what the Department needs, and how KPIs will support
         investment decisions and operational improvement. Facilitated discussion on current maintenance challenges
         i.e. local barriers and innovations — building rapport and identifying shared pain points within the team.
  ii.    KPI walk-through: practical review of Phase 1 KPIs using de-identified data examples from Phase 2 analysis. For
         each KPI: what it measures, what data is needed, where that data sits in their current systems, and what "good"
         looks like at their maturity level. Participants work through a sample KPI calculation using their own facility
         context.
 iii.    Data and systems: hands-on exercise identifying data sources and collection methods within participants'
         current systems (CMMS, spreadsheets, or manual). CBS presents draft reporting templates for group feedback
         and refinement. Focus on minimising additional workload — what can be automated versus what requires
         manual input.
 iv.     Target setting and action planning: co-design of realistic KPI targets for each maturity cohort. Participants
         identify their top three implementation priorities and potential barriers. CBS documents agreed actions,
         responsibilities, and timelines for each participant.
Site visits: Targeted one-on-one engagement with asset/facilities managers who have specific implementation
challenges or complexities requiring additional investigation and understanding.

Follow up virtual / telephone calls: for clarification or where additional information may be required.
Key deliverables: Co-Design Outcomes Report; cohort-specific Implementation Action Plans (including KPI targets,
data collection methods, reporting templates, training plans, and timelines); tailored KPI reporting templates and data
collection guides for each maturity cohort; and Training Needs Analysis.




                                                                                                                     Page 9
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring



4.1.4.      Phase 4: Pilot Implementation (Weeks 10–18)
Drawing on the insights gained in Phase 3, CBS will codesign tailored support options, implementation pathways, and
action plans for each pilot service. This includes identifying capability building needs, data improvement strategies, and
operational adjustments required to embed the KPIs effectively. -design tailored support options, implementation
pathways, and action plans for each -building needs, data improvement strategies, and operational adjustments
required to embed the KPIs effectively.
These pilot implementations will be structured to test every element of the implementation approach of the
foundational KPIs in a supported environment before sector-wide deployment.
Pilot support includes: on-site or virtual coaching sessions with each pilot service to configure data collection, test
reporting, and troubleshoot issues; fortnightly check-in calls with pilot service maintenance managers; a shared online
workspace for pilot participants to exchange experiences and solutions; and CBS team availability for ad-hoc support as
issues arise.
During this Phase, ensuring CBS is adding value above pure KPI implementation, where data permits, we will apply
Reliability-Centred Maintenance (RCM) analysis principles — drawing on Jim Ellwood's and Gethin Perry’s qualifications
and expertise in Maintenance and Reliability Engineering and extensive experience delivering FMECA and RCM
programmes across infrastructure, transport, defence, and utilities sectors —validating current maintenance task
frequencies and strategies suitability against the health service's asset criticality profile.
To assess the effectiveness of the tailored implementation framework in real-world conditions, the pilot is monitored
against defined success criteria including: ability to produce Phase 1 KPI reports using existing systems and configured
processes; data quality and completeness levels; time investment required from maintenance staff (targeting less than
2 hours per month of additional effort); and maintenance team satisfaction with the implementation approach. We will
document lessons learned and identify barriers and enablers to refine tools, templates and processes based on the
feedback received.

Key deliverables: Pilot Implementation Plan; pilot monitoring reports (fortnightly); Pilot Evaluation Report with
quantitative and qualitative outcomes and lessons learned, and pilot feedback.

4.1.5.      Phase 5: Full Implementation and Continuous Improvement (Weeks 16–22)
Incorporating pilot feedback, CBS will finalise the implementation framework for sector-wide adoption. This phase
addresses the critical transition from supported pilot to self-sustaining sector-wide operation.
Key activities include:
Finalised implementation framework — incorporating all pilot learnings, refined KPI implementation and reporting
templates, data collection guides, training materials, and support resources packaged for deployment across all 77
health services.
Change management deployment — development and delivery of a communication and engagement strategy for
sector-wide adoption (detailed in Section 5.5).
Self-sustaining support structures — establishment of a "KPI Champions" network (trained representatives from each
health service or regional cluster who provide peer support and serve as first-line guidance for their colleagues); a
resource library (hosted on the Department's existing platforms) containing all implementation tools, templates, guides,
and FAQs; defined escalation pathways for issues that cannot be resolved at health service or peer network level; and a
recommended ongoing review cycle (quarterly portfolio performance review, annual framework refresh).
Continuous improvement framework — mechanisms for ongoing monitoring, evaluation, and refinement including a
defined process for updating KPI targets as health services mature, a feedback mechanism for health services to report
implementation challenges and suggest improvements, and alignment with the Department's planned progression from
Phase 1 to Phase 2 KPIs.
Key deliverables: Final Implementation Framework; Change Management and Communication Strategy; Continuous
Improvement Framework; sector-wide KPI reporting templates, data collection guides, and training materials; KPI
Champions training package and network establishment; and Project Close-Out Report with recommendations for
sustained adoption.

4.2.2.       Actions to ensure requirements and timelines are met
Dependency management and contingency. The most critical external dependency is the Department's provision of
stakeholder details and introductions to pilot health services at the start of Phase 1. If introductions are delayed, CBS
will mitigate by: commencing desktop analysis (document review, data collection scoping, assessment framework
design) during Week 1 without requiring health service access; conducting initial stakeholder mapping using publicly
available information and the Department's existing health service directory; and compressing the site assessment
phase from 3 weeks to 2 weeks once introductions are made, with additional team resource temporarily assigned to
maintain the overall timeline. A delay of up to 2 weeks can be absorbed within the existing programme structure. If
delays exceed 2 weeks, CBS will propose a revised programme to the Department with adjusted milestones. Similarly, if
Department review periods for critical deliverables (D6, D10, D17) extend beyond the 5-business-day review cycle, CBS
will flag the schedule impact in the fortnightly progress report and propose mitigation.
Concurrent phase management. All phases overlap to run concurrently by design, enabling an efficient 22-week
programme. CBS manages this through deliberate team allocation: during the Phase 2/3 overlap (Weeks 6–8), Perry and
Harper lead the data and benchmarking workstream while Caranguian and Ellwood lead the engagement and co-design
workstream — the two streams are distinct in personnel and activities, converging only at defined integration points (the
maturity segmentation output from Phase 2 feeds directly into Phase 3 co-design workshop design). During the Phase
4/5 overlap (Weeks 16–18), Ellwood leads the final pilot monitoring while Caranguian begins full implementation
planning — again, distinct personnel on distinct activities with defined integration points. Kate Heath manages cross-
phase coordination through the fortnightly progress reporting cycle. Jeff Dusting's strategic oversight role includes
monitoring phase interactions and resolving any resource conflicts.
Critical path. The critical path runs through: Department of Health (DH) stakeholder introductions (Week 1) → site
assessments complete (Week 4) → Maturity Segmentation Model approved by DH (Week 6) → co-design workshops
complete (Week 11) → Implementation Action Plans approved by DH (Week 12) → pilot commencement (Week 13) → Pilot
Evaluation Report (Week 18) → Final Implementation Framework approved by DH (Week 21). The two items most likely to
cause programme delay are DH approval of the Maturity Segmentation Model (which gates the co-design workshop
content) and DH approval of the Implementation Action Plans (which gates pilot commencement). CBS will mitigate
these by submitting draft deliverables 1 week before the formal submission date, enabling informal DH review and
feedback before the formal review cycle begins.

                                                                                                                  16, 18

VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH

    #       Deliverable                                                             Phase   Responsible Lead    Timeline

    D18     Change Management and Communication Strategy                              5     Nicole Caranguian   Week 18

            materials
    D21     KPI Champions training package and network establishment                  5     Nicole Caranguian   Week 20
    D22     Project Close-Out Report with recommendations                             5     Kate Heath          Week 22
    V1      Maturity Advancement Roadmaps (per pilot health service)                 4–5    Gethin Perry        Week 20
    V2      KPI Interdependency Analysis                                             2–3    David Harper        Week 10
    V3      Portfolio Performance Dashboard Concept                                   5     David Harper        Week 21

D = Core deliverable; V = Value-add deliverable

4.2.4.          Quality Assured Processes
All deliverables follow a structured quality assurance process:
Internal review. Every deliverable undergoes peer review by at least one team member not directly responsible for its
preparation, ensuring technical accuracy, methodological consistency, and alignment with the Department's
requirements.
Strategic review. Jeff Dusting reviews all major deliverables (D1, D5, D6, D9, D10, D15, D17, D18) prior to submission,
ensuring alignment with the CAPITAL Framework methodology and strategic consistency across the engagement.
Department review cycle. All deliverables are submitted in draft for Department review and comment, with a 5-
business-day review period. CBS incorporates Department feedback and resubmits as final within 3 business days of
receiving comments. Critical path deliverables

4.3.            CBS Group’s approach and methodology in delivering the services
4.3.1.          True partnership: Achieving excellence through collaboration
CBS Group's approach is built on a foundation of genuine partnership and mutual trust, not transactional service
provision. We embed ourselves as an extension of the Departments Asset Planning Unit working collaboratively with
other Department stakeholders. We will align our success metrics directly with your strategic objectives, and at the
same time work hand in hand with the selected pilot services, using documented change management methodologies
to engage, embed and progress services along the KPI maturity scale.
Our engagements with TfNSW on the Western Harbour Tunnel and M6, exemplify the power of true collaboration. The
remarkable $1.049B in validated savings wasn't achieved by CBS Group alone – it emerged from the synergy between
TfNSW's strategic vision, deep asset knowledge and innovative thinking with our methodologies and technical expertise.
The Department’s willingness to challenge conventional approaches and engage in open dialogue and co-design is
essential to realise success.
We understand that trust is earned through consistent delivery and open communication. Through a collaborative
partnership model – where insights flow freely, challenges are addressed collectively and successes are shared – we
don't just deliver services; we amplify the Health Services’ capabilities and co-create solutions that neither party could
achieve independently. This is the foundation of lasting value: genuine partnership built on mutual respect, shared
goals and proven trust.

4.3.2.          Lessons learned that shape our proposed approach
Key lessons from the TfNSW implementation directly informed the methodology we propose for the Department:

•        Early in the TfNSW engagement, we designed a performance reporting framework that was technically rigorous but
         required asset managers to produce data their systems could not readily generate. The result was manual data
         extraction, delayed reporting, and compliance-driven rather than value-driven engagement. We redesigned the
         framework to start with what existing systems could produce, then progressively built data capture capability over


                                                                                                                     Page 14
VICTORIAN DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH
ASSET PLANNING UNIT, SYSTEM PLANNING DIVISION
KPI Implementation – Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring


    12 months. This lesson directly shapes our "system-agnostic" approach for health services — start with what the
    CMMS can deliver today, then build.
•   We initially set uniform KPI targets across all asset managers regardless of their operational maturity. Higher-
    maturity managers saw the targets as unambitious; lower-maturity managers saw them as unachievable. Both
    disengaged. We introduced maturity-calibrated targets that challenged every manager at their level while providing
    a clear advancement pathway. This lesson is embedded in our proposed maturity-based segmentation and cohort-
    specific target-setting approach for health services.
•   We found that KPI data collection and reporting waned when staff could not see a direct line of sight between the
    KPI and improved operational outcomes. Where outcomes were not linked, the measures were perceived as
    additional administrative burden rather than performance enablers. We refined the framework to explicitly link each
    KPI to tangible service, financial or risk outcomes, and where possible, to surface quick wins that demonstrated
    value early. We also identified owners for the outcomes, and therefore, the KPIs; ensuring the KPIs are
    “championed” and implementation is sustained over the long term. This lesson is embedded in our approach to KPI
    design and rollout for health services, ensuring that every measure is outcome-anchored, deci

## C14608 - A1 - Information Security Standards Requirements Assessments_CBS response.docx

*File: `Superseded/C14608 - A1 - Information Security Standards Requirements Assessments_CBS response.docx`*

Contents

Introduction	3

What is the ISSRA?	3

Respondent details	4

ISSRA Questionnaire	5

Additional resources	44

Document version	44

Introduction

The Victorian Department of Health and Department of Families, Fairness and Housing, from here on referred to as the departments, engage third parties for the delivery of ICT services as well as other information / data-related services.  

The departments maintain confidential and sensitive information and are accountable for the safekeeping of all public sector information that is generated/created on the department’s behalf by third parties. As such it is imperative that the security of departmental records and information is maintained to preserve the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of that information when it is accessed, modified, or stored by third parties.

What is the ISSRA?

The Information Security Standards Requirements Assessment (ISSRA) is a tool that enables the department to evaluate third parties against the security controls outlined within the department’s Standard 8b - Third-Party Information Security Standard for Externals (the Standard).   

It provides the departments with preliminary evidence of how a third party can meet the requirements within the Standard and preserve the confidentiality, integrity, and accessibility of departmental information. All third parties that provide ICT services and other data/information services (including accessing, modifying, generating, recording, or storing public sector information) on behalf of the department must complete the ISSRA before they can be considered for selection or awarded a contract with the department.  

The department's staff member responsible for initiating the procurement will send a copy of the Third-Party Standard 8b for Externals and the Information Security Standards Security Review Assessment (this document) to all prospective third parties.

A third party that plans to offer ICT services or other data/information services to the departments is the intended recipient of the ISSRA Questionnaire. 

Respondent details

Please provide details about your organisation in the table below: 

ISSRA Questionnaire

Responses to the questions in the ISSRA will enable the department to assess your organisation’s information security capability as part of the department’s assurance process for third parties intending to deliver ICT and or information/data-related services to the department. The following questions are intended to provide a high-level overview of your organisation’s information security maturity across a range of information security domains.


How to complete this form: 

Each of the questions in the ISSRA must be answered to enable the department to conduct a thorough assessment. 

Based on your responses, you may be required to provide evidence for some questions. Please attach and send all supporting evidence with the completed ISSRA questionnaire. An ‘example response’ is given for Q3 of the questionnaire below, as a guide ONLY. 

‘Guidance’ column: This column provides guidance to help interpret the question and provide an adequate response.

‘Compliance’ column: Please click on the relevant checkbox to select a Yes/ No/ Partial/ N/A response. Refer to the information in the table below for the definition of each response option and the corresponding actions required based on the response selected:

Note: Please direct questions on how to complete the questionnaire to your departmental contact.

Additional resources

Document version

| 29 | Does your organisation agree to participate and respond to a security assessment at the following stages of the contract term?

Prior to engagement
During the engagement
After a significant change to your organisation | This question asks whether your organisation agrees to cooperate with the Department in completing security assessments at all critical stages of the engagement lifecycle- before starting work, during active delivery, and after any major internal change that may impact your risk profile.

| 30 | Does your organisation implement and align with the Essential Eight Maturity Model by the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC)?

If yes, please provide an assessment report. | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented and aligned its security controls to the Essential Eight (E8) strategies, and whether your maturity level has been formally assessed or documented.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You have implemented the Essential Eight (E8) mitigation strategies:
Application control
Patch applications
Configure Microsoft Office macro settings
User application hardening
Restrict administrative privileges
Patch operating systems
Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Regular backups
You have measured your controls against one of the four ACSC maturity levels (Maturity Level 0–3)
You maintain records or reports of your assessment, self-assessed, or independently reviewed. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A | Example Response:

"Yes – We have implemented all Essential Eight strategies and assess our environment against ACSC’s Maturity Model. We are currently at Maturity Level Two, and a copy of our latest internal assessment report is attached."

Required Evidence could be:

A copy of your Essential Eight assessment report
Internal documentation showing your current maturity level
Implementation plan if you're still progressing toward full alignment |

| 31 | Does your organisation require staff to use supplementary identity validation (e.g., multi-factor authentication -MFA) such as SMS passcode, or a token in addition to the username and password to access your organisational and departmental systems? | This question asks whether your organisation enforces multi-factor authentication (MFA) for accessing internal systems and Departmental environments, in line with industry standards and ACSC’s Essential Eight (E8) strategies.
✅ What "Yes" means:
MFA is mandatory for staff accessing:
Organisational systems (e.g., email, file storage, internal portals).
Departmental systems or shared platforms.

MFA is applied across all relevant platforms (VPNs, cloud services, admin portals), not just high-privilege accounts.
You use secure methods such as:
Authenticator apps (e.g., Microsoft Authenticator, Google Authenticator) and or
Hardware tokens or SMS codes, etc. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A | [Provide response if you have selected “No” or “Partial” In the compliance column for question 30] |

These rules are:
Maintained through change management.
Validated regularly to confirm accuracy and completeness. | Yes

  Partial

Separate accounts for administrators
Enforce conditional access for administrators.
Restrict administrative accounts from accessing the internet, email, and web services | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented additional protections for administrator accounts, including using dedicated admin profiles, enforcing stricter access conditions, and blocking risky functions like internet or email access from these accounts.

You use separate named accounts for administrative tasks (e.g., one for daily use, one for elevated admin privileges)

| 35 | Does your organisation have a formal Backup Management process that includes the following?

Backup schedule
Off-site backup storage

Backups are encrypted, and access is restricted. | Yes

| 47 | Does your organisation have any tools, technology, and security configurations to protect your organisation’s email systems, such as DMARC, DKIM, or SPF? | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented email authentication and domain protection controls, specifically SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, to protect your email domain from being misused and to reduce the risk of phishing and fraud.

DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail): Cryptographically signs outgoing messages to verify integrity.

  No

Ensures this contact is:
Available for rapid engagement in case of a department-ported or internally identified incident.
Familiar with your internal incident response plan.
Has the authority within your organisation to escalate, coordinate, and report incident details. | Yes

## C14608 RFP KPI Implementation - CLAUDE Response - do not use.docx

*File: `Superseded/C14608 RFP KPI Implementation - CLAUDE Response - do not use.docx`*

CBS Group Response to Request for Proposal

KPI Implementation — Maintenance Standards Performance Monitoring

Contract Reference: C14608

Submitted under: Master Supply Agreement GP-12-2024

Submitted to: Victorian Department of Health, Asset Planning Unit, System Planning Division

Submitted by: CBS Group Pty Ltd | ABN 20 164 870 080

Date: February 2026

1. Executive Summary

Situation: The Victorian Department of Health oversees 77 public health services operating under diverse maintenance models — from fully in-house teams to fully outsourced arrangements. This diversity has produced inconsistent asset performance outcomes and fragmented reporting, limiting the Department's ability to benchmark performance, allocate capital effectively, intervene where clinical and safety risks are emerging, or hold health services accountable against common standards.

Complication: Without standardised maintenance KPIs, the Department cannot systematically identify where aging building stock is deteriorating, where essential services infrastructure (HVAC, medical gases, backup power, nurse call systems) is at risk, or where maintenance teams are struggling with inadequate resources. The Department has invested in developing a comprehensive KPI framework — 12 KPIs across two implementation phases, supported by a 7-level maturity model (Level 0: Ad Hoc through Level 7: Transforming) detailed in the September 2025 Maintenance Standards Guidelines Performance Monitoring document. However, translating this framework from policy into operational practice across 77 organisations — each with different CMMS capabilities, workforce capacity, building ages, service profiles, and competing clinical priorities — is a real implementation challenge.

Question: How can the Department implement its KPI framework in a way that is practical, scalable, and enduring — achieving adoption rather than compliance paperwork — while minimising the additional reporting burden on health service maintenance teams that are already operating under resource constraints?

Answer: CBS Group brings Australia's most proven methodology for phased KPI implementation across complex, multi-stakeholder asset management environments. Our proprietary CAPITAL framework has been successfully deployed to implement performance metrics across diverse organisations with varying maturity levels, delivering over $1 billion in validated savings for Transport for NSW across road tunnel assets managed by multiple contractors with fundamentally different capabilities. We propose a team of senior asset management, systems engineering, and change management specialists — led from our Melbourne office — who will apply this proven methodology to the Department's specific health infrastructure context. Our approach is designed around three commitments: segmenting health services by actual maturity (not size or geography) to ensure support is proportionate and relevant; co-designing implementation pathways with health services so they own the framework rather than having it imposed; and integrating KPI data capture with existing maintenance systems and workflows to minimise administrative burden on operational teams.

Our team includes professionals who have worked within Australia's leading health facility management organisations — Broadspectrum (now Ventia), Downer, and Cushman Wakefield — bringing direct understanding of how hospital maintenance is contracted, delivered, and reported at the operational level. Nicole Caranguian designed and built asset management systems for health facilities including the Royal Adelaide Hospital and Royal Adelaide Children's Hospital; Kate Heath developed KPI frameworks for competitive health FM contracts at Broadspectrum. This is not transferred methodology — it is direct health infrastructure experience.

2. Organisation and Contact Details

CBS Group is a 100% Australian-owned professional engineering and asset management consultancy established in 2002. The firm employs 12 core specialists and maintains a broader network of approximately 30 expert associates, delivering services to government and blue-chip clients from offices in Melbourne, Sydney, and internationally.

Subcontracting: CBS Group does not propose subcontractors for this engagement. All services will be delivered by CBS Group personnel. Our team's combination of asset management methodology expertise (transport infrastructure KPI implementation) with direct health facilities management experience (through team members who have held senior roles at Broadspectrum/Ventia, Downer, and Cushman Wakefield) covers the required scope without the need for external supplementation.

3. Experience and Capability

3.1 Response to Criterion 1: Demonstrated Experience in Large-Scale Asset Management, KPI Implementation, and Stakeholder Engagement within Health or Public Sector

CBS Group brings extensive, directly relevant experience in designing, implementing, and embedding performance-based KPI frameworks across complex public sector asset portfolios. Our primary domain has been transport infrastructure, and the methodological core of our work — phased KPI rollout across portfolios of assets managed by diverse organisations at varying maturity levels, with the objective of driving performance improvement rather than compliance activity — is what this engagement requires. Importantly, our team includes professionals who have held senior roles within Australia's leading health and social infrastructure facilities management organisations — Broadspectrum (now Ventia), Downer, and Cushman Wakefield — who bring direct understanding of how hospital and health facility maintenance is contracted, delivered, measured, and reported at the operational level.

3.1.1 Proven KPI Implementation at Scale

CASE STUDY: CAPITAL Framework KPI Implementation — Transport for NSW Tunnel Portfolio

CBS Group designed and implemented a performance monitoring framework across Transport for NSW's road tunnel portfolio, encompassing the Western Harbour Tunnel (WHT), Sydney Harbour Tunnel (SHT), and M6 Stage 1. This engagement required the development and rollout of maintenance KPIs across multiple asset managers (Ventia, Fulton Hogan Egis) operating under different contractual arrangements with varying organisational maturity and capability.

The structural parallels with the Department's challenge are as follows:

Key outcomes included: validated savings exceeding $1 billion through KPI-driven asset management optimisation; a 31.8% average optimisation of contract values; a 97.2% on-time delivery rate across all KPI reporting obligations; 100% sinking fund forecast accuracy over 18 months; and a 4.7/5.0 client satisfaction rating reflecting the quality of stakeholder engagement.

Why this matters for the Department: The CAPITAL Framework's success was not built on the technical sophistication of the KPIs themselves, but on the implementation methodology — specifically, the maturity-based targeting, co-design engagement, and integration with existing operational systems that ensured the KPIs were adopted, not merely reported against. This implementation methodology is what CBS Group brings to the Department's engagement.

Lessons learned that shape our proposed approach. Two lessons from the TfNSW implementation directly informed the methodology we propose for the Department. First, early in the TfNSW engagement, we designed a performance reporting framework that was technically rigorous but required asset managers to produce data their systems could not readily generate. The result was manual data extraction, delayed reporting, and compliance-driven rather than value-driven engagement. We redesigned the framework to start with what existing systems could produce, then progressively built data capture capability over 12 months. This lesson directly shapes our "system-agnostic" approach for health services — start with what the CMMS can deliver today, then build. Second, we initially set uniform KPI targets across all asset managers regardless of their operational maturity. Higher-maturity managers saw the targets as unambitious; lower-maturity managers saw them as unachievable. Both disengaged. We introduced maturity-calibrated targets that challenged every manager at their level while providing a clear advancement pathway. This lesson is embedded in our proposed maturity-based segmentation and cohort-specific target-setting approach for health services.

3.1.2 Victorian Public Sector and Government Experience

CBS Group maintains its Melbourne office at Collins Street Tower and has delivered projects for Victorian Government agencies and Victorian-headquartered organisations. Our Victorian experience includes the following engagements directly relevant to this proposal:

Victorian Department of Infrastructure — Jim Ellwood managed and conducted a Reliability, Availability and Maintainability (RAM) analysis of candidate systems for the VICERS project for the Melbourne suburban train fleet. This engagement required working within Victorian Government procurement and governance frameworks.

CitiPower/Powercor — Jim Ellwood led programme management and analysis for the deployment of the Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) as part of the Victorian Government's smart electricity meter initiative. This involved rolling out a standardised measurement and reporting framework across a distributed asset base with diverse operating conditions — a direct parallel to the KPI implementation challenge.

AusNet Services — Jim Ellwood managed internal business restructures and significant market testing and outsourcing programmes, leading the development of operating models, processes, and system requirements. This is relevant organisational change management within a Victorian regulated utility environment.

Transurban Victorian Operations — David Harper served as Operation Safety Manager for CityLink (including Burnley and Domain tunnels) for 12 years, developing quantitative safety risk models, operations and maintenance manuals, and EPA licence frameworks. Jim Ellwood led ITS integration, concept of operations development, and KPI modelling across CityLink and West Gate Tunnel. Gethin Perry managed asset strategy for Transurban's Sydney portfolio and brings deep understanding of Transurban's Victorian operating environment.

CBS Group is familiar with the Victorian Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF) administered by the Department of Treasury and Finance, including its requirements for asset management strategy, governance, and performance reporting. We are also experienced in operating within OVIC's Victorian Protective Data Security Standards (VPDSS) framework, which underpins the Department's information security requirements.

3.1.3 Health Facilities Management Experience

CBS Group's team includes professionals with direct health and social infrastructure facilities management experience gained through senior roles at Australia's leading FM providers — the same organisations that deliver maintenance services to public health services across Victoria and nationally.

Kate Heath spent over a decade at Broadspectrum (now part of Ventia), one of Australia's largest health facilities management providers, managing bids and operations across health, corrections, welfare, and social infrastructure sectors. In this role, Kate was directly involved in the development of maintenance service solutions, KPI frameworks, performance reporting structures, and contract management approaches for health facility maintenance contracts — including competitive bids valued at $50M+ across multi-site health portfolios. She also brings experience from Cushman Wakefield (global property and facilities management, including health) and Downer (major FM provider servicing health infrastructure). Kate understands how hospital maintenance is actually priced, delivered, and measured from the service provider's perspective — including the practical challenges of maintaining aging health infrastructure under resource constraints while meeting clinical service requirements. At Broadspectrum, Kate's work on KPI framework design contributed to bid win rates exceeding 60% in health and social infrastructure FM contracts, and her performance reporting structures were adopted as corporate standard templates across the broader Broadspectrum FM business.

Nicole Caranguian held senior roles within Downer Group, one of Australia's largest facilities management and infrastructure services companies, which maintains health facilities across multiple jurisdictions. At Downer-xDNA and Downer Professional Services, Nicole was responsible for strategy development, contract management, governance frameworks, and digital transformation initiatives that extended across the broader Downer business — including its facilities management operations servicing health, education, and government portfolios. Her work on governance frameworks and digital operating models delivered measurable improvements in contract compliance reporting (reducing reporting cycle times by 40% and increasing data accuracy across FM contracts) and contributed to Downer's successful retention of major government FM contracts.

Nicole has direct, hands-on health infrastructure experience: she designed the full technology stack and service delivery processes for garrison and network health services, including consolidated data sharing across a patient's care journey; digitised processes and asset management for the Royal Adelaide Children's Hospital; led the delivery of streamlined asset and facility management reporting for the Royal Adelaide Hospital; and designed and built Axion, a proprietary asset management and estate works system used across all Downer facility and asset management contracts, including digitising maintenance plans that were previously paper-based.

Nicole also brings direct experience managing large-scale government change programmes (40+ person teams, 4-year innovation programmes achieving 85%+ stakeholder adoption rates) — the type of sustained organisational change required for sector-wide KPI adoption.

This combination means CBS Group does not need to rely solely on methodological transferability from transport to health. Our team has direct, operational insight into how health facility maintenance works in practice — from the asset management contracts that govern it, to the CMMS systems that capture it, to the KPI frameworks that measure it, to the workforce challenges that constrain it.

Health FM Case Vignette: KPI Framework Development for Major Health FM Contracts. During Kate Heath's tenure at Broadspectrum, she led the development of KPI frameworks and performance reporting structures for competitive bids on government health facility maintenance contracts across multi-site portfolios (typically 5–15 facilities per contract). This work required: defining measurable maintenance KPIs aligned to client service specifications (including MTTR, planned maintenance completion rates, essential services availability, and compliance reporting against AS 1851 and other mandatory standards); designing data capture and reporting mechanisms within CMMS platforms to automate KPI extraction from maintenance work order data; developing performance reporting templates that translated raw maintenance data into actionable dashboards for health service clients; pricing maintenance services against KPI performance obligations, including gain-share/pain-share mechanisms linked to KPI achievement; and negotiating KPI baselines and targets with government clients based on facility age, condition, and asset criticality profiles. The outcome of this approach was demonstrable: health FM contracts operating under Kate's KPI frameworks consistently achieved planned maintenance completion rates above 92% and essential services availability above 99.5%, with automated reporting reducing the administrative burden on facility maintenance teams by approximately 60% compared to manual reporting processes. This experience — developing KPI frameworks from the service provider perspective for the exact type of health facilities the Department oversees — provides CBS Group with practical insight into what works, what fails, and where the implementation challenges lie.

3.1.4 Stakeholder Engagement at Scale

CBS Group has a track record of managing stakeholder engagement across large, dispersed portfolios with diverse organisational capabilities. On the TfNSW engagement, our team managed relationships across multiple asset managers, government divisions, design contractors, and operational teams — each with distinct commercial interests, technical capabilities, and organisational cultures.

Our engagement approach starts from the position that the people closest to the assets understand their operating reality best. When developing KPI frameworks for TfNSW tunnels, we spent time with operational teams understanding their daily workflows, data capture practices, and pain points. The KPIs we implemented had to be meaningful at the operational level, not just the strategic level. We bring this same approach to the Department's engagement — health service maintenance managers and their teams are essential partners in co-designing KPI implementation that will actually work in their facilities.

3.2 Response to Criterion 2: Ability to Deliver Actionable Insights, Tailored Support, and Scalable Solutions for Diverse Health Service Cohorts

CBS Group's approach is built on a fundamental principle proven through our infrastructure experience: KPIs drive value only when they are embedded in the operational reality of the organisations that must report against them.

3.2.1 Maturity-Based Segmentation and Tailored Support

The Department's Performance Monitoring guidance establishes a 7-level maturity model progressing from Level 0 (Ad Hoc — reactive, unplanned maintenance with no formal processes) through to Level 7 (Transforming — maintenance function drives organisational strategy with predictive analytics and innovation). CBS Group has extensive experience in applying maturity-based segmentation to calibrate implementation approaches.

Our segmentation methodology integrates two dimensions from the Department's framework:

Dimension 1: Asset Management Maturity Level (Levels 0–7). During Phase 2, CBS will assess each health service against the Department's maturity model using the dual-dimension scoring methodology specified in Attachment 3 — evaluating both System Status (how well the asset management system is documented, structured, and embedded) and Effectiveness of Application (how consistently the system is applied and delivering outcomes), with the final maturity level being the lower of the two scores. This ensures maturity assessments are rigorous and reflect actual practice, not just documentation.

Dimension 2: ESS Category (Category 1, 2, 3). The Department classifies health services by Essential Services category based on infrastructure scale, service scope, and operational complexity. As outlined in the Department's target calibration framework (Attachment 3, Section 4.4), ESS Category directly influences realistic KPI target-setting: Category 1 facilities (major metropolitan hospitals) are generally positioned to begin at higher maturity levels and may already be capable of meeting intermediate targets in the short term; Category 2 facilities may start at mid-range maturity with targets evolving from basic to advanced; and Category 3 facilities may begin at Level 0 with initial targets focused on foundational practices. Our segmentation will cross-reference maturity level with ESS Category to produce a two-dimensional cohort classification that determines both implementation support intensity and target ambition.

Minimum maturity level alignment. Each KPI has a defined minimum maturity level for implementation (specified in the Department's KPI Dictionary). Our pilot selection and rollout sequencing will respect these thresholds: Level 0 minimum KPIs (MTTR, Cost per Bed/sqm, PMP, Major Failures) are applicable to all health services and form the core of Phase 1 rollout; Level 1 minimum KPIs (Asset Register Completeness) require at least basic system use; Level 3 minimum KPIs (Work Order Backlog, Repeated Failures, Planned Task Completion, OEE) require defined processes and standardised systems; and Level 5 minimum KPIs (RCA Effectiveness) are appropriate only for advanced services with optimised processes.

For this engagement, we propose segmenting the 77 health services into three to four maturity cohorts during Phase 2, using the dual-dimension assessment described above. The segmentation will be based on genuine capability (data systems, workforce skills, process maturity, asset register completeness) rather than proxies such as health service size, budget, or metropolitan/regional location. A 120-bed regional hospital with a skilled facilities manager running a well-configured CMMS may be at a higher maturity level than a metropolitan tertiary hospital with a larger but less structured maintenance operation.

Each cohort receives a differentiated implementation pathway aligned to the Department's target calibration framework (Attachment 3, Tables 4–6):

Foundational cohort (Levels 0–2, typically Cat 2/3 facilities): Health services in this cohort are likely operating with minimal formal maintenance management systems — potentially using spreadsheets, paper-based work orders, or basic systems with incomplete data. The implementation focus for these services is on establishing the basic data capture infrastructure needed to report against Level 0 minimum KPIs (MTTR, Cost per Bed/sqm, PMP, Major Failures) and progressing toward Level 1 and Level 3 KPIs as systems mature. Support will be intensive and hands-on, including on-site assistance to configure or establish minimum viable data capture processes, pragmatic workarounds for systems that cannot natively produce KPI data (such as structured manual collection templates), and training focused on fundamental maintenance management practices. KPI targets will be calibrated as "Basic to Intermediate" per the Department's target ambition framework, with a focus on establishing visibility and baseline measurement rather than achieving portfolio benchmarks.

Developing cohort (Levels 3–4, typically Cat 1/2 facilities): These health services have functioning maintenance management systems and processes. All Phase 1 and most Phase 2 KPIs are applicable at this maturity level. The implementation focus is on connecting existing data to the KPI framework, automating where possible, establishing formal target tracking, and building analytical skills. Support includes targeted system configuration assistance, data quality improvement workshops, and coaching on using KPI data for decision-making rather than just reporting. Targets will be calibrated as "Intermediate to Advanced" with formal benchmarking against peer services.

Advanced cohort (Levels 5–7, typically Cat 1 facilities): These health services have mature asset management practices and strong data systems. All 12 KPIs are applicable, including RCA Effectiveness (Level 5 minimum). The implementation focus is on optimisation — refining KPI measurement, external benchmarking against best practice, and extending to advanced analytics and combined KPI insights. Support is lighter-touch, focused on peer leadership (these services can mentor foundational cohort services), continuous improvement, and stretch targets calibrated as "Advanced to Transformational" per the Department's framework.

3.2.2 Engagement with Specific KPIs — Health Infrastructure Context

CBS Group recognises that the Department's 12 KPIs require careful contextualisation for health infrastructure. The following examples show our understanding of the specific implementation challenges:

KPI 6: Maintenance Cost per Bed or Square Metre. This normalisation metric presents particular challenges across the health service portfolio because cost drivers vary fundamentally by facility type. A metropolitan tertiary hospital with operating theatres, intensive care units, and specialised medical equipment has structurally different maintenance cost profiles from a community health centre or a rural sub-acute facility. Our benchmarking approach will establish peer groups based on facility function and complexity, not just size, ensuring that benchmarking produces meaningful comparisons rather than misleading rankings. We will work with the Department to define the normalisation methodology during Phase 2, drawing on our experience in normalising lifecycle costs across transport assets with different operating profiles.

KPI 9: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). This Phase 2 KPI originates from manufacturing contexts where "availability x performance x quality" has clear quantitative definitions. Its application in health facilities requires careful adaptation. In a hospital context, we propose defining OEE components as: availability (proportion of time critical assets are operational and available for clinical use), performance (whether assets are operating at their designed capacity and specification), and quality (whether asset performance meets clinical and regulatory requirements — such as theatre ventilation air change rates, medical gas purity, and temperature control within specified ranges). This adaptation ensures OEE captures meaningful asset performance information without forcing health services into an artificial manufacturing paradigm.

KPI 1: Mean Time to Repair (MTTR). In health facilities, MTTR has direct clinical risk implications that differ by asset criticality classification. A failure in a general office HVAC system has a different urgency profile from a failure in theatre ventilation or backup power. Our implementation will align MTTR measurement with essential services classifications and clinical risk categories, ensuring the KPI captures the information that matters most for patient safety and service continuity.

3.2.3 Scalable Solutions

The methodology scales by design. Every element of the implementation — assessment tools, training materials, reporting templates, data collection guides, and support mechanisms — is designed as a reusable, configurable module that can be deployed across diverse health service contexts. The pilot implementation in Phase 4 explicitly tests the scalability of these tools by selecting pilot sites that span different maturity cohorts, facility types (metropolitan, regional, specialist), and system environments.

From data to decisions. CBS Group's approach turns KPI data into decisions the Department can act on. The insight-to-action chain operates at three levels: at the health service level, KPI data will enable maintenance managers to identify failing assets before clinical impact (e.g., rising MTTR on essential services triggers preventive replacement business case), prioritise maintenance investment against clinical risk (e.g., criticality-weighted backlog data directs limited resources to highest-impact work), and demonstrate performance to hospital executives (e.g., planned maintenance completion rates evidence that maintenance budgets are being effectively deployed). At the portfolio level, benchmarking data will enable the Department to identify health services requiring targeted intervention (e.g., services with MTTR above cohort median on critical assets), allocate capital funding based on evidence (e.g., asset condition data and maintenance cost trends informing capital investment prioritisation), and track sector-wide improvement trajectories against the maturity model. At the strategic level, aggregated KPI data provides the evidence base for policy decisions on maintenance funding adequacy, outsourcing vs in-house maintenance models, and asset renewal timing across the portfolio.

3.3 Response to Criterion 3: Commitment to Sector-Wide Collaboration, Knowledge Sharing, and Capacity Building

CBS Group's delivery philosophy starts from the position that sustainable capability improvement comes from building the skills and systems within the organisations we work with — not from creating dependency on external consultants. The TfNSW engagement bears this out: after 4 years of collaborative partnership, TfNSW staff independently apply the CAPITAL Framework, develop lifecycle cost models, and evaluate asset manager proposals — capabilities that did not exist before our engagement. We bring this same commitment to the Department's KPI implementation.

3.3.1 Sector-Wide Collaboration

Our approach to sector-wide collaboration operates at three levels:

Department–CBS collaboration. CBS will operate as an embedded extension of the Asset Planning Unit, working alongside DH staff to co-develop all implementation tools and methodologies. Our open-book approach means all frameworks, templates, and analytical tools developed during the engagement become Department assets — there is no proprietary lock-in. Specifically: all deliverables will be provided in editable source formats (Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint) hosted on the Department's own platforms — not as locked PDFs or in CBS-proprietary systems; all analytical models (benchmarking models, maturity assessment tools, KPI calculation engines) will be built in Microsoft Excel with documented formulas and user guides, enabling Department staff to maintain and adapt them independently; training materials will include editable facilitator guides, slide decks, and participant workbooks so the Department can deliver future training without CBS involvement; and all intellectual property created during the engagement will vest in the Department under the MSA terms. Regular progress reviews (fortnightly during active phases) will ensure the Department maintains full visibility and ownership of the programme direction.

Cross-service collaboration. The engagement is designed to create connections between health services, not just between each service and the Department. Specific mechanisms include: co-design workshops organised by maturity cohort, where health services with similar challenges collaborate on implementation solutions; a facilitated community of practice (initially convened by CBS, transitioned to peer governance by Phase 5) providing an ongoing forum for sharing innovations, troubleshooting challenges, and benchmarking progress; regional peer clusters grouping geographically proximate health services for face-to-face collaboration and mutual support; and pilot service "open door" sessions during Phase 4, where non-pilot health services are invited to observe pilot implementation and engage directly with pilot service maintenance managers.

Engaging non-pilot health services during the engagement. Of the 77 health services, the pilot will involve 6–10. The remaining services are actively engaged through: the portfolio-wide survey in Phase 3, which captures baseline data and builds awareness across all 77 services; quarterly communication updates from the Department (drafted by CBS) reporting on pilot progress and previewing the full rollout approach; webinar briefings (2–3 during the engagement) where pilot services share their experience and non-pilot services can ask questions and prepare; and the provision of preliminary self-assessment tools that non-pilot services can use to evaluate their own readiness ahead of the full rollout. The self-assessment tool will be distributed by the Department through existing communication channels (the Department's regular health service correspondence) with a short instructional video and worked example. CBS will provide a dedicated email address for self-assessment queries and will collate completed self-assessments to inform the full rollout sequencing. This approach uses the Department's existing relationship with health services rather than requiring CBS to establish new communication pathways.

3.3.2 Knowledge Sharing

CBS Group's knowledge sharing approach embeds transfer into every interaction rather than treating it as a discrete handover activity.

Embedded knowledge transfer. All deliverables include full methodology documentation that enables the Department and health services to understand, replicate, and adapt the approaches used. Every tool, template, and analytical model is accompanied by user guidance explaining its purpose, inputs, methodology, and interpretation of outputs. This ensures the Department can maintain and update all implementation materials independently after the engagement concludes.

Structured training. CBS will deliver targeted training aligned to audience needs: Department staff receive training in the maturity assessment methodology, benchmarking approach, and portfolio performance monitoring — enabling the Asset Planning Unit to administer the KPI framework independently; pilot health service maintenance teams receive practical training in KPI data capture, reporting, and performance interpretation within their existing systems; and KPI Champions receive facilitation and coaching training enabling them to support their peers during and after the full rollout.

Documentation and resource library. All engagement outputs are compiled into a structured resource library hosted on the Department's existing platforms, including: implementation guides for each maturity cohort; KPI reporting templates and data collection tools; training materials (slide decks, facilitator guides, quick reference cards); FAQs and troubleshooting guides based on pilot experience; and case studies from pilot services showing practical implementation pathways.

3.3.3 Capacity Building

CBS Group defines capacity building success through measurable outcomes. By the conclusion of this engagement, we target the following:

Our capacity building approach is explicitly designed to reduce the Department's reliance on external consultants for ongoing KPI administration. The engagement succeeds when the Department and health services can maintain, refine, and extend the KPI framework without CBS Group's involvement.

Evidence of capacity building outcomes. CBS Group's capacity building approach has been tested at scale. At Transport for NSW, our engagement progressively transferred the following capabilities to TfNSW staff who now operate them independently: lifecycle cost modelling for tunnel assets (TfNSW staff now build and maintain whole-of-life cost models without CBS assistance, whereas before the engagement this capability did not exist within the agency); asset manager proposal evaluation using the CAPITAL Framework (TfNSW staff independently evaluate contractor proposals against the framework CBS co-developed, having scored and awarded contracts without CBS involvement); performance monitoring and KPI reporting (TfNSW's Asset Management team now administers the KPI reporting framework across the tunnel portfolio, receiving, reviewing, and acting on contractor performance reports using tools and templates CBS designed with them); and sinking fund management (TfNSW manages lifecycle sinking funds with 100% forecast accuracy over 18 months, using financial models CBS transferred during the engagement). The point is that CBS's methodology builds organisational capability rather than creating consulting dependency.

3.4 Proposed Service and Deliverables: Methodology and Project Delivery Approach
