At Transport for NSW, our engagement progressively transferred the following capabilities to TfNSW staff who now operate them independently: lifecycle cost modelling for tunnel assets (TfNSW staff now build and maintain whole-of-life cost models without CBS assistance, whereas before the engagement this capability did not exist within the agency); asset manager proposal evaluation using the CAPITAL Framework (TfNSW staff independently evaluate contractor proposals against the framework CBS co-developed, having scored and awarded contracts without CBS involvement); performance monitoring and KPI reporting (TfNSW's Asset Management team now administers the KPI reporting framework across the tunnel portfolio, receiving, reviewing, and acting on contractor performance reports using tools and templates CBS designed with them); and sinking fund management (TfNSW manages lifecycle sinking funds with 100% forecast accuracy over 18 months, using financial models CBS transferred during the engagement). The point is that CBS's methodology builds organisational capability rather than creating consulting dependency.

3.4 Proposed Service and Deliverables: Methodology and Project Delivery Approach

The following section addresses the 11 methodology sub-criteria specified in the RFP evaluation table: understanding of key issues and risks (3.4.1); identification of critical activities and outputs (3.4.2, 3.4.3a); sequencing of activities (3.4.2, 3.4.3); communication of key activities (3.4.2); collaboration and lateral thinking (3.3, 3.4.4); culture of transparency and cooperation (3.3, 3.6); robust project plan with milestones, timelines, resources (3.4.3, 3.4.3b); clear understanding of objectives, issues and risks (3.4.1); transparent stakeholder communication (3.6.2); risk identification and mitigation (3.4.1); and leveraging best-practice frameworks (3.1.1, 3.4.2, 3.4.4).

3.4.1 Understanding of Key Issues and Risks

CBS Group has identified the following key issues and risks based on our analysis of the RFP, the Department's Performance Monitoring guidance document, and our experience implementing KPI frameworks across complex multi-stakeholder environments.

1. Data quality, availability, and system diversity. Health services at lower maturity levels are likely to have incomplete, inconsistent, or inaccessible maintenance data. Phase 1 KPIs (MTTR, Reactive Maintenance SLA Compliance, Maintenance Work Order Backlog, Repeated Failures, Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate, Maintenance Cost per Bed/sqm) assume a level of systematic data capture that may not exist uniformly. Some health services may use enterprise CMMS platforms (such as MEX, Maximo, or Pronto), others may use basic systems or spreadsheets, and some may rely on paper-based processes. Our approach addresses this through early data readiness assessment in Phase 1, pragmatic data collection workarounds for lower-maturity services, and a system-agnostic data requirements specification that defines what information is needed rather than prescribing which system must produce it.

Mitigation: Phase 1 Discovery includes a specific data readiness assessment for each pilot health service, cataloguing the CMMS platform in use and its current configuration. Phase 2 develops minimum viable data collection templates for services that cannot natively produce KPI data from existing systems. CBS will work directly with common CMMS platforms used across Victorian health services — including MEX (widely deployed in Australian health and government), IBM Maximo (used by larger health services and shared services arrangements), and Pronto Xi (common in regional health services) — to configure standard KPI extraction reports. For services using spreadsheet-based or manual systems, we will develop structured data collection workbooks with built-in validation and automated KPI calculation that can be progressively migrated to a CMMS as the service matures. Our approach does not require health services to change their systems — we configure reporting within whatever platform they currently use.

2. Organisational resistance and competing priorities. Health service maintenance teams are typically lean and focused on keeping facilities operational. KPI implementation may be perceived as an additional compliance burden imposed by head office, particularly where maintenance budgets have been under sustained pressure. Clinical priorities will always — and should always — take precedence over reporting activities.

Mitigation: Our co-design approach (Phase 3) involves health services in shaping how KPIs apply to their context, building ownership rather than imposing obligations. We design data capture to integrate with existing workflows rather than creating parallel processes. We frame KPIs as tools that strengthen the case for maintenance investment, not as surveillance mechanisms.

3. Normalisation and benchmarking validity. Meaningful benchmarking across 77 health services requires appropriate normalisation that accounts for facility type, age, complexity, service profile, and geographic context. Without sound normalisation, benchmarking will produce misleading comparisons that undermine confidence in the framework.

Mitigation: Phase 2 develops facility-type peer groups and normalisation methodologies that account for structural differences in cost drivers and performance profiles. Benchmarking is presented as a tool for identifying improvement opportunities, not as a league table.

4. Essential services and clinical risk. Health facility maintenance has direct patient safety implications that distinguish it from other infrastructure contexts. Asset failures in critical clinical systems (theatre ventilation, medical gases, backup power, sterilisation, nurse call) carry clinical risk that must be reflected in KPI prioritisation and escalation frameworks. Health services are also subject to specific Australian Standards governing essential services maintenance, including AS 1851 (Routine Service of Fire Protection Systems and Equipment), AS 2896 (Medical Gas Systems), and AS/NZS 3003 (Electrical Installations in Patient Care Areas), which impose mandatory maintenance frequencies and performance requirements that must be reflected in KPI measurement.

Mitigation: Our KPI implementation will align with essential services classifications, clinical risk categories, and applicable Australian Standards maintenance requirements. The Victorian Health Building Authority (VHBA), which plays a central role in health infrastructure planning and standards across the state, provides additional guidance on facility performance expectations that will inform our benchmarking and target-setting methodology. KPI measurement frameworks will be cross-referenced against mandatory maintenance obligations under AS 1851, AS 2896, and AS/NZS 3003 to ensure that essential services compliance is visible within the KPI reporting structure — enabling health services to demonstrate compliance performance alongside operational performance in a single framework.

5. Sustaining momentum beyond pilot. The transition from a supported pilot environment to self-sustaining sector-wide adoption is the highest-risk phase. Without ongoing support structures, early adoption gains are likely to decay.

Mitigation: Phase 5 establishes self-sustaining support mechanisms — peer networks, KPI champion training, resource libraries, and escalation pathways — designed to maintain momentum after the engagement concludes.

3.4.2 Project Delivery Approach

CBS Group proposes a five-phase delivery approach aligned to the Department's specified structure, underpinned by the CAPITAL Framework methodology adapted for the health infrastructure context.

CAPITAL Framework — Health Infrastructure Application

The CAPITAL Framework's seven core capabilities, originally developed for transport infrastructure, apply directly to the Department's KPI implementation challenge:

Phase 1: Discovery and Assessment (Weeks 1–4)

This phase establishes the evidence base for a targeted implementation. CBS will conduct a systematic assessment of each pilot health service's maintenance model, systems, processes, and data capabilities. The assessment is structured around four dimensions aligned to the Department's maturity model:

Organisational readiness — governance structures, management commitment, workforce skills and capacity, maintenance team structure and resourcing levels.

Operational readiness — maintenance management processes, planned versus reactive maintenance mix, work order management, contractor management arrangements.

Data capability — CMMS maturity (system type, configuration, data completeness), ability to extract KPI data from existing systems, data quality and consistency, reporting capability.

Resource availability — workforce capacity to support KPI implementation alongside operational duties, training needs, system upgrade requirements.

The assessment will be conducted through a combination of structured interviews with facilities managers and maintenance leads (60–90 minutes, on-site or video), system walkthroughs to understand data capture workflows, and document review (maintenance plans, asset registers, recent performance reports where available). We will use the Department's stakeholder details to plan engagement and will supplement this with our own stakeholder mapping where gaps are identified.

Key deliverables: Discovery Report documenting the current state across pilot health services; Stakeholder Engagement Plan; Data Readiness Assessment for each pilot service; and Risk Register with mitigation strategies.

Phase 2: Data Analysis and Benchmarking (Weeks 3–8)

Overlapping with late Phase 1 to maintain momentum, this phase gathers, validates, and analyses maintenance data from participating health services. The overlapping timeline reflects that data collection can commence for early-assessed services while discovery continues for later services.

CBS will benchmark current performance against the Department's 12 KPIs (focusing on Phase 1 KPIs initially) and relevant standards, including alignment with ISO 55001 (Asset Management) and ISO 41001 (Facility Management) where applicable. Where data permits, we will apply Reliability-Centred Maintenance (RCM) analysis principles — drawing on Jim Ellwood's Masters qualification in Maintenance and Reliability Engineering and his extensive experience delivering FMECA and RCM programmes across infrastructure, defence, and utilities sectors — to validate whether current maintenance task frequencies and strategies are appropriate for the health service's asset criticality profile. This analytical rigour ensures that KPI targets are grounded in engineering evidence, not arbitrary benchmarks. A critical output is the maturity segmentation — classifying health services into cohorts using assessment criteria mapped to the Department's 7-level model. The segmentation informs pilot group composition and tailored implementation pathways for each cohort.

For health services with limited data, we will establish minimum viable baselines using available information supplemented by structured estimation where necessary. This ensures every health service has a starting point for measurement, even if initial data is imperfect — recognising that perfect data is not a prerequisite for beginning to improve.

A critical challenge in benchmarking across the 77 health services is the diversity of building stock — from recently constructed facilities to buildings over 100 years old, with varying configurations, service profiles, and asset condition. CBS will normalise benchmarking data using a peer group methodology based on three dimensions: facility complexity (determined by service profile — tertiary/specialist, general acute, subacute/rehabilitation, community health), asset age band (grouped into post-2010, 1980–2010, pre-1980 cohorts reflecting distinct construction standards and asset lifecycles), and maintenance model (in-house, partially outsourced, fully outsourced). This segmentation produces meaningful peer groups where like is compared with like, preventing misleading benchmarks that penalise older facilities or reward lower-complexity services. Where a health service spans multiple building ages or configurations, we will benchmark at the building or precinct level where data permits, aggregating to the health service level for portfolio reporting.

Key deliverables: Benchmarking Report with performance baselines and peer group normalisation methodology; Maturity Segmentation Model mapping each pilot health service to the Department's 7-level maturity model; pilot group definition and selection rationale (proposing 6–10 health services spanning maturity cohorts and facility types); and Gap Analysis identifying priority improvement areas for each cohort.

Phase 3: Engagement and Co-Design (Weeks 6–12)

This phase is the critical bridge between analysis and implementation. CBS will facilitate stakeholder engagement through three structured mechanisms:

Co-design workshops (half-day, in-person where possible). Facilitated sessions with health service maintenance managers and key stakeholders, structured around the practical question: "How will these KPIs work in your facility?" Workshops are organised by maturity cohort to ensure peer relevance. A typical workshop follows this structure:

Sample half-day co-design workshop agenda (3.5 hours):
Session 1 (45 min) — Context setting: overview of the KPI framework purpose, what the Department needs, and how KPIs will support investment decisions and operational improvement. Facilitated discussion on current maintenance challenges — building rapport and identifying shared pain points within the cohort.
Session 2 (60 min) — KPI walk-through: practical review of Phase 1 KPIs using de-identified data examples from Phase 2 analysis. For each KPI: what it measures, what data is needed, where that data sits in their current systems, and what "good" looks like at their maturity level. Participants work through a sample KPI calculation using their own facility context.
Session 3 (45 min) — Data and systems: hands-on exercise identifying data sources and collection methods within participants' current systems (CMMS, spreadsheets, or manual). CBS presents draft reporting templates for group feedback and refinement. Focus on minimising additional workload — what can be automated versus what requires manual input.
Session 4 (45 min) — Target setting and action planning: co-design of realistic KPI targets for each maturity cohort. Participants identify their top three implementation priorities and potential barriers. CBS documents agreed actions, responsibilities, and timelines for each participant.

Participants include: health service facilities/maintenance manager, one maintenance team representative, and (where available) a finance or performance reporting contact. DH Asset Planning Unit staff attend as observers. CBS provides a facilitator (Nicole Caranguian) and a technical adviser (Jim Ellwood) for each workshop.

Structured interviews (45–60 minutes, on-site or video). Targeted one-on-one engagement with facilities managers who cannot attend workshops or who have specific implementation challenges requiring individual attention. These interviews capture granular insights about local barriers and innovations.

Online survey. A brief, targeted survey distributed to all 77 health services to capture baseline information about maintenance management systems, processes, and perceived barriers to KPI adoption. This extends engagement beyond the pilot group and provides portfolio-wide intelligence for the full rollout plan.

Key deliverables: Co-Design Outcomes Report; cohort-specific Implementation Action Plans (including KPI targets, data collection methods, reporting templates, training plans, and timelines); tailored KPI reporting templates and data collection guides for each maturity cohort; and Training Needs Analysis.

Phase 4: Pilot Implementation (Weeks 10–18)

CBS will implement the KPI framework with selected pilot health services (recommended 6–10 services spanning maturity cohorts and facility types: metropolitan, regional, specialist). The pilot is structured to test every element of the implementation approach in a supported environment before sector-wide deployment.

Pilot support includes: on-site or virtual working sessions with each pilot service to configure data collection, test reporting, and troubleshoot issues; fortnightly check-in calls with pilot service maintenance managers; a shared online workspace for pilot participants to exchange experiences and solutions; and CBS team availability for ad-hoc support as issues arise.

The pilot is monitored against defined success criteria including: ability to produce Phase 1 KPI reports using existing systems and configured processes; data quality and completeness levels; time investment required from maintenance staff (targeting less than 2 hours per month of additional effort); and maintenance team satisfaction with the implementation approach.

Key deliverables: Pilot Implementation Plan; pilot monitoring reports (fortnightly); Pilot Evaluation Report with quantitative and qualitative outcomes and lessons learned; and revised Implementation Action Plans incorporating pilot feedback.

Phase 5: Full Implementation and Continuous Improvement (Weeks 16–22)

Incorporating pilot feedback, CBS will finalise the implementation framework for sector-wide adoption. This phase addresses the critical transition from supported pilot to self-sustaining sector-wide operation.

Key activities include:

Finalised implementation framework — incorporating all pilot learnings, refined KPI reporting templates, data collection guides, training materials, and support resources packaged for deployment across all 77 health services.

Change management deployment — development and delivery of a communication and engagement strategy for sector-wide adoption (detailed in Section 3.5).

Self-sustaining support structures — establishment of a "KPI Champions" network (trained representatives from each health service or regional cluster who provide peer support and serve as first-line guidance for their colleagues); a resource library (hosted on the Department's existing platforms) containing all implementation tools, templates, guides, and FAQs; defined escalation pathways for issues that cannot be resolved at health service or peer network level; and a recommended ongoing review cycle (quarterly portfolio performance review, annual framework refresh).

Continuous improvement framework — mechanisms for ongoing monitoring, evaluation, and refinement including a defined process for updating KPI targets as health services mature, a feedback mechanism for health services to report implementation challenges and suggest improvements, and alignment with the Department's planned progression from Phase 1 to Phase 2 KPIs.

Key deliverables: Final Implementation Framework; Change Management and Communication Strategy; Continuous Improvement Framework; sector-wide KPI reporting templates, data collection guides, and training materials; KPI Champions training package and network establishment; and Project Close-Out Report with recommendations for sustained adoption and Phase 2 KPI implementation.

3.4.3 Project Plan Summary

Dependency management and contingency. The most critical external dependency is the Department's provision of stakeholder details and introductions to pilot health services at the start of Phase 1. If introductions are delayed, CBS will mitigate by: commencing desktop analysis (document review, data collection scoping, assessment framework design) during Week 1 without requiring health service access; conducting initial stakeholder mapping using publicly available information and the Department's existing health service directory; and compressing the site assessment phase from 3 weeks to 2 weeks once introductions are made, with additional team resource temporarily assigned to maintain the overall timeline. A delay of up to 2 weeks can be absorbed within the existing programme structure. If delays exceed 2 weeks, CBS will propose a revised programme to the Department with adjusted milestones. Similarly, if Department review periods for critical deliverables (D6, D10, D17) extend beyond the 5-business-day review cycle, CBS will flag the schedule impact in the fortnightly progress report and propose mitigation.

Concurrent phase management. Phases 2–3 and 4–5 run concurrently by design, enabling an efficient 22-week programme. CBS manages this through deliberate team allocation: during the Phase 2/3 overlap (Weeks 6–8), Perry and Harper lead the data and benchmarking workstream while Caranguian and Ellwood lead the engagement and co-design workstream — the two streams are distinct in personnel and activities, converging only at defined integration points (the maturity segmentation output from Phase 2 feeds directly into Phase 3 co-design workshop design). During the Phase 4/5 overlap (Weeks 16–18), Ellwood leads the final pilot monitoring while Caranguian begins full implementation planning — again, distinct personnel on distinct activities with defined integration points. Kate Heath manages cross-phase coordination through the fortnightly progress reporting cycle. Jeff Dusting's strategic oversight role includes monitoring phase interactions and resolving any resource conflicts.

Critical path. The critical path runs through: DH stakeholder introductions (Week 1) → site assessments complete (Week 4) → Maturity Segmentation Model approved by DH (Week 6) → co-design workshops complete (Week 11) → Implementation Action Plans approved by DH (Week 12) → pilot commencement (Week 13) → Pilot Evaluation Report (Week 18) → Final Implementation Framework approved by DH (Week 21). The two items most likely to cause programme delay are DH approval of the Maturity Segmentation Model (which gates the co-design workshop content) and DH approval of the Implementation Action Plans (which gates pilot commencement). CBS will mitigate these by submitting draft deliverables 1 week before the formal submission date, enabling informal DH review and feedback before the formal review cycle begins.

3.4.3a Consolidated Deliverables Register

D = Core deliverable; V = Value-add deliverable

3.4.3b Primary Delivery Personnel by Phase

The RFP pricing structure allows two personnel lines per phase. The following table identifies the two primary delivery personnel for each phase, noting that Jeff Dusting's strategic oversight and quality assurance is included at no additional cost, and other team members contribute as required within the phase fee.

3.4.3c Quality Assurance Process

All deliverables follow a structured quality assurance process:

Internal review. Every deliverable undergoes peer review by at least one team member not directly responsible for its preparation, ensuring technical accuracy, methodological consistency, and alignment with the Department's requirements.

Strategic review. Jeff Dusting reviews all major deliverables (D1, D5, D6, D9, D10, D15, D17, D18) prior to submission, ensuring alignment with the CAPITAL Framework methodology and strategic consistency across the engagement.

Department review cycle. All deliverables are submitted in draft for Department review and comment, with a 5-business-day review period. CBS incorporates Department feedback and resubmits as final within 3 business days of receiving comments. Critical path deliverables (Maturity Segmentation Model, Implementation Action Plans, Final Implementation Framework) include a structured review workshop rather than written feedback alone.

3.4.4 Value-Add to Project Deliverables

Beyond the specified requirements, CBS Group offers the following value-add elements:

Maturity Advancement Roadmap. For each participating health service, a clear pathway from current maturity level to target state with defined milestones. This gives individual health services and the Department a shared view of the improvement trajectory and the support needed to achieve it.

KPI Interdependency Analysis. A mapping of how the 12 KPIs interact and influence each other — for example, how improving Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate (KPI 5) is likely to reduce Repeated Failures (KPI 4) and improve MTTR (KPI 1). This analysis enables health services to prioritise effort for maximum cross-KPI impact.

Portfolio Performance Dashboard Concept. A design specification (not a built system) for a Department-level dashboard enabling real-time visibility of KPI performance across the health service portfolio, segmented by cohort, region, facility type, and maturity level. This concept can be implemented using the Department's existing reporting platforms and provides a foundation for ongoing portfolio oversight.

3.5 Response to Criterion 4: Adherence to Relevant Government Guidelines, Standards, and Compliance Requirements

CBS Group has a strong track record of operating within government regulatory and compliance frameworks. Our approach to compliance is substantive rather than procedural — we integrate compliance requirements into our delivery methodology rather than treating them as separate obligations.

Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF). The Department's KPI framework operates within the Victorian AMAF administered by DTF. CBS Group's methodology is grounded in ISO 55001 (Asset Management), which shares foundational principles with the AMAF, including requirements for: an asset management policy and strategy linked to organisational objectives; defined roles, responsibilities, and accountabilities for asset management; performance evaluation and monitoring frameworks; and continual improvement mechanisms. Our implementation approach will ensure that KPI frameworks developed for health services are consistent with their AMAF obligations, particularly the requirements around asset management accountability, planning, and performance evaluation.

ISO Standards. Our approach draws on the following international standards: ISO 55001:2014 (Asset Management — Management Systems), which provides the overarching framework for asset management practice; and ISO 41001:2018 (Facility Management — Management Systems), which is the applicable standard for health facility maintenance and provides specific guidance on facility service delivery, performance measurement, and continual improvement. The Department's 12 KPIs align well with both standards' requirements for performance evaluation and monitoring.

Australian Standards for Essential Services. Our KPI implementation will be cross-referenced against mandatory maintenance standards applicable to health facilities, including: AS 1851:2012 (Routine Service of Fire Protection Systems and Equipment), which prescribes mandatory inspection, testing, and maintenance frequencies for fire detection, suppression, and evacuation systems; AS 2896:2021 (Medical Gas Systems — Installation and Testing), which governs the maintenance and performance verification of medical gas reticulation systems; and AS/NZS 3003:2018 (Electrical Installations — Patient Care Areas), which specifies maintenance requirements for electrical systems in clinical environments. Alignment with these standards ensures that KPI measurement captures essential services compliance as an integral component of maintenance performance.

Victorian Health Building Authority (VHBA). We acknowledge the VHBA's role in health infrastructure planning, design guidance, and performance standards across Victorian health services. Our benchmarking methodology and KPI target-setting will be informed by VHBA standards and guidelines where applicable, ensuring alignment with the broader health infrastructure governance framework.

Information Security. CBS Group commits to completing the Information Security Standards Requirements Assessment (ISSRA) in full. Our security practices are aligned with the Victorian Protective Data Security Standards (VPDSS) and the Australian Government's Essential Eight mitigation strategies. Specific commitments include: multi-factor authentication enforced across all CBS systems and personnel; application control and administrative privilege restriction; regular patching (critical patches within 48 hours, routine within 2 weeks); daily backups with offsite replication; encryption at rest and in transit for all Department data; no offshore data processing or storage; formal access provisioning and deprovisioning processes with access reviews; documented incident response plan, tested annually; and personnel security awareness training completed annually by all staff.

CBS Group holds Cyber Security insurance with an APRA-approved insurer. We will participate in Department security assessments and audits at engagement commencement and at intervals during the engagement as required.

Privacy and Data Handling. CBS Group will handle all Department and health service data in accordance with the Victorian Privacy and Data Protection Act 2014 and the Health Records Act 2001. We will apply appropriate protective markings to all documents and data in accordance with the Department's information classification framework. We will not use AI tools to process Department data without prior written approval from the Department.

Professional Standards. Our team members are registered professionals with Engineers Australia and hold relevant certifications including Chartered Engineer (CEng), Fellow of Engineers Australia (FIEAust), Certified Fellow of Asset Management (CFAM), and ISO 55001 certification.

Victorian Government Strategic Alignment. CBS Group acknowledges the Department's System Planning Division's responsibility for addressing climate threats and integrating broader government directions into infrastructure planning. Our KPI implementation approach supports the Victorian Government's sustainability and resilience objectives by: establishing asset condition visibility that enables proactive maintenance (reducing emergency interventions and associated environmental impact); supporting evidence-based capital investment planning that enables prioritisation of energy efficiency and climate resilience upgrades; and providing data foundations that can be extended to capture energy consumption and environmental performance metrics as the KPI framework matures.

3.6 Response to Criterion 5: Robust Change Management and Communication Planning

Implementing KPIs across 77 health services with diverse maturity levels is a change management challenge first and a technical one second. The technical KPI framework is well-developed in the Department's Performance Monitoring guidance. The implementation requires sustained behavioural and cultural change across organisations with competing clinical priorities and constrained maintenance resources.

3.6.1 Change Management Approach

CBS Group's change management approach draws on three lessons from our experience implementing performance frameworks across complex multi-stakeholder environments.

Principle 1: Co-ownership over compliance. KPIs that are imposed generate resistance; KPIs that are co-designed generate ownership. Our Phase 3 co-design process is not a consultation exercise — it is a collaborative design process where health service maintenance managers shape how the framework applies to their facility. Practically, this means: workshops are structured around the question "How will this work in your facility?" rather than "Here is what you must do"; health services choose which data collection method works best within their existing systems; KPI targets are negotiated based on current maturity, not imposed as universal benchmarks; and local innovations and workarounds are captured and shared across the portfolio.

Principle 2: Minimise burden, maximise value. Every element of the KPI implementation is designed to minimise additional administrative workload for maintenance teams. Specifically: data capture is integrated with existing maintenance management systems and workflows (not parallel reporting); for services with capable CMMS platforms, we configure automated KPI reports that require minimal manual intervention; for services with basic systems, we design structured data collection templates that require less than 2 hours per month of additional effort; and KPI data is framed as a tool that strengthens the business case for maintenance investment (helping facilities managers advocate for the resources they need), not as a top-down surveillance mechanism.

Principle 3: Visible early wins. Momentum comes from early wins. Our pilot implementation (Phase 4) is designed to generate visible, communicable wins that can be shared across the sector. By selecting pilot sites that span different maturity levels and facility types, we create relatable proof points for every cohort. CBS will produce a Pilot Outcomes Bulletin (2–4 pages) at the conclusion of Phase 4, co-authored with pilot health service facilities managers, documenting specific wins such as: a regional service discovering through MTTR analysis that a simple change to parts stocking eliminates 40% of repeat callouts; or a metropolitan service using backlog data to secure approval for an additional maintenance technician. This bulletin will be distributed by the Department to all 77 health services via existing communication channels (email distribution, intranet, and asset management circulars) and presented at the Phase 5 webinar briefings. Each win will be framed as "Health Service X used KPI Y to achieve Z" — concrete, relatable, and attributable.

Principle 4: Managing resistance constructively. Not all stakeholders will welcome external involvement in measuring their maintenance performance. CBS has extensive experience engaging resistant stakeholders — at TfNSW, introducing performance-based contracts with established asset managers required navigating organisational resistance from contractors who viewed KPI transparency as a threat to their commercial position. Our approach to resistant health service stakeholders follows a structured escalation pathway: first, listen and acknowledge — resistance often signals legitimate concerns about resource constraints, data quality, or fear of unfair comparison; second, reframe the value proposition — for a facilities manager under pressure, the most powerful argument for KPI adoption is that it strengthens their case for additional maintenance funding ("your data shows you need two more technicians, and now you have the evidence to make that case to your CEO"); third, adjust the approach — for health services where resistance is driven by real capacity constraints, we will work with the Department to agree a reduced-scope initial implementation (fewer KPIs, longer timeline) rather than forcing full compliance; and fourth, escalate only if necessary — if a health service remains resistant after sustained engagement, we will advise the Department on the appropriate escalation pathway, noting that our role is to support adoption, not to enforce compliance. CBS will identify stakeholder engagement risk early (Phase 1 assessment) and flag high-risk services to the Department for proactive management.

3.6.2 Communication Strategy

CBS Group will develop and deliver a multi-layered communication strategy:

Executive-level communications to health service CEOs and Chief Operating Officers positioning the KPI framework as a tool for improvement and investment justification, not surveillance. These communications will be developed in collaboration with the Department and tailored to the health service governance context.

Operational-level communications to facilities managers and maintenance teams explaining practical impacts on daily work, framed around the commitment to minimise additional burden and the value that KPI data provides for their resource advocacy.

Peer-to-peer mechanisms — a community of practice among health service facilities managers and maintenance leads, facilitated initially by CBS Group and transitioned to Department/peer governance by the end of the engagement. This includes quarterly virtual forums for sharing experiences and solutions, a shared online resource library, and regional cluster groups for face-to-face peer support where geographic proximity permits.

Progress communications to the Department providing regular visibility of adoption rates, data quality trends, emerging challenges, and good practice examples across the portfolio.

Communication timeline. The following schedule provides a summary of key communication activities mapped to the project timeline: Phase 1 (Weeks 1–4) — executive briefing letter from DH to pilot health service CEOs introducing the engagement and CBS's role; CBS introductory emails to pilot service facilities managers; stakeholder mapping and communication preferences captured. Phase 2 (Weeks 3–8) — portfolio-wide awareness communication from DH to all 77 health services introducing the KPI programme; first community of practice invitation distributed. Phase 3 (Weeks 6–12) — co-design workshop invitations and pre-reading distributed; post-workshop summary communications to participants; first quarterly sector update drafted for DH distribution. Phase 4 (Weeks 10–18) — pilot commencement notification to pilot services; fortnightly pilot progress updates to DH; mid-pilot webinar for non-pilot services (Week 14); Pilot Outcomes Bulletin (Week 18). Phase 5 (Weeks 16–22) — second quarterly sector update; full rollout preview communication to all 77 services; KPI Champions network launch communication; community of practice first independent meeting; close-out communication summarising achievements and next steps.

3.6.3 Change Management Risks

Beyond the technical delivery risks identified in Section 3.4.1, CBS has identified three change-specific risks that require distinct management strategies.

Change Risk 1: Health service disengagement during pilot. There is a risk that pilot health services initially committed to participation may lose momentum during the 8-week pilot, particularly if staff turnover, competing clinical priorities, or operational pressures reduce available maintenance team capacity. Mitigation: CBS will identify a named KPI Champion at each pilot site during Phase 3 (co-design) and establish a direct, regular relationship with that individual. If a pilot service shows signs of disengagement (missed data submissions, cancelled check-ins), CBS will escalate to the Department within one reporting cycle and propose remediation — which may include temporarily reducing the scope of KPI data collection or providing additional on-site support to get the service back on track.

Change Risk 2: Executive-level resistance to transparency. Health service CEOs or boards may resist KPI reporting if they perceive it as exposing poor maintenance performance to the Department, particularly if there is concern about funding implications or reputational comparison with other services. Mitigation: The executive communications strategy (developed in Phase 3) will explicitly frame KPIs as an evidence base for increased maintenance investment, not as a performance compliance mechanism. CBS will work with the Department to ensure that early communications from senior DH leadership reinforce this message. Benchmarking data will be presented to individual health services as "your performance relative to your cohort" rather than published league tables.

Change Risk 3: Data integrity manipulation. Once KPIs are implemented, there is a risk that some health services may adjust data entry practices to achieve favourable KPI results without genuine performance improvement — for example, closing work orders prematurely to reduce MTTR, or reclassifying reactive work as planned maintenance to improve planned maintenance completion rates. Mitigation: CBS will build data validation checks into the KPI reporting templates (cross-referencing related KPIs to detect anomalies — e.g., if MTTR improves dramatically but repeat failure rates remain high, this suggests premature work order closure rather than genuine improvement). The continuous improvement framework (Phase 5) will include periodic data quality audits as a standard component of the Department's ongoing portfolio oversight.

Pilot success stories packaged as case studies for sector-wide dissemination during Phase 5, showing practical value across different facility types and maturity levels.

3.6.4 Sustainability Beyond the Engagement

We have designed the approach to leave the Department and health services self-sufficient after the engagement concludes. This works through: full documentation of all implementation tools, templates, processes, and methodologies; a trained network of "KPI Champions" capable of providing first-line peer support and serving as the ongoing link between health services and the Department; an established community of practice with defined governance, communication channels, and meeting cadence; a continuous improvement framework with clear review cycles, escalation pathways, and a process for updating KPI targets as health services mature; and a transition plan for Phase 2 KPI implementation, building on the infrastructure established during this engagement.

KPI Champion identification and recruitment. We acknowledge that health service facilities managers and maintenance leads are already resource-constrained, and asking them to take on an additional "Champion" role requires a practical and realistic approach. CBS will identify suitable Champions during Phases 1 and 3 by looking for individuals who demonstrate: an interest in data-driven maintenance improvement (often the person already producing informal reports or advocating for system upgrades); the trust of their maintenance team (the Champion needs peer credibility, not seniority); and a baseline comfort with their CMMS or data tools. The Champion role is defined as a maximum of 2 hours per month — attendance at the quarterly community of practice forum, plus ad hoc peer support within their health service. This is not a formal appointment requiring management approval but a voluntary, peer-recognition role that confers professional development value. For health services where no suitable individual is identified during the engagement, the Department will serve as the direct support link until a Champion emerges through the community of practice network.

Community of practice transition. We acknowledge that self-sustaining professional networks typically require 12–18 months of active facilitation before they operate independently. A 5-month engagement cannot fully mature a community of practice, and we do not claim otherwise. Our approach establishes the infrastructure, governance, and initial momentum during the engagement, with the following staged transition: during the engagement (Months 1–5), CBS convenes and facilitates all community of practice activities, establishing the cadence, format, platform, and norms; in Months 6–9 (post-engagement), the Department's Asset Planning Unit takes the facilitation role using the materials, templates, and facilitator guides CBS has provided — CBS can be available for light-touch advisory support during this transition if the Department wishes to extend the engagement on a limited basis; and from Month 10 onwards, peer governance emerges, with rotating facilitation among experienced KPI Champions and the Department providing strategic oversight rather than operational facilitation. CBS will include a recommended governance charter and transition plan as a specific deliverable (within D17: Final Implementation Framework) to give the Department a clear roadmap for this transition.

4. Proposed Team

CBS Group proposes a lean, senior team that combines deep asset management and KPI implementation methodology with direct health facilities management experience. Our team's dual expertise — rigorous infrastructure asset management frameworks proven at scale, paired with operational understanding of how health facility maintenance is actually delivered — positions CBS Group to bridge the gap between the Department's policy framework and the operational reality of 77 health services.

4.1 Team Structure and Roles

4.2 Key Personnel Profiles

Jeff Dusting — Project Director and Strategic Lead

Jeff is the Director and COO of CBS Group and author of the CAPITAL Framework. He holds an MBA (Distinction), MSc (Test and Evaluation), BEng (Aerospace, Hons), and is a Certified Fellow of Asset Management (CFAM), Fellow of Engineers Australia (FIEAust), Chartered Engineer (CEng), and ISO 55001 certified. With over 25 years of strategic asset management experience, Jeff has led KPI framework design and implementation across Transport for NSW's tunnel portfolio, delivering validated savings exceeding $1 billion. Jeff provides strategic oversight, quality assurance, and escalation management for this engagement, ensuring the CAPITAL Framework methodology is applied effectively to the health infrastructure context.

Jim Ellwood — Technical Services Lead

Jim is a senior consulting engineer with over 25 years' experience in the analysis and management of complex,

## CBS_VicDH_Panel_ReEvaluation.md

*File: `CBS_VicDH_Panel_ReEvaluation.md`*

# Virtual Evaluation Panel — Briefing, Re-Evaluation and Remediation

## Part 1: Panel Briefing — What the Previous Assessment Missed

### 1.1 Evaluation Criteria Structure Discovery

The RFP evaluation criteria table (Section 2, Table 3) reveals critical information the panel did not previously consider.

**Undisclosed weightings.** The evaluation criteria table has a "Weighting" column, but all weighting cells are blank. The RFP evaluation guidance notes: "Weightings are optional and a decision concerning the use of these must be made by the Engagement Manager. If weightings are used, they must be disclosed." Since the weightings are not disclosed, one of two conclusions follows: either weightings exist but are deliberately undisclosed (unusual for Victorian Government procurement), or the Department has not assigned specific weightings and will apply equal weighting or evaluator judgement. For our assessment, we model both an equal-weight scenario and a methodology-heavy scenario, given Criterion 3 is the only criterion with detailed sub-criteria spelled out in the evaluation table.

**Criterion 3 discrepancy — confirmed as two separate requirements.** The evaluation criteria table describes Criterion 3 as "Methodology and project delivery approach" with 11 detailed sub-criteria. However, the response template (Section 3, Table 11) describes Criterion 3 as "Commitment to sector-wide collaboration, knowledge sharing, and capacity building." These are substantively different requirements. The most conservative reading is that both must be addressed. Our current response addresses both as separate sections (3.3 Collaboration and 3.4 Methodology).

**Only Criterion 3 has sub-criteria in the evaluation table.** This strongly suggests the methodology/collaboration criterion (however defined) is the most important criterion to evaluators. The 11 sub-criteria are: sound understanding of key issues and risks; identification of critical activities and outputs; sequencing of activities; effective communication of key activities and steps; collaboration and lateral thinking ensuring value-add to deliverables; culture of transparency and cooperation; robust project plan with milestones, timelines and resource allocation; clear understanding of objectives, issues and risks; transparent stakeholder communication; risk identification and mitigation strategies; and leveraging best-practice frameworks and innovative approaches.

### 1.2 DH Scoring Rubric

The previous assessment used a generic 1–5 scale. The DH specifies a **0–5 scale** with precise descriptors that the panel must now use.

| Score | Label | Key Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| **5** | Superior | Comprehensively documented with **all claims fully substantiated** |
| **4** | Excellent | Convincing and credible; overall claims are **supported** |
| **3** | Adequate | Credible but **not completely convincing** |
| **2** | Marginal | Has **minor omissions**; marginal capability |
| **1** | Poor | Unconvincing; significant flaws |
| **0** | Unacceptable | Did not provide requested information |

The critical insight: moving from 4 (Excellent) to 5 (Superior) requires every claim to be fully substantiated with evidence — named projects, quantified outcomes, verifiable credentials. This is an extremely high bar.

### 1.3 PAS Procurement Context

The Department has issued this RFP to fewer than 5 PAS panel members. Under the Victorian PAS SPC framework, the number of quotes depends on agency procurement policy, with guidance to seek broader quotations for complex or high-value services. The Administrative Guidelines on Engaging Professional Services require Secretary-level approval for engagements exceeding $150,000 (incl GST).

Selecting fewer than 5 suppliers strongly suggests the estimated engagement value is in the **$150,000–$500,000 (incl GST)** range. Our fee must sit credibly within a **$250,000–$350,000 (incl GST)** envelope to be competitive without appearing under-resourced.

---

## Part 2: Re-Evaluation Using DH 0–5 Scoring Rubric

**Panel composition:** (A) DH Asset Planning Unit manager, (B) DH procurement officer, (C) external health FM operations expert, (D) Victorian AMAF/DTF governance specialist, (E) independent asset management professional.

### Criterion 1: Experience (Health/Public Sector AM, KPI Implementation, Stakeholder Engagement)

| Panellist | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Strong methodology evidence via CAPITAL; health FM experience adds credibility but no health-sector outcome data |
| B | 4 | Convincing team credentials; PAS panel member; good case study |
| C | 3 | Transport case study well articulated but limited direct health evidence; health FM experience described by roles held not outcomes delivered |
| D | 4 | ISO 55001 alignment strong; AMAF understanding demonstrated; Victorian presence confirmed |
| E | 4 | CAPITAL framework genuinely impressive; team credentials well documented |

**Average: 3.8** | Weaknesses: No quantified health FM outcomes (W1); no health-sector mini case study (W2)

### Criterion 2: Tailored Solutions for Diverse Health Service Cohorts

|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Maturity segmentation is exactly what is needed; CMMS approach practical |
| B | 4 | Worked example is compelling; tailored approach well described |
| C | 4 | KPI health adaptations demonstrate genuine understanding |
| D | 4 | Maturity model alignment strong; scalability well addressed |
| E | 4 | Comprehensive; worked example differentiates |

**Average: 4.0** | Weakness: "Actionable insights" not explicitly mapped to DH decision-making chain (W3)

### Criterion 3 (Response Template): Collaboration, Knowledge Sharing, Capacity Building

| Panellist | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Capacity building targets are exactly what evaluators want to see |
| B | 4 | Comprehensive collaboration plan; knowledge transfer documented |
| C | 4 | Practical non-pilot engagement approach; KPI Champions model proven |
| D | 3 | "Open-book" claim needs specificity on what exactly transfers and in what format |
| E | 4 | Measurable outcomes rare in proposals — strong differentiation |

**Average: 3.8** | Weaknesses: Open-book not substantiated (W4); non-pilot uptake mechanism unclear (W5)

### Criterion 3 (Evaluation Table): Methodology and Project Delivery Approach

| Panellist | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Comprehensive methodology; deliverables register excellent |
| B | 4 | Well-structured; risks and mitigations specific to this engagement |
| C | 4 | Phase descriptions practical; Australian Standards add credibility |
| D | 4 | ISO 55001/44001 mapping strong; VHBA reference appropriate |
| E | 5 | Best-practice approach; CAPITAL framework genuinely differentiated; RCM adds rigour |

**Average: 4.2** | Weaknesses: No critical path identified (W6); sub-criteria not explicitly cross-referenced (W7)

### Criterion 4: Compliance with Government Guidelines and Standards

| Panellist | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Comprehensive compliance response |
| B | 3 | ISSRA committed but not completed with submission |
| C | 4 | Standards references appropriate for health context |
| D | 4 | AMAF and VPDSS well-addressed |
| E | 4 | Thorough |

**Average: 3.8** | Weakness: ISSRA not completed (W8 — limited remediation possible)

### Criterion 5: Change Management and Communication Planning

| Panellist | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | Distributed change approach credible; KPI Champions well-articulated |
| B | 3 | Missing specific communication plan/timeline |
| C | 3 | Good philosophy but limited evidence of dealing with resistant organisations |
| D | 4 | Sustainability mechanisms well-designed |
| E | 4 | Change approach integrated into delivery — more credible than standalone |

**Average: 3.6** | Weaknesses: No sample communication timeline (W9); no change-specific risks (W10)

---

## Part 3: Score Summary

### Equal Weighting (5 criteria at 20% each; C3 = average of collaboration and methodology)

| Criterion | Score |
|---|---|
| C1: Experience | 3.8 |
| C2: Tailored Solutions | 4.0 |
| C3: Combined (Collaboration 3.8 + Methodology 4.2) | 4.0 |
| C4: Compliance | 3.8 |
| C5: Change Management | 3.6 |
| **Weighted Total** | **3.84** |

### Methodology-Heavy (C3 at 30%, others at 17.5%)

| Criterion | Score | Weight | Weighted |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 3.8 | 17.5% | 0.665 |
| C2 | 4.0 | 17.5% | 0.700 |
| C3 | 4.0 | 30.0% | 1.200 |
| C4 | 3.8 | 17.5% | 0.665 |
| C5 | 3.6 | 17.5% | 0.630 |
| **Total** | | | **3.86** |

**Assessment:** CBS scores solidly in the Excellent (4) band across most criteria, with C5 (Change Management at 3.6) the weakest area. Addressing W9 and W10 should push C5 to 3.8+. Overall 3.84–3.86 is competitive but to win against a health-sector specialist, C1 needs strengthening (W1, W2).

---

## Part 4: Weakness Register

| # | Weakness | Criterion | Remediation | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | No quantified health FM outcomes from Heath/Caranguian | C1 | Add 1–2 quantified outcomes from health FM roles | High |
| W2 | No health-sector mini case study | C1 | Add brief secondary example from Caranguian/Heath experience | High |
| W3 | "Actionable insights" not mapped to DH decisions | C2 | Add insight → decision → action paragraph | Medium |

| W8 | ISSRA not completed | C4 | Strengthen commitment language; offer to complete within 5 days of request | Low (limited remediation) |
| W9 | No sample communication plan/timeline | C5 | Add communication timeline paragraph | High |
| W10 | No change management risks identified | C5 | Add 2–3 change-specific risks with mitigations | High |

---

## Part 5: Resourcing and Fee Issues

### 5.1 Over-Resourcing Risk

Current plan: ~203 person-days → estimated fee $500k–$600k incl GST. This exceeds the likely procurement band ($150k–$500k) for an RFP issued to <5 suppliers. 

**Recommended adjustment:** Reduce to ~140 person-days → fee ~$310k excl GST → ~$350k incl GST (including regional travel). This positions CBS as credible without being non-competitive.

### 5.2 Phase Overlap Allocation

The weekly plan cannot be cleanly split into phase-based pricing because phases overlap. The RFP pricing table needs clear day counts per person per phase. These need reconciliation.

### 5.3 David Harper Title

Listed as "Senior Consultant" but is CBS Group's Managing Director. Should be corrected to Director-level PAS position title.
