## C14608 - Attachment 1 - Information Security Standards Requirements Assessments.docx

*File: `Client Docs/C14608 - Attachment 1 - Information Security Standards Requirements Assessments.docx`*

Contents

Introduction	3

What is the ISSRA?	3

Respondent details	4

ISSRA Questionnaire	5

Additional resources	44

Document version	44

Introduction

The Victorian Department of Health and Department of Families, Fairness and Housing, from here on referred to as the departments, engage third parties for the delivery of ICT services as well as other information / data-related services.  

The departments maintain confidential and sensitive information and are accountable for the safekeeping of all public sector information that is generated/created on the department’s behalf by third parties. As such it is imperative that the security of departmental records and information is maintained to preserve the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of that information when it is accessed, modified, or stored by third parties.

What is the ISSRA?

The Information Security Standards Requirements Assessment (ISSRA) is a tool that enables the department to evaluate third parties against the security controls outlined within the department’s Standard 8b - Third-Party Information Security Standard for Externals (the Standard).   

It provides the departments with preliminary evidence of how a third party can meet the requirements within the Standard and preserve the confidentiality, integrity, and accessibility of departmental information. All third parties that provide ICT services and other data/information services (including accessing, modifying, generating, recording, or storing public sector information) on behalf of the department must complete the ISSRA before they can be considered for selection or awarded a contract with the department.  

The department's staff member responsible for initiating the procurement will send a copy of the Third-Party Standard 8b for Externals and the Information Security Standards Security Review Assessment (this document) to all prospective third parties.

A third party that plans to offer ICT services or other data/information services to the departments is the intended recipient of the ISSRA Questionnaire. 

Respondent details

Please provide details about your organisation in the table below: 

ISSRA Questionnaire

Responses to the questions in the ISSRA will enable the department to assess your organisation’s information security capability as part of the department’s assurance process for third parties intending to deliver ICT and or information/data-related services to the department. The following questions are intended to provide a high-level overview of your organisation’s information security maturity across a range of information security domains.


How to complete this form: 

Each of the questions in the ISSRA must be answered to enable the department to conduct a thorough assessment. 

Based on your responses, you may be required to provide evidence for some questions. Please attach and send all supporting evidence with the completed ISSRA questionnaire. An ‘example response’ is given for Q3 of the questionnaire below, as a guide ONLY. 

‘Guidance’ column: This column provides guidance to help interpret the question and provide an adequate response.

‘Compliance’ column: Please click on the relevant checkbox to select a Yes/ No/ Partial/ N/A response. Refer to the information in the table below for the definition of each response option and the corresponding actions required based on the response selected:

Note: Please direct questions on how to complete the questionnaire to your departmental contact.

| 30 | Does your organisation implement and align with the Essential Eight Maturity Model by the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC)?

If yes, please provide an assessment report. | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented and aligned its security controls to the Essential Eight (E8) strategies, and whether your maturity level has been formally assessed or documented.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You have implemented the Essential Eight (E8) mitigation strategies:
Application control
Patch applications
Configure Microsoft Office macro settings
User application hardening
Restrict administrative privileges
Patch operating systems
Multi-factor authentication (MFA)
Regular backups
You have measured your controls against one of the four ACSC maturity levels (Maturity Level 0–3)
You maintain records or reports of your assessment, self-assessed, or independently reviewed. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A | Example Response:

"Yes – We have implemented all Essential Eight strategies and assess our environment against ACSC’s Maturity Model. We are currently at Maturity Level Two, and a copy of our latest internal assessment report is attached."

Required Evidence could be:

A copy of your Essential Eight assessment report
Internal documentation showing your current maturity level
Implementation plan if you're still progressing toward full alignment |

Organisational systems (e.g., email, file storage, internal portals).

You use secure methods such as:
Authenticator apps (e.g., Microsoft Authenticator, Google Authenticator) and or
Hardware tokens or SMS codes, etc. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A | [Provide response if you have selected “No” or “Partial” In the compliance column for question 30] |

Configured rules to quarantine or block suspicious content before it reaches the end user.
Spam filters to block suspicious or unsolicited emails
Attachment and link scanning tools to inspect incoming email content.
Web proxy services or DNS filtering to restrict access to harmful websites. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 47 | Does your organisation have any tools, technology, and security configurations to protect your organisation’s email systems, such as DMARC, DKIM, or SPF? | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented email authentication and domain protection controls, specifically SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, to protect your email domain from being misused and to reduce the risk of phishing and fraud.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation has:
SPF (Sender Policy Framework): Restricts which mail servers are authorised to send emails on behalf of your domain.
DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail): Cryptographically signs outgoing messages to verify integrity.
DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance): Provides instructions to recipients on how to handle failed SPF/DKIM messages and sends back reports. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 48 | Does your organisation have a network management policy that covers implementation, maintenance, and continuous monitoring of perimeter controls to protect their network from external attacks? | This question asks whether your organisation has a formal network management policy or process that defines how the network is protected, especially at the perimeter, through security controls like firewalls, intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS), segmentation, and continuous monitoring.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You maintain a Network Security Policy or Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) that includes:
Design and deployment of perimeter controls (e.g., firewalls, secure gateways).
Use of monitoring tools for suspicious traffic patterns and intrusion attempts.
Network segmentation to reduce lateral movement.
Ongoing maintenance of firewall rules, NAT, VPN tunnels, and security zones.
Logging and alerting for unusual behaviour at the network boundary/edge. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 49 | Has your organisation deployed and regularly (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, etc) maintain and monitor the network for any attacks or malicious activity using firewalls, intrusion detection or prevention systems (IDS or IPS)? | This question asks whether your organisation uses Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) or Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) to detect or block suspicious activity across your network. It also seeks confirmation that these tools are maintained and actively monitored, alongside firewalls or other boundary controls.

✅ What "Yes" means:

You have deployed one or more of the following:

Network firewalls configured with current rule sets.
IDS tools that detect and log anomalies or known threat signatures.
IPS tools that can automatically block or respond to threats.
Next Generation Firewalls. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 50 | Does your organisation conduct regular (annual) penetration testing of its network, systems, or websites by engaging an independent and accredited supplier? | This question asks whether your organisation conducts external penetration testing on a regular basis using independent, accredited security professionals to assess the security of networks, applications, or web environments.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You conduct penetration testing:
At least annually or after major system changes.
Across relevant areas - network, web applications, APIs, cloud infrastructure, etc. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| Information Security Awareness & Training | Information Security Awareness & Training | Information Security Awareness & Training | Information Security Awareness & Training | Information Security Awareness & Training |

| 51 | Does your organisation provide cybersecurity awareness training to staff? 

Please provide a summary of the topic areas covered | This question asks whether your organisation provides cybersecurity awareness training to all staff, enabling them to understand how to identify threats, protect data, and follow secure practices in their day-to-day work.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You have a structured cyber awareness training program in place.
Training is delivered:
During onboarding and then regularly (e.g., annually, biannually, etc).
To all staff, including temporary or contract workers.
Training covers common security topics, such as, but not limited to:
Phishing and email scams.
Password management.
Safe internet and remote work practices.
Physical and mobile device security.
Reporting incidents or suspicious activity. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 52 | Does your organisation ensure staff understand their security obligations regarding the secure handling of information? | This question asks whether your organisation actively ensures that staff are aware of and comply with their responsibilities to handle information securely, including how it is stored, accessed, shared, and disposed of, in line with organisational or contractual requirements.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your staff are formally made aware of their security obligations, such as:
Onboarding training.
Signed Acceptable Use Agreements or Security Awareness Policies.
Ongoing refresher sessions or compliance attestations.
Proper use of IT systems and storage.
Use of encryption and secure information / data sharing channels. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 53 | If requested, does your organisation agree that your staff would undertake the required departmental information security training? | This question asks whether your organisation is willing to ensure that relevant staff (especially those with access to departmental data or systems) will complete mandatory security training provided by the department when requested.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation is prepared to:
Nominate appropriate staff to complete security awareness or system-specific training issued by the department.
Make this training a pre-requisite for access to departmental systems or information.
Track and report completion or compliance when requested. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| Physical Access | Physical Access | Physical Access | Physical Access | Physical Access |

| 54 | Does your organisation have physical security measures that restrict access to office areas, systems, and data that is stored on site? | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented physical access controls to prevent unauthorised individuals from accessing office spaces, hardware, or data stored on-site. It applies to both permanent and temporary locations where sensitive or departmental information might reside.
✅ What "Yes" means:
At a minimum, you would have the following physical security controls:
Access card systems, biometric readers, or secure entry points.
Visitor check-in procedures.
Secured server rooms or locked file storage areas.
CCTV monitoring and/or alarm systems. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 55 | Does your organisation keep records in a document that states who has access to the offices or data storage locations? | This question asks whether your organisation maintains logs or records of personnel access to physical locations where sensitive systems, equipment, or data are stored, such as office buildings, server rooms, or secure cabinets.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You maintain up-to-date access control logs for:
Permanent staff with building or room access.
Visitors and contractors (e.g., sign-in/sign-out registers). | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| End of Contract | End of Contract | End of Contract | End of Contract | End of Contract |

| 56 | Do you agree to permanently remove departmental information/data from any equipment used to store department information/data at the end of the contract and/or upon request from the department? | This question asks whether your organisation commits to securely deleting all departmental data from any systems, devices, servers, or storage media it controls when the contract ends or when directed by the department.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:
Has a defined data disposal and or sanitisation process.
Ensures all data (including backups and cached versions) is permanently deleted from:
Servers
Cloud storage
Laptops/desktops
Mobile or removable media.
Is prepared to verify and confirm deletion upon request. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 57 | Do you agree to ensure that your staff’s access to departmental systems and data will be removed at the end of the contract and/or upon request from the department? | This question asks whether your organisation has a formal commitment and process in place to promptly remove access for staff (including contractors) who no longer need to access departmental systems or information, especially at contract termination or upon explicit request.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You maintain an offboarding and deprovisioning process for:
Internal staff working / accessing the department’s systems or data.
Contractors/subcontractors working or accessing the  department's systems or data.
Access removal:
Happens immediately or within required timelines (as per contract).
Covers all types of access, including systems, email, cloud platforms, VPNs, etc. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 58 | Do you agree to ensure all departmental access tokens and entry tags are returned at the end of the contract or upon request from the department? | This question asks whether your organisation has a clear process for recovering and returning all department-issued access tools, such as building swipe cards, smartcards, security keys, or hardware tokens, upon termination of access or when specifically requested by the department.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You maintain a formal offboarding checklist or return policy for:
Entry/swipe cards.
Security tokens (e.g., RSA SecurID, etc).
Department-issued access badges or hardware.
Items are:
Tracked and collected during offboarding.
Returned securely to the Department.
Documented as part of contract closure. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| Offshore Access and Location – Transborder | Offshore Access and Location – Transborder | Offshore Access and Location – Transborder | Offshore Access and Location – Transborder | Offshore Access and Location – Transborder |

| 59 | Will the departmental information/data/systems be stored in Victoria, Australia?

If not, please provide more information about the storage location.
If data is to be stored, viewed, processed, or transmitted outside of Australian borders, what controls are in place to meet the departments data protection requirements stipulated as per the protective marking? | This question asks whether your organisation will ensure that the department’s data remains within Victoria (AU), and if not, whether robust transborder data protection measures are in place. 
✅ What "Yes (stored in Victoria/Australia)" means:
All systems, databases, or storage platforms (including backups) that hold Departmental data are:
Hosted within Australian borders, preferably in Victoria.
Managed by providers with Australian-based data centres.
Covered by Australian privacy and data security regulations. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 60 | Does your organisation plan to store/host departmental information/data/systems offshore?
If yes, a written confirmation from the department is required. | This question asks whether your organisation intends to host or store the department’s data outside Australia, including any cloud environments. If that is the case, written approval from the department is mandatory before such arrangements can be accepted.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You are planning to:
Store, process, or back up departmental information in data centres located overseas.
Use international SaaS, PaaS, or cloud providers with non-Australian data residency.
You must:
Disclose full details of the location(s) and providers.
Request and receive explicit written approval from the department before proceeding.
Comply with any requirements that the department stipulates if approval is provided. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| Information Security Incidents | Information Security Incidents | Information Security Incidents | Information Security Incidents | Information Security Incidents |

| 61 | Does your organisation have a defined process for managing information security incidents, including incident response, business continuity, and disaster recovery plans? | This question ensures you have an incident management  plan and can respond to incidents  and whether your the incident management, business continuity and disaster recovery plans are clearly documented and enable your organisation to:
Identify and respond to information security incidents
Maintain critical business operations during disruptions
Recover systems and data following a major outage or breach
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation has:
A Security Incident Response Plan (SIRP).
A Business Continuity Plan (BCP).
A Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP).
All staff are trained to respond according to these plans.
You test your plans on a regular basis e.g. annually. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 62 | Is your organisation aware that access to departmental systems may be removed while a security incident is being responded to, with access reinstated upon satisfactory containment of the incident being demonstrated to the department? | This question checks whether your organisation understands and accepts that, in such events, the department may temporarily suspend your access to its systems or data. Access will only be restored after the department is satisfied that the incident is contained, investigated, and resolved. This is covered in your incident response or escalation process.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:
Acknowledges that access suspension is a departmental right during an information security incident.
Will cooperate during such periods.
Understands that access will only resume upon:
Containment of the threat.
Approval by the department. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 63 | Upon request from the department, does your organisation agree to participate in an annual incident exercise with the departments that may be undertaken to test the security incident management processes and identify any gaps? | This question asks if your organisation agrees to actively participate in department-led cybersecurity incident response exercises, which are typically conducted once per year. These exercises may simulate data breaches, ransomware attacks, or service disruptions, helping all stakeholders identify weaknesses and improve coordination.
✅ What "Yes" means:
You are willing to:
Engage in department-initiated tabletop or live-fire cyber exercises, etc. 
Involve relevant internal staff (e.g., security, IT, incident response leads). | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| Designated Roles and Responsibilities | Designated Roles and Responsibilities | Designated Roles and Responsibilities | Designated Roles and Responsibilities | Designated Roles and Responsibilities |

| 64 | Does your organisation agree to have a designated role to authorise organisational access to departmental systems/data? | This question asks whether your organisation has formally assigned a specific role or person responsible for reviewing and authorising which staff can be granted access to the department’s system/data.
This role is part of your internal access management or governance process.

Your organisation:
Has designated an individual (e.g., IT Manager, Security Officer, System Admin, etc) who:
Validates access requests
Ensures the access is justified and appropriate
Maintains an access approval record
Undertakes access reviews. | Yes

Responding to departmental security-related communications.
Coordinating submission of evidence, reports, or questionnaire responses. | Yes

Your organisation:

| 67 | Does your organisation agree to have a designated role or roles to be the key contact in relation to incident response and management? | This question asks whether your organisation has formally assigned a dedicated role or individual to manage and coordinate all information security incidents involving the department’s systems, data, or services. This ensures swift containment, investigation, and communication during an incident.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:

Ensures this contact is:
Available for rapid engagement in case of a department-ported or internally identified incident.
Familiar with your internal incident response plan.

  No

| 68 | Does your organisation have a formalised process to inform key department contacts about changes in key contact staff or designated roles, such as contract management, incident management, or information security in your organisation? | This question asks whether your organisation has a documented and repeatable process for promptly informing the Department when there are changes to key roles, including those responsible for like Information Security, Incident Response, Contract Management, and Access Authorisation.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:

  No

  N/A |  |

| Information Security Compliance Assessment | Information Security Compliance Assessment | Information Security Compliance Assessment | Information Security Compliance Assessment | Information Security Compliance Assessment |

| 69 | Does your organisation hold any security certifications that have been independently assessed?
E, g. ISO27001, SOC 2, GDPR, IRAP, Other
If yes, please provide a copy of or links to the relevant certificates and reports | This question asks whether your organisation has been formally audited or certified by a recognised external authority against well-known information security or data protection standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001, SOC 2, IRAP etc). Certifications demonstrate maturity, accountability, and alignment with best practices, especially when handling departmental or sensitive information.
You can supply certificates, audit summaries, or links for verification.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation holds current and valid certifications that have been independently verified by an accredited body (e.g., BSI, PwC, SAI Global).
Examples may include:
ISO/IEC 27001 – Information Security Management System.
SOC 2 Type II – Service Organisation Controls for security, availability, processing integrity.
IRAP assessment – Australian Government standard. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 70 | Has your organisation recorded a significant security breach in the last two years or has any significant incident resolution in progress?
If yes, please provide the date of the incident, incident summary, and status (in progress, resolved, closed) | This question asks whether your organisation has experienced any material security incidents, such as data breaches, ransomware attacks, service disruptions, or unauthorised access that may have impacted customer data, system integrity, or service availability. It also asks if any such incidents are currently being managed or investigated.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation has experienced a significant security event within the past 24 months and is willing to:
Provide a brief incident summary (e.g., type of incident, affected systems/data, impact).
Disclose the incident date and current resolution status (e.g., resolved, under investigation).
Share evidence that appropriate mitigation and containment actions were taken. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 71 | Does your organisation offer federated identity management for single sign-on, and willing to implement the functionality to enable easy access to multiple departmental systems using a single set of credentials and centralised management of user identities? | This question asks whether your organisation supports or is willing to implement federated identity management (FIM), a security approach that enables Single Sign-On (SSO) using a centralised identity provider (e.g., Azure AD, Okta, etc). This allows users to securely access multiple department systems using a single set of credentials while maintaining access control, auditability, and streamlined onboarding/offboarding.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:
Has or is willing to implement federated identity protocols (e.g., SAML, OAuth, OpenID Connect).
Can integrate with the Department’s identity management systems.
Understands the security and usability benefits of SSO and centralised authentication.
Will manage user provisioning/de-provisioning in alignment with agreed access control policies. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 72 | Has your organisation provisioned a Security Operations Centre (internally or externally managed)?
Please provide evidence that outlines coverage and support levels and association with the incident management plan. | This question asks whether your organisation has implemented a Security Operations Centre (SOC), either in-house or outsourced (for e.g., MSSP (Managed Security Service Provider) or 3rd party), to continuously monitor, detect, and respond to cyber threats across systems and networks. The SOC should support incident response activities, align with your incident management plan, and provide timely escalation and communication with the department if required.
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation has an operational SOC that:
Provides real-time or near real-time threat detection and alerting.
Monitors logs, endpoints, network traffic, and system behaviour.
It is governed by a Service Level Agreement (SLA) detailing:
Coverage hours (e.g., 24x7, business hours).
Response times and escalation paths.
Integration with incident response and containment plans. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 73 | Does your organisation have a formal data encryption process to secure departmental information at rest (online and offline storage) and in transit (during transmission)?
If yes, please provide a copy of the Encryption Policy or process or an overview of the policy | This question asks whether your organisation has a formalised, consistently applied encryption process or policy to protect departmental information for data at rest (e.g., on hard drives, databases, servers, backups, USBs etc) and in transit (e.g., over the internet, internal networks, APIs, email etc)
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation:
Is able to encrypts sensitive and departmental data at rest and in transit.
Uses industry-accepted encryption standards (e.g., TLS 1.2+, AES-256, SHA-2).
Has a documented Encryption Policy or process that:
Describes technical controls
Covers mobile, cloud, and on-premises environments
Is reviewed regularly and enforced across teams. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

| 74 | Does your organisation agree to provide departmental staff or a representative  access to audit logs and other evidence for the purpose of performing a forensic investigation? | This question asks whether your organisation agrees to cooperate with the department by granting access to relevant audit logs, system records, or security event data when needed for conducting a forensic investigation. 
✅ What "Yes" means:
Your organisation agrees to:
Provide relevant log files, audit trails, and system evidence upon the department's request.
Facilitate secure transfer or access for forensic analysis (e.g., by department staff or external forensic teams).
Retain logs in a manner that allows retrieval within agreed-upon retention windows. | Yes
  No
  Partial
  N/A |  |

                                                                          OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                                                      2


Document Version ...................................................................................................................................... 19




                                                                     OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                               3




Purpose
The Victorian Department of Health and Department of Families, Fairness and Housing, from here on referred
to as the departments, engage third parties for the delivery of ICT services as well as Client/Community
services to the organisation.
This standard has been written to outline the expectations the departments have of all third parties that are
engaged to work with the departments’ IT, information, and data. Applying the controls set out in this standard
will provide assurance to the departments that third parties understand the importance of securing systems
and information and that they will work with the department to ensure the confidentiality, integrity and
availability of departmental information.

Authority
This standard derives its’ authority from the Information Security Policy which has been approved by the
departments’ executive committees. The departments’ Information Security Policy requires that the following
are adhered to:
           We will ensure that third parties securely collect, hold, manage, use, disclose or transfer public
           sector information.
           Sharing of the department’s information with external parties will be based on an understanding of
           the recipient’s capability to adequately protect the information.
With some contracts / agreements, specified controls may have already been included. The following controls
should be applied in conjunction with any information security requirements described in the contract /
agreement, and where there are similar controls, those that are stipulated in the contract / agreement will take
precedence over the controls in this document. If there are no controls stipulated in the contract / agreement,
then the controls described in the standard shall apply.

Scope
This standard applies to all third parties, and their staff, that have been engaged by the departments to:
•     work with departmental information / data
•     collect information / data on the departments’ behalf
•     provide or develop systems / applications, either on-premise or cloud-based, IT Services, to the
      departments


Roles and Responsibilities
    Role                                 Responsibility
    CeSO                                 •    Oversight of all required actions to give effect to the Standard
    CISO                                 •    Accountability for the management, articulation, support, and
                                              promotion of this Standard
    Cyber Security Unit                  •    Delegated responsibility for the implementation, maintenance
                                              and review of the Standard.
                                         •    Responsible for undertaking information security assessment
                                              activities.




                                                     OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                            4




 Department Business Areas             •   Understand and follow the process requirements when
                                           engaging third parties.
                                       •   Engage with Cyber Security Unit and direct their selected third
                                           party to this Standard.
 Third Parties                         •   Comply with this standard.
                                       •   Advise the business area where there could be an issue with
                                           compliance to this standard.
 Procurement                           •   Understand and provide advice to business areas in relation to
                                           the processes required for information security compliance
                                           when engaging a third party.




Exemption
Departmental contacts will be required to seek exemptions to Mandated controls on behalf of a third-party
from the Cyber Security Unit, info.security@health.vic.gov.au


References
General references for the controls can be found in the Related Documents and References section at the
end of this document. References to specific resources relevant to a control are cited with the control and
may be subject to change over time.


Reading the standard – layout
This standard is set out under specific topic areas that address the department’s information security
requirements.
The specific requirement for each topic area is provided with an associated rationale for the requirement.
The controls that address the requirement are listed in the controls section of the table along with the
applicability of the control and any specific references.
Control deemed “Mandatory” MUST be implemented as they are regarded as essential elements of a
minimum set of controls. “Highly Recommended” controls will be useful and WILL be implemented if they are
practically achievable.
Requests for exemption from implementation of a Mandated control need to follow the documented
Exemption Process.




                                                  OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals              5




                                     OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                              6




Controls
1.0 - Assurance to the department
 Requirement      Third parties are required to participate in information security assessment activities.

 Rationale        Participating in information security assessments provides assurance to the department that the
                  third party has the capability to meet the department’s data protection requirements and
                  reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting compromise.

 Controls                                                                                        Applicability

 1.1 Undertake information security assessment activities as required by the departments.        Mandatory




2.0 Governance
 Requirement      Third parties are required to have information security governance within their
                  organisation.

 Rationale        Implementing information governance practises and tools enables an organisation to address
                  and manage information security risks using a structured and cohesive approach.



 Controls                                                                                        Applicability
 2.1 Third parties should as part of their information security governance approach, at a        Mandatory
     minimum, have in place the following:
      2.1.2 Information Security Risk management. To manage risks in relation to changes in
         their ICT environment, changes to their operating environments, legislative
         requirements, staff and third parties, and the cyber security threat landscape.
      2.1.3 Policies and standards, and supporting process, that address information security
         within their organisation.
      2.1.4 Defined information security roles and responsibilities.
      2.1.5 A process that provides oversight of the cyber security program within their
         organisation.




3.0 - Identification and verification
 Requirement      Any users with access to department systems shall be vetted before they are provided
                  access to departmental information and systems.

 Rationale        Ensuring a person are who they say they are, reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting
                  compromise or misuse and/or management of departmental information.

 Controls                                                                                        Applicability




                                                  OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                7




 3.1 Ensure that the identity, and any stated credentials, of your staff working with             Mandatory
     departmental systems / information / data have been verified by your organisation
     before your staff are granted access to departmental systems / information / data.
 3.2 Undertake vetting activities i.e., police checks, baseline clearance as applicable, of all   Mandatory
     staff working with departmental systems / information / data prior to your staff accessing
     departmental systems / information / data.



4.0 - Safety screening for funded organisations
 Requirement      Funded organisation staff with access to department systems shall have a safety
                  screening completed before they are provided access to departmental information and
                  systems.

 Rationale        A safety screening reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting compromise or misuse and/or
                  mismanagement of departmental information.

 Controls                                                                                         Applicability

 4.1 Ensure that staff complete all the safety screening tasks set out in your organisation       Mandatory for all
     Safety Screening Policy.                                                                     organisations
                                                                                                  that come under
                                                                                                  the SAMS
 Reference: Safety Screening policy: https://providers.dffh.vic.gov.au/safety-screening-policy    agreement.

 4.2 Once the safety screening has been completed, the nominated Organisational Authority         Highly
     must confirm to the department that the user has a legitimate requirement to access the      recommended
     departments system for work purposes.



5.0 - Access management
 Requirement      Access management processes should be in place to ensure that access to departmental
                  information and systems is only available to authorised personnel.

 Rationale        Unauthorised access or disclosure of departmental information has the capacity to cause grave harm
                  to the department’s reputation, core functions, third parties and stakeholders.
                  Having access management processes that are based on genuine business requirements for access,
                  and formal approval processes to access sensitive information, ensures the security of sensitive
                  information and the integrity and availability of third party and departmental systems.

 Controls                                                                                         Applicability

   5.1 Manage their staff and contractor access to departmental information / systems based       Mandatory
       on the principles of least privilege and need to know.

   5.2 Stipulate to their staff and contractors to not share their login credentials to           Mandatory
       departmental systems with any other persons.

   5.3 Stipulate to their staff to not use other staff member’s credentials to log into           Mandatory
       departmental systems.




                                                   OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                              8




   5.4 Undertake regular access reviews of their staff and contractors who work with              Mandatory
       departmental systems / information.

   5.5 Have a documented, formal process to manage access to systems and environments.            Mandatory
       The process should include:
     5.5.1 Specified time frames for timely access and deprovisioning to systems / networks
         / data.
      5.5.2 An access review schedule to confirm that a user’s access is still required for the
          system / networks / data.

   5.6 Advise the department within the required timelines, set out in the contract, when their   Mandatory
       staff no longer require access to departmental systems / networks.

   5.7 If required deprovisioning timelines are not set out in the contract / agreement, then     Mandatory
       the following deprovisioning timelines applicable will be based on the protective
       marking of the departmental system / information / data. They are as follows:
    5.7.1 OFFICIAL – within 5 business days
     5.7.2 OFFICIAL: Sensitive – within 3 business days
     5.7.3 PROTECTED – within 1 business day.



   5.8 Ensure their staff understand that their use / access of departmental systems /            Highly
       networks / data is monitored, logged, and audited.                                         recommended



6.0 - Security Assessment of fourth party suppliers
 Requirement      Third parties engaged by the department must ensure that their own third parties are
                  able to comply with the departments’ information security requirements if they handle
                  departmental information / systems.

 Rationale        Managing third party engagements and obtaining assurance in relation to their information
                  security capability supports information security across the service delivery supply chain.
 Controls                                                                                         Applicability

 6.1 Declare, to the department, all sub-contracting arrangements, and its purpose, where it      Mandatory
     involves systems and data collection processing/sharing of departmental
     information/data.
 6.2 Have a due diligence process to assess any third parties for solvency and security prior     Mandatory
     to engagement. The program may include certification verification, self-assessment
     questionnaires, and risk assessment.

 6.3 Ensure to include obligations to comply with their own organisation’s security policies      Highly
     and standards, right to audit, sharing of and security testing in any contracts with their   Recommended
     third-party service providers.

 6.4 Third parties engaging another third party, when working with departmental information /     Mandatory
     data / systems, must assess the information security risk the third party may bring to the
     engagement.




                                                   OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                9




 6.5 Third parties should advice their own third parties of any departmental information             Mandatory
     security requirements that the departments have stipulated and that they would be
     required to implement for the engagement / project.



7.0 - Security Controls audit /assurance
 Requirement      A program of third-party assurance activities shall be implemented.

 Rationale        To confirm that third parties are applying, and continue to apply, required information security
                  requirements, the departments will validate third party compliance with the information security
                  requirements.
 Controls                                                                                            Applicability

 7.1 Engage with the department or their nominated auditor during a security controls audits         Mandatory
     when requested by the departments.




8.0 - Information / data management
 Requirement      Victorian government protective markings will inform the handling and management of
                  all departmental information / data.

 Rationale        Protective markings indicate the sensitivity and confidentiality of the information / data and are
                  the basis of the information security requirements that third parties are required to apply to their
                  practises / application / systems when working with departmental information / data.
 Controls                                                                                            Applicability

 8.1 Understand the protective markings the department uses.                                         Highly
                                                                                                     Recommended
 Reference: Practitioner guide protective markings, Office of the Victorian Information
 Commissioner (OVIC): https://ovic.vic.gov.au/information-security/practitioner-guide-
 protective-markings/?highlight=Protective+markings

 8.2 The protective marking that is assigned by the department to the system / information /         Mandatory
     data will inform the security provisions that are put in place to ensure the confidentiality,
     integrity, and availability of the system / information / data.

 8.3 Third parties should adopt any guidance from the department in protecting and securing          Mandatory
     departmental systems / information / data.

 8.4 Third parties and their suppliers, are not to upload any departmental (public sector)           Mandatory
     information into any Artificial Intelligence (AI) solutions without prior consent from the
     department.

 8.5 Departmental (public sector) information must be returned to the department upon                Mandatory
     termination or completion of the agreement.

 8.6 Where information / data cannot be returned to the department, third parties should             Mandatory
     permanently de-identify or delete the information / data and send a confirmation to the
     department once it has been done.




                                                    OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                          10




9.0 - Information sharing
 Requirement      Security controls shall be implemented for information sharing

 Rationale        Implementing security controls into information sharing processes ensures the departments’
                  data protection obligations are met and reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting
                  compromise.
 Controls                                                                                       Applicability
 9.1 Secure methods should be used when sharing / transferring data / information with the      Mandatory
     department.

 9.2 Departmental data cannot be shared with other third parties (and or third parties to the   Mandatory
     service provider) unless:
    9.2.1 A provision has already been written into the contract / agreement to do so.
    9.2.2 Written permission is obtained from the department.

 9.3 Information sharing requirements and restrictions should be written into contracts /       Mandatory
     agreements.



10.0 – Reviews of information security requirements of contracts
 Requirement      Security requirements of contracts shall be subject to initial and ongoing reviews

 Rationale        Review of contractual security requirements enables continuous improvement and the
                  implementation of lessons learnt, reducing the likelihood of unwitting compromise.
 Controls                                                                                       Applicability

 10.1 Third parties will be required to participate in a security assessment at the following   Mandatory
     stages of a contract / agreement:
    10.1.1 Prior to engagement.
    10.1.2 At time points during the contract / agreement period where security assessments
    / audits are required.
    10.1.3 When there is significant change to the third-party organisation and / or
    deliverable requirements.



11.0 – Technical baseline security controls
 Requirement      Have minimal technical baseline security controls in place to protect systems, networks,
                  and information.

 Rationale        Implementing technical baseline security controls within an organisation’s ICT environment
                  uplifts information and cyber security defences.
 Controls                                                                                       Applicability

 11.1 Third parties are required to have, at a minimum and dependent on the protective          Mandatory
      marking of the data / information or system, the technical baseline security control
      specified in Appendix 1, in place.




                                                  OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                              11




12.0 – Information security awareness & training
 Requirement      Third party personnel accessing departmental information and environments shall
                  undertake information security awareness training

 Rationale        Information security awareness training facilitates personnel’s understanding of information
                  security concepts, requirements and risks, and provides the basis for understanding what their
                  information security obligations are to protect their organisation’s information security as well as
                  the departments’.
 Controls                                                                                           Applicability
 12.1 Third parties should provide information security awareness training to their staff on a      Highly
      regular basis.                                                                                recommended

 12.2 Third parties should ensure their staff understand their security obligations in regard to    Highly
      secure handling of information.                                                               recommended

 12.3 Third party staff must undertake departmental information security awareness training         Highly
      where required as part of the contract / agreement.                                           Recommended




13.0 - Physical access
 Requirement      Any facility containing hardware and software that processes or stores departmental
                  information shall implement appropriate physical security controls

 Rationale        Implementing physical security requirements is essential for securing systems, networks and
                  information, and reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting compromise.

 Controls                                                                                           Applicability

 13.1 Physical security measures should control access to premises and non-public areas,            Mandatory
      and a logbook or electronic audit trail should be maintained.

 13.2 Third parties have guidelines, for their staff, to ensure the physical security of their      Highly
     devices and workspace when working offsite including working from home.                        Recommended



14.0 - End of contract
 Requirement      A process to manage the end of contract / agreement must be in place to ensure that the
                  departments systems / networks and information / data remain secure.

 Rationale        It is expected that departmental information is protected throughout the lifecycle of the contract
                  including at the end of contract / agreement / engagement. End-of-life provisions for information
                  / data that are written into contracts / agreements will be applied by third parties to reduce the
                  likelihood of witting or unwitting compromise.

 Controls                                                                                           Applicability

 14.1 Ensure that at the end of the contract / agreement with the department the following is       Mandatory
      done:



                                                    OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                           12




      14.1.1 All departmental information / data is returned to the department and any copies
        are deleted by the third-party provider.
      14.1.2 Equipment used to store department information / data undergoes a cleansing
        process permanently remove departmental information / data.
      14.1.3 Third party’s staff access is removed (where applicable) from departmental
        systems / networks.
      14.1.4 All departmental access tokens and entry tags are returned.



15.0 - Offshore access and location – Transborder
 Requirement      Hosting of information shall be located according to the departments’ security
                  requirements.

 Rationale        Awareness and management of legal domains assists in the detection and mitigation of a
                  security incident and can reduces the likelihood of witting or unwitting compromise.
 Controls                                                                                        Applicability

 15.1 Provide the department with information whether departmental information / data /          Mandatory
     systems will be hosted in Australia or outside Australia/offshore.

 15.2 Where it is proposed to store / host departmental information / data / systems offshore,   Mandatory
     a threat and risk assessment will need to be undertaken by the department prior to any
     information / data / system being located offshore and confirmed in a written
     agreement.



16.0 - Information Security Incidents
 Requirement      Planning for security incidents shall be undertaken and when a security incident occurs
                  third parties will have provisions in place to contain, investigate, report, and manage the
                  incident.

 Rationale        Having provisions in place when a security incident occurs will mitigate the impact and support
                  a better recovery from the incident.

 Controls                                                                                        Applicability

 16.1 Advise the department of security / privacy incidents and data breaches within your        Mandatory
     organisations or your third parties (4th parties) as soon as it becomes apparent that
     departmental information / data has been impacted.

 16.2 Have an established incident response plan that covers security incidents.                 Mandatory

 16.3 Third party staff access to departmental systems may be removed whilst a security          Highly
     incident is being responded to, with access reinstated upon satisfactory containment of     recommended
     the incident demonstrated to the department.

 16.4 Annual incident exercise with the departments may be undertaken to test the security       Highly
     incident management processes and identify any gaps.                                        recommended




                                                  OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                          13




17.0 - Designated roles and responsibilities
 Requirement      Assigning and confirming roles and responsibilities is required for each engagement.

 Rationale        Having nominated points of contact for each relationship shall ensure that both the department
                  and the third party can better manage the engagement.
 Controls                                                                                       Applicability

 17.1 Designated roles and responsibilities should be assigned to enable communication          Mandatory
      between the third party and the department. These roles should include:
      17.1.1 Authorising organisational access to departmental systems / data.
      17.1.2 Key contacts for Information Security.
      17.1.3 Key contacts for contract management.
      17.1.4 Key contacts in relation to incident response and management.

   17.2 If a person or role changes, the key contact from the department should be notified.    Mandatory




                                                 OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                           14

                                     departmental information

 Next review date                    12 months from effective date

Related documents and references

                       collection, handling, access to and correction of personal data by the Victorian public
                       sector.
                    2. Privacy Act 1988 (federal) - This Act regulates 'information privacy' and the
                       protection of people's personal information.
                    3. Health Records Act 2001 (Victoria)

                       government/essential-cyber-security/essential-eight/essential-eight-explained

                       Information Systems and Organisations

                       https://www.cisecurity.org/controls

                       Office

                    9. Victorian Government Risk Management Framework (VGRMF)

Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                                                           16




Appendix
Appendix 1 - Baseline Technical Controls
The table below describes the technical control and its description. It is intended to provide third-parties with an overview of the expected security controls
that they should implement (if not already done so) to enable them to secure their networks, systems / applications and data / information. This in turn will
provide assurance to the department that the third party will have the minimum controls to keep departmental data / information secure.

 Control name                         Control description
 Multi-Factor Authentication          Multi-factor authentication is defined as ‘a method of authentication that uses two or more authentication
 (MFA)                                factors to authenticate a single claimant to a single authentication verifier’.
                                      MFA is a core component of a strong identity and access management (IAM) policy. Rather than just
                                      asking for a username and password, a person trying to access a system or network must provide
                                      additional identification verifiers.
                                      The authentication factors that make up a multi-factor authentication request must come from two or more
                                      of the following:
                                         1. something the claimant knows (e.g. a personal identification number (PIN), password or response
                                            to a challenge)
                                         2. something the claimant has (e.g. a physical token, smartcard, mobile apps such as authenticators
                                            or software certificate)
                                         3. something the claimant is (biometrics e.g. a fingerprint or iris scan).
 Application control                  Application control is a security approach designed to protect against malicious code (also known as
                                      malware) executing on systems. When implemented robustly, it ensures only approved applications (e.g.
                                      executables, software libraries, scripts, installers, compiled HTML, HTML applications, control panel
                                      applets and drivers) can be executed.
                                      While application control is primarily designed to prevent the execution and spread of malicious code, it
                                      can also prevent the installation or use of unapproved applications.
 Restrict administrative privileges   Users with administrative privileges for operating systems and applications can make significant changes
                                      to their configuration and operation, bypass critical security settings and access sensitive information.




                                                                           OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                                                     17


 Control name                        Control description
                                     Domain administrators have similar abilities for an entire network domain, which usually includes all of the
                                     workstations and servers on the network.
                                     Restricting administrative privileges makes it more difficult for malicious code to elevate its privileges,
                                     spread to other hosts, hide its existence, persist after reboot, obtain sensitive information or resist removal
                                     efforts.
                                     An environment where administrative privileges are restricted is more stable, predictable, and easier to
                                     administer and support, as fewer users can make significant changes to their operating environment,
                                     either intentionally or unintentionally.
 Patch operating systems and         Once a patch is released by a vendor, the patch should be applied in a timeframe commensurate with an
 applications                        organisation’s exposure to the security vulnerability and the level of cyber threat the organisation is aiming
                                     to protect themselves against.
                                     By maintaining a clear and streamlined patch management process – including an awareness of
                                     information sources used to determine whether security vulnerabilities are currently being exploited, an
                                     awareness of the regular patch release schedules of vendors, defined responsibilities for individuals
                                     involved in patching activities and regular vulnerability scanning for missing patches – organisations can
                                     position themselves to act swiftly upon security bulletin or patch releases. In doing so, organisations can
                                     dramatically reduce the time between noticing information on new security vulnerabilities and applying
                                     patches or implementing temporary workarounds where appropriate.
                                     The department requires that all workstations and devices that access or store departmental information
                                     are patched in a timely manner.
 Regular backups                     When performing backups, all important information, software and configuration settings for software,
                                     network devices and other ICT equipment should be captured daily. This will ensure that should a system
                                     fall victim to a ransomware attack, important information will not be lost and that business operations will
                                     have reduced downtime. To mitigate the likelihood of information becoming unavailable due to accidental
                                     or malicious deletion of backups, organisations should ensure that backups are protected from
                                     unauthorised modification, corruption and deletion. This can be achieved by storing backups offline,
                                     ideally at multiple geographically dispersed locations, or online but in a non-rewritable and non-erasable
                                     manner, such as through the use of write once, read many technologies. To ensure that backups can be
                                     restored when the need arises, and that any dependencies can be identified and managed, it is important
                                     that full restoration of backups has been tested at least once following the implementation of backup



                                                                           OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                                                      18


 Control name                        Control description
                                     technologies and processes. Furthermore, full restoration of backups should be tested each time
                                     fundamental information technology changes occur, such as when deploying new backup technologies. In
                                     the intervening time, it is important that regular testing in the form of partial restoration of backups is also
                                     undertaken.
 Inventory and Control of            Actively manage (inventory, track, and correct) all enterprise assets (end-user devices, including portable
 Enterprise Assets                   and mobile; network devices; non-computing/Internet of Things (IoT) devices; and servers) connected to
                                     the infrastructure physically, virtually, remotely, and those within cloud environments, to accurately know
                                     the totality of assets that need to be monitored and protected within the enterprise. This will also support
                                     identifying unauthorized and unmanaged assets to remove or remediate.
 Inventory and Control of            Actively manage (inventory, track, and correct) all software (operating systems and applications) on the
 Software Assets                     network so that only authorized software is installed and can execute, and that unauthorized and
                                     unmanaged software is found and prevented from installation or execution.
 Secure Configuration of             Establish and maintain the secure configuration of enterprise assets (end-user devices, including portable
 Enterprise Assets and Software      and mobile, network devices; non-computing/IoT devices; and servers) and software (operating systems
                                     and applications) with end point protection.
 Account Management                  Processes and tools to assign and manage authorization to credentials for user accounts, including
                                     administrator accounts, as well as service accounts, to enterprise assets and software is in place.
 Continuous Vulnerability            Develop a plan to monitor, assess and track vulnerabilities on all enterprise assets within the enterprise’s
 Management                          infrastructure, to remediate, and minimize, the window of opportunity for attackers continuously and
                                     proactively. Monitor public and private industry sources for new threat and vulnerability information.
 Audit Log Management                Collect, alert, review, and retain audit logs of events that could help detect, understand, or recover from an
                                     attack.
 Email and Web Browser               Improve protections and detections of threats from email and web vectors, as these are opportunities for
 Protections                         attackers to manipulate human behaviour through direct engagement.
 Malware Defences                    Prevent or control the installation, spread, and execution of malicious applications, code, or scripts on
                                     enterprise assets, including remote desktop protocols.
 Network Infrastructure              Establish, implement, and actively manage (track, report, correct) network devices, in order to prevent
 Management                          attackers from exploiting the devices as an entry point to the network.




                                                                            OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                                                 19


 Control name                        Control description
 Network Monitoring and              Operate processes and tooling to establish and maintain comprehensive network monitoring and defence
 Defence                             against security threats across the enterprise’s network infrastructure and user base.
 Penetration Tests                   Test the effectiveness and resiliency of enterprise assets through identifying and exploiting weaknesses in
                                     controls (people, processes, and technology), and simulating the objectives and actions of an attacker.
 Enterprise / Organisational         Ensure a mobile working policy is established that requires mobile devices (including BYOD) to be kept up
 Mobile device management            to date with vendor updates and app patches, and where BYOD is used to access department
                                     systems/information and data, there is appropriate security protection in place on the BYOD




                                                                          OFFICIAL
Third Party Standard for Externals                                                                                20
