Executive summary
The Maintenance Guidance for Victorian Health Facilities – Performance Monitoring has been
developed by the Department of Health (the department) to supplement the updated Maintenance
Guidance for Victorian Health Facilities published in 2024. This document is designed to assist
healthcare facilities in monitoring maintenance activities of engineering assets effectively.
Its primary goal is to establish consistent guidance for maintenance management and reporting
across Health Services. It promotes a systematic approach to monitoring, adaptable to
organisations of varying sizes and asset management maturity levels.
The guidance aims to enhance maintenance capabilities while aligning with the Asset Management
Accountability Framework (AMAF) requirements for consistency in asset management.
Maintenance is an ongoing activity that requires continuous monitoring and timely updates.
The introduction of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) marks a significant advancement in
strengthening asset management and maintenance practices across all Victorian Health Services.
By offering a structured, measurable, and scalable framework, these KPIs enable Health Services
to proactively assess and improve their maintenance operations.
This initiative fosters a culture of continuous improvement, encouraging all Health Services—
regardless of their current asset management maturity—to benchmark performance, identify gaps,
and implement targeted enhancements. Ultimately, the KPI framework promotes greater
consistency, transparency, and accountability in maintenance practices. It supports alignment with
AMAF and other relevant standards, ensuring that all facilities are better equipped to deliver safe,
reliable, and efficient healthcare environments.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                                     8




Glossary
 Term            Definition
 AM              Asset Management

 AMAF            Asset Management Accountability Framework


 CSMEP           Civil, Structural, Mechanical, Electrical, Public Health

 DH              Department of Health (Victoria State Government)

 DTF             Department of Treasury and Finance

 EES             Essential Engineering Services

 FCI             Facility Condition Index

 FM              Facilities Management

 FTFR            First Time Fix Rate

 GFA             Gross Floor Area - The total gross floor area of all storeys of a building, measured from the
                 outside of the external walls and the centre of any common walls. It excludes areas used for
                 building services, plant, access between levels, public lobbies, malls, parking, and
                 unenclosed balconies

 GIFA            Gross Internal Floor Area - The total gross internal floor area contained within the building
                 measured to the internal face of the external walls. It includes areas such as stairwells,
                 internal walls, and service areas, but excludes external walls.

 HS              Health Services

 KPI             Key Performance Indicator

 MTBF            Mean Time Between Failures

 MTTR            Mean Time to Repair

 NIA             Net Internal Area - The net internal usable area within a building measured to the internal
                 face of perimeter walls, excluding areas such as lobbies, stairwells, lifts, plant rooms, and
                 toilets. Often used for calculating lettable or functional space.

 NUA             Net Usable Area - A subset of NIA, nett useable areas typically excludes primary circulation
                 spaces such as main corridors. It represents the area directly usable for the intended
                 function (e.g., beds, desks, labs).

 O&M             Operation and Maintenance

 PMP             Planned Maintenance Percentage

 PPP/PFI         Public-Private Partnership / Private Finance Initiative

 RCA             Root cause analysis

 SLA             Service Level Agreement

 VHHSBA          Victorian Health and Human Services Building Authority




                                                   OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                               9




1 Introduction
1.1 Background
The Department of Health (the department) has been established to deliver a world-class health
system that leads to better health outcomes for Victorians. The department’s infrastructure plays a
pivotal role in enabling the provision of a wide range of vital services, including aged care, public
hospitals, Aboriginal and Indigenous services, illness prevention and recovery, mental health,
rehabilitation, and community facilities. It also assumes responsibility for monitoring and facilitating
the department and Health Services’ compliance with the Asset Management Accountability
Framework (AMAF). This document reflects the requirements of the AMAF introduced by the
Department of Treasury and Finance (DTF) in 2016.
Operating under a partnership service model, in accordance with the Public Health and Wellbeing
Act 2008, the department oversees the operational facilities managed by independent Health
Service providers. The department acknowledges the need for improvement in maintaining these
facilities and monitoring assets performance. To address this challenge, the Maintenance
guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance monitoring has been developed to serve
as non-prescriptive guideline and support for hospitals and Health Services in asset maintenance
monitoring. This document supplements the Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities
published in 2024.


1.2 Objectives
The objectives of this document are to define a clear and strategic foundation for maintenance
performance monitoring of engineering assets across Victorian health facilities. These objectives
align with the Department of Health’s asset management priorities and are intended to guide the
development and implementation of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that support safe, efficient,
and resilient healthcare environments.
The following objectives have been identified as critical to the success of maintenance and asset
management practices:
1. Reliability & Resilience
   Ensure high system uptime, eliminate or reduce single points of failure, and implement robust
   maintenance strategies that support continuity of care. This includes planning for resilience
   through robustness, redundancy, resourcefulness, response, and recovery mechanisms.
2. Compliance & Risk Management
   Maintain full compliance with regulatory statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations. This
   includes health and safety, environmental, and legal requirements, and supports risk mitigation
   through structured frameworks and emergency preparedness.
3. Quality and Performance
   Deliver high-quality maintenance services that support clinical operations and extend asset
   longevity. Performance should be continuously monitored and improved through data-driven
   insights and formal review mechanisms.
4. Cost Management
   Promote cost-effective maintenance practices that balance performance and risk. This includes
   lifecycle costing, budget adherence, and efficient, value-for-money strategies that ensure
   financial sustainability.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                        10



5. Asset & Data Lifecycle Management
    Embed a whole-of-life approach to asset management, ensuring that assets are planned,
    acquired, operated, maintained, and recycled / retired in a way that maximises value and
    performance. This includes the integration of accurate, complete, and standardised asset data
    to support forecasting, investment planning, and long-term service outcomes.
6. Service Delivery & Customer Service
   Ensure maintenance services are responsive, efficient, and aligned with stakeholder needs.
   Feedback mechanisms and service level agreements should be used to drive continuous
   improvement.
7. Communication
   Establish clear and structured communication channels across all levels of maintenance and
   asset management and other relevant stakeholders. This supports transparency, accountability,
   and timely decision-making.
8. Maintenance Personnel Performance
    Monitor and support the performance of maintenance personnel, including in-house teams and
    contractors. This includes role clarity, training, and alignment with asset management plans and
    KPIs.
9. Governance & Asset Management Integration
   Strengthen the integration of governance frameworks with asset management systems and
   processes. This includes aligning with AMAF requirements, standardising procedures, and
   ensuring that leadership, controls, and strategic oversight are embedded into operational
   delivery and performance monitoring.


1.3 How to use this document
This document is intended to support Health Services (HS) in monitoring and improving the
performance of their maintenance management practices. It assumes that most HS already have
maintenance strategies in place, whether based on statutory requirements, manufacturer
recommendations, minimum essential life safety maintenance, or risk-based approaches.
Rather than replacing existing practices, this document provides a structured framework to enhance
them. Its primary focus is on measuring and improving maintenance performance through the use of
Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).
Health Services can apply this document in the following ways:
•   Aligning with AMAF: Use the guidance to support compliance with the Asset Management
    Accountability Framework (AMAF), particularly in areas of performance monitoring, governance,
    and continuous improvement.
•   Supporting Continuous Improvement: The document encourages a phased approach to KPI
    implementation—starting with indicators supported by available data and expanding as systems
    and processes mature.
•   Assessing and Advancing Maintenance Maturity: The KPI framework is scalable and
    adaptable to different levels of asset management maturity. Section 2.7 includes a maturity
    model that HS can use to identify their current level and apply KPIs accordingly.
•   Selecting and Implementing KPIs: The KPI’s included in this document indicate




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                      11



        o   Year 1: those deemed necessary to implement, and by HS of all asset management
            maturity levels to establish a platform for future years.
        o   Year 2: those deemed appropriate for a HS to implement after the initial KPI’s.
        o   Year 2+: those deemed appropriate for future implementation.
Note:
Health Services are encouraged to refer to the full schedule of KPI’s and the AMAF assessment
model, to tailor their approach. These can support the development and enhancement of the
organization’s current capabilities and strategic goals.
The document is structured as follows:

2 KPI framework
A set of 12 KPIs has been outlined for initial implementation based on their relevance, feasibility,
and alignment with strategic objectives across reliability, resilience, compliance, service delivery,
and asset performance. To ensure effective rollout and adoption, the KPIs are intended to be
implemented in two distinct phases as outlined below. Further KPIs may be introduced at a later
stage (Implementation Phase 3), and these are currently not included in this document.


2.1 Implementation phase 1 (year 1): foundational
The first phase includes six KPIs that were deemed to be readily implementable, with minimal
barriers to data collection or system integration, and applicable across all asset management
maturity levels. These KPIs are already supported by existing Facilities Management Software and
processes, making them suitable for immediate deployment, and are summarised in Table 1 below.
A detailed breakdown of each KPI is provided in Appendix 01: KPI Dictionary - Phase 1.
Table 1: KPI Implementation Plan – Phase 1 (Foundational)


                 KPI Name                                               Narrative

 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)                    Easily measurable through Facilities Management Software.


 Reactive Maintenance Response Time            Easily identifiable and trackable via existing systems.

 Maintenance Work Order Backlog                Straightforward to monitor using current software tools.


                                               Supported by system logs and useful for asset replacement

                                               Important for evidencing essential services management.
 Rate

                                               Useful for benchmarking and cost optimisation. Requires
 Maintenance Cost per Bed or Square
                                               consistent unit definitions and accurate cost attribution
 Metre
                                               across facility types.



2.2 Implementation phase 2 (year 2): developmental
The second phase includes five KPIs that require further refinement, definition, or contextual
adaptation before implementation. These KPIs were recognised by DH as valuable but may involve
complexities such as variable asset contexts, reporting depth, or the need for standardised
templates, and are summarised in Table 2 below. A detailed breakdown of each KPI is provided in

3 Performance monitoring framework
3.1 Benefits of performance monitoring
The introduction of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) provides a structured and measurable
approach to improving maintenance performance across Victorian health facilities. The benefits of
implementing KPIs include:
    •   Enhanced Visibility and Accountability
        KPIs offer a transparent view of maintenance performance, enabling Health Services and
        the Department of Health to monitor progress, identify gaps, and hold stakeholders
        accountable for outcomes.
    •   Alignment with Strategic Objectives
        Each KPI is mapped to one or more of the nine strategic objectives. This ensures that
        performance monitoring directly supports roader goals such as reliability, compliance, cost
        efficiency, and service quality.
    •   Support for Continuous Improvement
        By tracking trends over time, KPIs help Health Services identify areas for improvement,
        implement corrective actions, and measure the impact of those changes.
    •   Scalability Across Maturity Levels
        The KPI framework is designed to be scalable and adaptable. Health Services at different
        stages of asset management maturity can implement KPIs progressively, starting with
        foundational metrics and advancing to more complex indicators as capability grows.
    •   Data-Driven Decision Making
        KPIs provide evidence-based insights that support informed decision-making around
        maintenance planning, resource allocation, and risk mitigation.
    •   Benchmarking and Standardisation
        The use of consistent KPIs across the sector enables benchmarking between facilities and
        promotes standardisation of maintenance practices, supporting equity and efficiency.
    •   Regulatory and Policy Compliance
        KPIs help demonstrate compliance with the Asset Management Accountability Framework
        (AMAF) and other relevant standards, reinforcing good governance and risk management.
To realise the benefits outlined above, the KPIs adopted should demonstrate sound design
principles and exhibit the characteristics of well-constructed indicators. These characteristics, such
as strategic alignment, clarity, relevance, and measurability, were considered in the development of
this framework and are further detailed in Appendix 04: Characteristics of “Good” KPIs.


3.1.1 Asset performance
When unexpected asset failures occur, or when asset performance is below expectations (for
example excessive failure rate, low functional performance), Health Services should undertake a
structured approach to identify the cause of such failures. This can be identified through
performance monitoring, as outlined in Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities
Section 2.5, to identify failure events that require review.
Active monitoring of failure rates across sites and asset types, combined with reporting on critical
failure (in accordance with a criticality framework, see Maintenance guidance for Victorian health
facilities Section 4) should be undertaken to enable the appropriate actions and responses to the
failures. This will enable identification of those failures that are either high-rate failures or critical
failures that should be addressed.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                          15



3.1.2 Maintenance strategy
Maintenance strategies and the asset performance should be monitored and reviewed on an
ongoing basis to identify opportunities or requirements for improvement. Health Services may
consider monitoring metrics that relate to cost, risk, and performance to ensure meeting the core
maintenance and organisational objectives.
Establishing metrics or key performance indicators (KPIs) is necessary to monitor the performance
of maintenance strategies. Tracking performance against the KPIs requires access to and analysis
of relevant data.
Some examples of KPIs for monitoring the performance of maintenance strategies are provided in
3.1.4 below, along with guidance regarding the minimum data required. It is assumed that Health
Services have an existing asset register containing basic asset information (including list of all
assets and asset IDs, description, date of purchase, purchase cost, asset class related through an
asset hierarchy, asset location, asset owner, current asset condition) to support performance
monitoring. The acceptable ranges of these KPIs will vary for each Health Service and should be
applicable to the size and scale of the organisation.


3.1.3 Maintenance management
Monitoring maintenance management should be undertaken to ensure the execution of
maintenance is scheduled and in compliance with standards, regulatory requirements, and industry
best practice. Where a contractor agreement is in place for maintenance, the KPIs should be
reviewed in tandem to ensure alignment.


3.1.4 Portfolio strategic oversight
Effective portfolio-level asset management requires structured oversight of maintenance execution
to ensure alignment with organisational standards, regulatory obligations, and industry best
practices. This includes not only verifying that maintenance activities are scheduled and completed
as planned, but also that they contribute meaningfully to the overall performance objectives of the
asset base.
Where maintenance services are delivered through contracted partners, performance should be
continuously evaluated against agreed Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). These KPIs must be
reviewed in conjunction with contract terms to ensure strategic alignment and drive accountability.
To support this, a suite of standardised KPIs, such as Planned Maintenance Percentage (PMP),
and Mean Time to Repair (MTTR), should be monitored across all sites. These indicators provide
visibility into the effectiveness of the maintenance function and enable benchmarking across the
portfolio. Guidance on minimum data requirements for these KPIs is outlined in Table 33.1.4.

 (OEE)



3.2 Asset management maturity
The application of KPIs should be carefully aligned with the “Asset Management” maturity level of
each site. While Category 1 sites are generally expected to demonstrate a higher degree of maturity
than Category 3 sites due to the nature and complexity of their operations this does not mean that
lower-category sites cannot achieve strong asset management performance.
However, it is essential to recognise that not all KPIs are universally applicable or practical across
all maturity levels. Some may be too complex or resource-intensive to implement effectively in
certain contexts. Therefore, KPIs should be selected and applied in a way that is both meaningful
and achievable, enabling operators to extract real value from their assets without being discouraged
by overly sophisticated metrics.
Further detail on how maturity levels inform KPI selection and target setting is provided in Targets
and Benchmarking. For the underlying maturity framework and self-assessment methodology, refer
to Appendix 05: Asset Management Maturity.


3.3 Maturity levels
The Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities refers to three maturity levels
(foundation, established and advanced). These levels will be expanded and evolve over time in line
with the advancing maturity of the engineering and building management sector. Regular reviews
and updates to performance monitoring of maintenance standards are crucial to align with the
maturing landscape of engineering systems and building management practices in Health Services.




                                                OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                       17



3.3.1 Foundation

3.3.1.1 Level 0 - Ad hoc
Minimal or no implementation of engineering systems; primarily manual and paper-based
processes.
Monitoring Focus: Engineering systems and infrastructure are documented. Patient safety
incidents related to assets are reported and investigated.
Example: Essential Safety system is documented manually; incidents are recorded in paper logs
and reviewed only after failures occur.
KPI Example:

    •   Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate: Tracks the percentage of planned
        maintenance tasks completed on schedule, ensuring adherence to maintenance plans.
Equation 1: Planned Maintenance Task Completion Rate

                                                                𝑃𝑙𝑎𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑑 𝐶𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑙𝑒𝑡𝑒𝑑 𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘
         𝑃𝑙𝑎𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑑 𝑀𝑎𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑒 𝑇𝑎𝑠𝑘 𝐶𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑙𝑒𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑒 % =                                  × 100
                                                                  𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑝𝑙𝑎𝑛𝑛𝑒𝑑 𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑘𝑠

3.3.1.2 Level 1 - Initial
Basic use of engineering systems with limited functionality.
Monitoring Focus: Engineering systems are documented digitally and include a minimum data set
to support understanding of system functionality. Maintenance costs for essential services can be
tracked against assets at a group level.
Example: Essential Safety system is documented digitally; basic asset data and incident logs are
stored in spreadsheets or standalone systems with limited integration.
KPI Example:
    •   Asset Register Completeness: Measures the percentage of asset entries in the register that
        are complete and validated.

3.3.2 Established

3.3.2.1 Level 2 – Repeatable
Expanding engineering capabilities with some standardisation.
Monitoring Focus: Engineering systems are documented, and management has commenced
implementing a standardised methodology including nomenclature system. A method for measuring
end-user satisfaction is established and occurs at suitable intervals. Financial monitoring of
maintenance spend can assist service to ensure they remain within a recommend range to prevent
overspending and include a balanced budget, ensure operational and capital spends are balanced.
Example: Essential Safety system documentation includes standardised asset categories and
maintenance procedures; incident data is reviewed periodically to identify recurring issues.
KPI Example:
(To be defined in future updates as new KPIs are introduced)




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                     18



3.3.2.2 Level 3 - Defined
More comprehensive use of engineering systems, integrated platforms, and standardised
processes.

Monitoring Focus: There is a process in place for standardisation of engineering processes.
Systems are integrated and engineering process are standardised allowing monitoring of key
performance indicators for equipment to measure the effectiveness of maintenance strategies.

Example: Essential Safety system costs are tracked monthly; maintenance and incident data are
integrated into a centralised platform and used to monitor performance against budget targets.
Example KPI:
    •   Maintenance Work Order Backlog – Tracks pending work orders to assess
        responsiveness and workload.

3.3.2.3 Level 4 – Managed
Advanced engineering system utilisation, advanced analytics, and continuous improvements.
Monitoring Focus: The coverage of assets monitored for performance covers all core assets and
maintenance may be broken down by activity types. Key Performance indicators are measured, and
the Health Service has set and tracks against targets.
Example: Essential Safety system performance is monitored across all buildings; maintenance
activities are categorised by asset type and prioritised based on service impact.

KPI Example:
(To be defined in future updates as new KPIs are introduced)


3.3.3 Advanced

3.3.3.1 Level 5 – Optimised
Robust engineering system utilisation, advanced analytics, and continuous process improvement.
Monitoring Focus: Performance is monitored to optimise process and ensure continuous
improvement in engineering systems. Performance is measured against targets which is used drive
continuous improvement and analytics of maintenance activities and system use is undertaken.
Example: Essential Safety system failures are analysed using structured RCA; insights are used to
redesign maintenance strategies and reduce recurrence.

Example KPI:

    •   Root Cause Analysis Effectiveness: Evaluates the success of RCA efforts in resolving asset
        failures and preventing recurrence.

                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                         19



3.3.3.2 Level 6 – Innovating
Innovations in engineering systems, stakeholder engagement, and sustainable building
management.

Monitoring Focus: Performance indicators and targets are used to encourage and explore
innovative engineering practices focused on improving clinical services. Maintenance strategies and
performance use remote monitoring.
Example: Essential Safety system is monitored in real time using IoT sensors; predictive analytics
trigger automated alerts for early intervention.
KPI Example:
(To be defined in future updates as new KPIs are introduced)

3.3.3.3 Level 7 – Transforming
Achieving transformation in building management through advanced engineering, focusing on
outcomes, quality, and safety.
Monitoring Focus: Performance indicators and targets are used to drive transformational changes,
outcome improvement and ensure high quality and safety standards in building management are
adaptable to dynamic changes in condition.
Example: Essential Safety system data is integrated with clinical risk systems; asset performance is
linked to patient safety outcomes and informs strategic planning.
KPI Example:
(To be defined in future updates as new KPIs are introduced)


3.4 Implementation approach

3.4.1 Scaled implementation
The KPI framework outlined in this document is designed to be scalable and adaptable to the
diverse operational contexts and maturity levels of Victorian Health Services. Recognising that not
all organisations are at the same stage of asset management capability, the implementation of KPIs
is intended to be progressive and supportive of continuous improvement.
Health Services are encouraged to adopt a phased approach to KPI implementation, beginning
with foundational indicators that align with available data and existing processes. These early-stage
KPIs typically focus on core compliance, reliability, and cost metrics, areas where most services
already have some level of data collection and reporting in place.
As asset management systems mature and data quality improves, services can expand their KPI
suite to include more comprehensive and strategic indicators. These may address lifecycle
costing, service delivery performance, and integration with broader governance frameworks. The
maturity model outlined in 4.2 provides a reference for aligning KPI selection with organisational
capability.
This approach ensures that:
•   Early adopters are not overwhelmed by complex metrics that require advanced systems or
    analytics.
•   All services can participate in performance monitoring, regardless of size or maturity.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                     20



•   Continuous improvement is embedded, with KPIs evolving alongside the organisation’s asset
    management journey.
For a full list of the KPIs recommended for each implementation phase, including their definitions
and implementation considerations, refer to Table 1 (Phase 1: Foundational) and Table 2 (Phase 2:
Developmental) in KPI .
For comprehensive definitions, calculation methods, and data requirements for each KPI, refer to
Appendix 01: KPI Dictionary - Phase 1 and Appendix 02: KPI Dictionary - Phase 2
.


3.5 Data collection
To support the effective implementation of KPIs, it is important to strive for consistent data
measurement practices across all Health Services. Clear definitions for each KPI, outlining the
activities and asset types included, the calculation methodologies, relevant measurement units, and
recommended data sources are necessary.
Providing guidance and training to those involved in data governance can help clarify roles,
responsibilities, and accountabilities related to the collection, validation, and reporting of
maintenance data.
Clear governance structures enhance understanding among stakeholders and help safeguard data
quality, reducing the risk of inconsistencies or gaps that can undermine confidence in reported
metrics. Reliable, comparable data enables Health Services and government bodies to make more
informed, evidence-based decisions, strengthening both operational oversight and long-term
planning.
For detailed definitions, calculation methods, and data requirements for each KPI, as well as
recommended data collection frequencies, refer to Appendix 01: KPI Dictionary - Phase 1 and
Appendix 02: KPI Dictionary - Phase 2.
.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                         21




4 Targets and Benchmarking
4.1 Influencing factors
The Department of Health supports a wide range of healthcare facilities, each with distinct levels of
service demand, asset complexity, and operational maturity.
To ensure that Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) remain meaningful and achievable, it is
recommended that performance targets be flexible and evolve over time. This approach aligns with
the principles of the Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF) and reinforces the
Department’s strategic objectives.
Targets should be structured around core performance principles, namely relevance, measurability,
and achievability. They may be defined across preferred time horizons (e.g. 1, 5, and 10 years) and
refined progressively as data quality improves and systems mature.
In future revisions of this guide, targets and benchmarks will be further defined and applied
to support continuous improvement and alignment with evolving operational contexts.
In shaping these targets, two key contextual factors should be considered:
    •   The facility’s Essential Engineering Services (EES) category
    •   The facility’s current asset management maturity level

This framework enables facilities to align performance expectations with their operational context
and developmental stage. It also encourages broader data visibility and integration with existing
systems, supporting a more tailored and sustainable approach to performance monitoring.


4.2 Maturity levels
Facilities may be assessed across a seven-level maturity scale, ranging from Level 0 – Ad hoc to
Level 7 – Transforming, as summarised below in the Table 4. Each level reflects increasing
sophistication in asset management practices, data utilisation, and performance monitoring
capabilities. To support fair and achievable benchmarking, KPI targets can be calibrated as follows
and this graduated approach encourages facilities to build capability over time, without imposing
unrealistic expectations.
The maturity levels referenced in this section are aligned with the Asset Management Accountability
Framework (AMAF) outlined in Appendix 05: Asset Management Maturity. This appendix provides
further detail on the AMAF requirements, self-assessment methodology, and references supporting
tools that underpin the calibration of KPI targets. Facilities are encouraged to refer to Appendix 05:
Asset Management Maturity details to ensure their performance monitoring approach is consistent
with mandated asset management practices and maturity evaluation protocols.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                                   22



Table 4: Maturity Levels and Target Ambitions



    Maturity Level         Target Ambition                               Description

 Level 0 – Ad hoc         Basic Targets            Recognises the starting point of each service.
                          Basic to Intermediate
 Level 1 – Initial        Targets                  Supports foundational development.
                          Intermediate to          Encourages structured progress, including consistent
 Level 2 – Repeatable     Advanced Targets         practices and early performance tracking.
                          Intermediate to          Enables defined performance management, including
 Level 3 – Defined        Advanced Targets         benchmarking and advanced analytics.
                                                   Drives strategic improvement, potentially incorporating
 Level 4 – Managed        Advanced Targets         predictive maintenance and outcome-based targets.
                                                   Reflects a high-functioning asset management
                                                   environment where continuous improvement is
                                                   embedded. Demonstrates proactive risk mitigation,
                          Advanced to Leading      integrated lifecycle planning, and use of real-time data.
 Level 5 – Optimised      Targets                  Performance is benchmarked externally and internally.
                                                   Represents pioneering services in maintenance and
                                                   asset management. Pilots emerging technologies (e.g.
                                                   IoT, AI-driven diagnostics), contributes to sector-wide
                          Leading to               learning, and influences policy through innovation.
                          Transformational         Targets are dynamic and co-developed with

                                                   management with clinical and community health
                                                   strategies, using predictive analytics and cross-sector
                          Transformational         collaboration. KPIs focus on long-term value, resilience,
 Level 7 – Transforming   Targets                  and sustainability.


4.3 Asset management maturity-based targets
To ensure that KPI targets are both meaningful and achievable, they should be tailored to each
facility’s asset management maturity level. This helps avoid a one-size-fits-all approach and
supports incremental progress.
Key principles include:
    •    Progressive ambition: Targets should grow in complexity as maturity increases.
    •    Capability alignment: KPIs should reflect what is practical given the facility’s systems and
         resources.
    •    Encouragement over enforcement: The framework is designed to inspire improvement,
         not penalise early-stage facilities.
This calibration ensures that all facilities, regardless of starting point, can engage meaningfully with
performance monitoring and improvement.
Target calibration by maturity level is summarised in Table 5 below:

4.4 ESS category level-based targets
Facilities are categorised (e.g. Category 1, 2, 3) based on their infrastructure scale, service scope,
and operational complexity. These categories help contextualise performance expectations.
•      Category 1 facilities (e.g. major metropolitan hospitals) may be better positioned to meet
       advanced targets or implement additional KPIs due to their scale and resources.
•      Category 2 and 3 facilities may focus on foundational or targeted KPIs, recognising their
       smaller size and differing service demands.
This structure ensures that benchmarking remains fair, relevant, and supportive of continuous
improvement across all facility types.




                                                  OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                                24



4.4.1 Illustrative Table
Table 6 illustrates how KPI targets may be adjusted over time, across different EES (Essential
Engineering Services) Categories and maturity levels.
Table 6: KPI Target Calibration by ESS Category, Time Horizon and Maturity Level


                                                                                               Target
            KPI           EES Category          Time Horizon         Maturity Level
                                                                                             Description

                                              1 Year                Level 2 -             Intermediate
                                                                    Repeatable            Target
    KPI 1                Category 1
                                              5 Years               Level 3 - Defined     Advanced Target

                                              10 Years              Level 4 - Managed     Advanced Target

                                              1 Year                Level 1 - Initial     Basic Target

                                              5 Years               Level 3 - Defined     Intermediate
    KPI 2                Category 2
                                                                                          Target

                                              10 Years              Level 4 - Managed     Advanced Target

                                              5 Years               Level 1 - Initial     Intermediate
    KPI 3                Category 3                                                       Target

                                              10 Years              Level 2 -             Intermediate
                                                                    Repeatable            Target



An Advanced Target is assumed for the purpose of this guide for maturity levels including Level 4
and beyond.
Explanation of the Logic:
      Category 1 facilities are generally positioned to begin at a higher asset management maturity
      level (e.g. Level 3 – Defined) and may already be capable of meeting intermediate targets in the
      short term. Over time, these facilities are encouraged to build on their existing capabilities and
      progress toward Level 4 – Managed maturity, with a view to maintaining or enhancing
      performance against more advanced targets.
•     Category 2 facilities may start at a lower or mid-range maturity level (e.g. Level 2 –
      Repeatable). These facilities are well-placed to make meaningful progress over time, with
      targets evolving from basic to advanced systems, processes, and data capabilities mature.
•     Category 3 facilities may often begin at e.g. Level 0 – Ad hoc maturity, where formal systems
      may be limited. Initial targets for these facilities are likely to focus on foundational practices, with
      the opportunity to reach intermediate performance levels by Year 10, aligned with a gradual
      progression toward Level 2 – Repeatable or higher.




                                                  OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                         25



4.5 Target timeline horizons
KPI targets may be structured across short, medium, and long-term horizons to reflect evolving
expectations and organisational growth and with this phased approach allows facilities to plan and
progress at a sustainable pace, with targets reviewed and refined periodically. Table 7 outlines the
KPI target timeline horizons examples:
Table 7: KPI Target Timeline Horizons


  Time Horizon                                              Purpose

 1 Year              Focus on immediate operational improvements and compliance baselines.

 5 Years             Support strategic alignment and capability building.

 10 Years            Reflect aspirational goals tied to innovation and system-wide transformation.




                                                 OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                                   26




5 Insights from combined KPI’s
While individual KPIs offer targeted insights into specific dimensions of maintenance performance,
such as responsiveness, reliability, or compliance, their combined analysis reveals deeper, more
strategic patterns. By examining multiple KPIs in conjunction, Health Services can move beyond
isolated metrics to uncover interdependencies, systemic strengths, and hidden inefficiencies across
their asset management practices.
This integrated approach enables:
•      Holistic performance evaluation: Understanding how operational metrics interact to influence
       strategic outcomes.
•      Proactive decision-making: Identifying early warning signals and opportunities for
       improvement that may not be visible through single indicators.
•      Benchmarking and maturity mapping: Aligning performance trends with asset management
       maturity levels and strategic objectives.
Table 8 below illustrates how selected KPI combinations can generate actionable insights across
both operational and strategic domains:

Appendices




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                         28




Appendix 01: KPI Dictionary - Phase 1
The following sections of this appendix will explain and detail each of the KPIs individually.


KPI 1: Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)
Purpose
MTTR measures the average time taken to repair an asset following a failure. Within DH
maintenance operations, it reflects the responsiveness and efficiency of maintenance teams in
restoring critical systems such as HVAC, electrical infrastructure, fire detection systems, and water
supply pumps. MTTR may refer to the time taken to restore service, which can include temporary
fixes, or to fully remediate the issue with a permanent repair, depending on organisational
definitions. In this case, MTTR will be measured based on full restoration of the asset. This KPI
applies only to critical assets and is intended for use at Foundation Level 0 maturity, where
monitoring is ad-hoc and focused on essential systems.

Benefits & Outcomes
   • Minimises downtime, ensuring continuity of patient care.
   • Enhances maintenance team performance and accountability.
   • Supports resource planning and contractor performance evaluation.
   • Enables benchmarking across facilities and asset types.

Main Objectives Alignment
Reliability & Resilience, Quality and Performance, Service Delivery & Customer Service,
Maintenance Personnel Performance.

Performance Insight
Lower MTTR values indicate faster recovery and better operational resilience. Monthly trends are
typically monitored against a 4-hour target (See also “Target / Benchmark” below). Persistent delays
may signal process inefficiencies or resource constraints.

Minimum Maturity Level
Foundation – Level 0: Ad-hoc.

Calculation

                                                𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑅𝑒𝑝𝑎𝑖𝑟 𝑇𝑖𝑚𝑒
                                     𝑀𝑇𝑇𝑅 =
                                                𝑁𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝑅𝑒𝑝𝑎𝑖𝑟𝑠

Definition of Terms:
   • Total Repair Time is the cumulative duration from failure to restoration of the asset. This
        includes the time from when the failure is first reported to when the asset is operational
        again.
   • Number of Repairs is the count of repair incidents in the reporting period. Only repairs
        classified under critical asset categories




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                           29



Example Calculation:
Consider a scenario where a particular asset requires five repairs within a month, resulting in a total
downtime of 50 hours. The Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) is calculated by dividing the total
downtime by the number of repair events:
                                            50 ℎ𝑜𝑢𝑟𝑠
                                  𝑀𝑇𝑇𝑅 =              = 10 ℎ𝑜𝑢𝑟𝑠
                                            5 𝑟𝑒𝑝𝑎𝑖𝑟𝑠
This indicates that, on average, it takes 10 hours to complete each repair and restore the equipment
to operational status.

Data Collection
   • Source Systems: CMMS, technician logs, work orders, service reports, technician details.
   • Inputs Required: Asset ID, failure and repair timestamps.
   • Frequency:
          o Data Collection: Monthly.
          o Reports: Three-Monthly.
   • Responsible Parties: Site maintenance teams and performance monitoring unit.

Data Quality Notes
Ensure accurate logging of start and end times.

Target / Benchmark
Industry practices recognise that MTTR targets can vary significantly depending on the asset’s
criticality and operational context. For example, highly critical equipment may require restoration
within as little as 15 minutes, while other critical systems may have a 2-hour target. Less critical
assets may allow up to 5 hours. These thresholds are often driven by service level agreements
(SLAs) with subcontractors and should be tailored to the specific asset type and facility
requirements.

For high-level performance analysis, assets of similar criticality could be grouped to determine an
overall MTTR average value for each criticality level.

Reference Target: ≤ 2 hours, tailored to the Health Service location and category.

KPI Question for Scoresheet: Has the mean time to repair remained within the 2 hours in average?

Further Notes
MTTR is a straightforward to implement due to its compatibility with facilities management systems.
Its simplicity and relevance to operational continuity make it ideal for early adoption, especially in
environments with limited data maturity.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                        30



KPI 2: Reactive Maintenance SLA Compliance
Purpose
Measures the percentage of reactive (unplanned) asset-related incidents resolved within the defined
Service Level Agreement (SLA) timeframes. This KPI ensures timely response and resolution of
maintenance issues, which is critical in hospital environments where delays can impact patient care
and safety. Reactive Maintenance Response Time works especially well when SLA targets are
defined for reactive maintenance. For example:
    • Critical issues may require a response within 30 minutes.
    • High-priority issues within 1 hour.
    • Routine issues within 24 hours.
This structure supports prioritisation, risk reduction, and service continuity, especially important in
hospital environments where delays can directly impact patient care.

Service Level Agreement (SLA) targets are defined based on a combination of internal policy
frameworks and contractual obligations with external service providers. These targets may vary
depending on:
    • Asset type: Critical assets often have stricter SLA timeframes due to their direct impact on
        patient care.
    • Location: Site-specific operational requirements or infrastructure limitations may influence
        SLA expectations.
In cases where maintenance is fully outsourced and no formal documentation exists, health
services may baseline their SLA strategies using current service provision and adapt them to the
asset’s operational context, by ensuring SLA targets are both realistic and take into account factors
such as asset location, risk exposure, and critical functionality assessments, aligning service
expectations with operational priorities and patient safety requirements.

Benefits & Outcomes
   • Improves responsiveness and service reliability.
   • Supports prioritisation of incidents based on criticality.
   • Enhances operational resilience and risk management.
   • Enables performance benchmarking and contractor accountability.

Main Objectives Alignment
Reliability & Resilience, Compliance & Risk Management, Quality and Performance, Service
Delivery & Customer Service, Communication, Maintenance Personnel Performance.

Performance Insight
High SLA compliance rates indicate effective and timely resolution of reactive maintenance
incidents. Monthly tracking helps identify delays, resource gaps, and process inefficiencies.

Minimum Maturity Level
Foundation – Level 0: Ad-hoc
Minimal or no implementation of engineering systems; primarily manual and paper-based
processes.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                           31



Calculation
                                               𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑣𝑒𝑑 𝑤𝑖𝑡ℎ𝑖𝑛 𝑆𝐿𝐴
                    𝑆𝐿𝐴 𝐶𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑙𝑖𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑒 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑒 =                                    𝑋 100
                                                  𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑟𝑒𝑎𝑐𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒 𝑖𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠
Where:
 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑣𝑒𝑑 𝑤𝑖𝑡ℎ𝑖𝑛 𝑆𝐿𝐴 = 𝑁𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝑖𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑤ℎ𝑒𝑟𝑒 (𝑅𝑒𝑠𝑜𝑙𝑢𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑇𝑖𝑚𝑒 − 𝑅𝑒𝑝𝑜𝑟𝑡𝑒𝑑 𝑇𝑖𝑚𝑒) ≤ 𝑆𝐿𝐴 𝑇𝑎𝑟𝑔𝑒𝑡



Definition of Terms:
    •    Incidents Resolved Within SLA: Number of reactive incidents where the actual resolution
         time is less than or equal to the SLA timeframe based on priority level.
    •    Total Reactive Incidents: All unplanned maintenance incidents reported during the period,
         regardless of resolution time.
    •    Reported Time: The timestamp indicating when a reactive maintenance incident was first
         logged or reported in the system. This marks the beginning of the response window and is
         used as the starting point for measuring compliance with SLA targets.
    •    Resolution Time: Refers to the total duration taken to fully resolve a reactive maintenance
         incident, calculated from the Reported Time (when the incident is first logged) to the
         moment the asset is restored to service.
Determine the SLA timeframe for each reactive incident based on its priority (e.g., critical = 30
minutes, high priority = 1 hour, routine = 24 hours).
This KPI is binary (met/not met), which simplifies performance tracking and enables clear trend
analysis over time. It also supports consistent comparison across departments or contractors,
helping identify areas for improvement and best practice sharing.
Example Calculation:
If there were 100 reactive incidents in a month, and 85 of them were resolved within their respective
SLA timeframes, the SLA Compliance Rate would be:
                                                          85
                               𝑆𝐿𝐴 𝐶𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑙𝑖𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑒 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑒 =          𝑋 100 = 85%
                                                          100
Priority-based SLA targets example:
    •    Critical: 30 mins
    •    High: 1 hour
    •    Routine: 24 hours


Incident Example:
    •    Reported: 08:00
    •    Resolution: 08:25
    •    Priority: Critical
    •    SLA Target: 30 mins
    •    Resolution Time: 25 mins → Resolved within SLA

Data Collection
    •    Source Systems: CMMS incident logs.
    •    Inputs Required: Incident ID, reported time, resolution time, priority level.
    •    Data Collection and Reporting Frequency: Monthly.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                    32



    •   Responsible Parties: Maintenance teams and performance monitoring units
    •   Data Quality Notes: Ensure accurate logging of incident timestamps and priority
        classification.

Target / Benchmark
The ≥80% SLA compliance target for reactive maintenance in healthcare settings is well-supported
by a combination of industry standards, benchmarking studies, and regulatory expectations, making
it a reasonable and achievable benchmark.
Has at least 80% of reactive maintenance incidents been resolved within their defined SLA
timeframe?
Further Notes
This KPI is binary in nature, each incident either meets or fails the SLA. It is essential for
maintaining service standards and improving response efficiency. Strong alignment with operational
performance frameworks makes it suitable for early adoption and continuous improvement.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                      33



KPI 3: Maintenance Work Order Backlog
Purpose
Tracks the number of pending maintenance work orders, reflecting responsiveness and workload
management.
Benefits & Outcomes
    •   Indicates maintenance efficiency and resource sufficiency.
    •   Supports prioritisation and scheduling.
    •   Reduces risk of delayed maintenance and asset failure.

Main Objectives Alignment
Reliability & Resilience, Compliance & Risk Management, Quality and Performance.
Performance Insight
Lower backlog percentages reflect better responsiveness and maintenance efficiency. According to
industry standards, monthly reviews are essential not only for tracking performance but also for
identifying bottlenecks, such as excessive work demand, inefficient execution, or insufficient
resources, that contribute to backlog accumulation. These reviews help uncover resource
gaps (e.g., lack of skilled personnel, delays in spare parts or supplier availability) and enable
proactive planning to address them. By analysing trends in deferred vs. unmanaged backlog,
organisations can assess risk exposure and determine whether backlog is being effectively
managed or if it's introducing unmanaged risk.
Minimum Maturity Level
Established – Level 3: Defined.
Calculation
                                               𝑁𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝑃𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑊𝑜𝑟𝑘 𝑂𝑟𝑑𝑒𝑟𝑠
                 𝑊𝑜𝑟𝑘 𝑂𝑟𝑑𝑒𝑟 𝐵𝑎𝑐𝑘𝑙𝑜𝑔 % =                                      𝑋 100
                                                    𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑊𝑜𝑟𝑘 𝑂𝑟𝑑𝑒𝑟𝑠


Definition of Terms:
    •   Pending Work Orders:
        These are reactive or preventive maintenance tasks that have been logged but not yet
        completed. This includes work orders that are open, assigned, or in progress but not closed.
    •   Total Work Orders:
        The sum of all maintenance work orders generated during the reporting period, including
        both completed and pending tasks.
Example Calculation:
Reporting Period: One month
    •   Total Work Orders Created: 200
    •   Pending Work Orders at Month-End: 18
        (Includes both on-time and overdue work orders)


                                                            18
                              𝑊𝑜𝑟𝑘 𝑂𝑟𝑑𝑒𝑟 𝐵𝑎𝑐𝑘𝑙𝑜𝑔 % =            𝑋100 = 9%
                                                            200




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                      34




The backlog is within the target benchmark of ≤10%, indicating good responsiveness and workload
management.
For deeper insight, the 18 pending work orders can be further segmented into:
    •    12 on-time
    •    6 overdue
Data Collection
    •    Source Systems: CMMS work order logs.
    •    Inputs Required: Work order ID, status, age.
    •    Data Collection and Reporting Frequency: Monthly.
    •    Responsible Parties: Maintenance teams.

Data Quality Notes
Prioritise by criticality.
Target / Benchmark
≤ 10% of total work orders.
KPI Question for Scoresheet: Has the maintenance work order backlog remained below 10% of
total work orders?
Further Notes
Work order backlog is a reliable indicator of maintenance responsiveness and is well-supported by
current digital systems. It reflects practicality and value in identifying resource constraints and
operational bottlenecks.




                                               OFFICIAL
Maintenance guidance for Victorian health facilities, Performance Monitoring                         35



KPI 4: Repeated Failures
Purpose
Measures the recurrence of asset failures, indicating unresolved issues or ineffective maintenance
strategies. This KPI acts as a feedback loop for reliability improvement.


Benefits & Outcomes
    •   Highlights assets requiring deeper investigation.
    •   Supports targeted interventions and RCA (Root Cause Analysis).
    •   Improves asset reliability and performance.

Main Objectives Alignment
Reliability & Resilience, Quality and Performance.


Performance Insight
Monthly tracking helps identify problematic assets. Lower counts of repeated failures reflect
effective maintenance. Repeated failures trigger RCA investigations for assets with high recurrence,
ensuring that systemic issues are properly understood and addressed, assesses whether RCA has
worked, and helps shift maintenance strategies from reactive to proactive. Its integration into
performance tracking strengthens the case for structured problem-solving and continuous
improvement.


Minimum Maturity Level
Established – Level 3: Defined


Calculation
                  

[... content truncated at 80,000 characters ...]

## C14608 - Request for Proposal - KPI Implementation.docx

*File: `Client Docs/C14608 - Request for Proposal - KPI Implementation.docx`*

Request for Proposal (RFP)

issued under the

Master Supply Agreement for the Professional Advisory Services: GP-12-2024

Seeks advice related to:

SECTION 1: Key Information

SECTION 2: Engagement Requirements

INTRODUCTION

Department of Health

The Department of Health (the department) plays a critical role in the Victorian health system and is responsible for shaping it to meet the health needs of Victorians into the future. 

For further information refer to the department’s website https://www.health.vic.gov.au/about   

System Planning Division 

The System Planning Division provides an integrated and joined-up approach to Victorian health system planning, reform, and leadership to support delivery of strategic reform priorities across the Victorian health system. The division integrates the department’s approach to strategic and systemic long-term reform and includes integrated approaches to system and service planning as well as asset management. The division is focused on health system structural reform, intergovernmental relations, and action to address climate threats, and responses to priority challenges and projects as they emerge. 

Health System and Asset Planning Branch 

The Health System and Asset Planning branch within the System Planning Division brings together infrastructure Investment Strategy, Asset Planning, and the Health Infrastructure Client Control Office to support an integrated approach to asset management, system, service, and clinical planning. Together the branch is responsible for managing system infrastructure risks and undertaking capital investment planning on a portfolio level. Climate and land policy functions, ensure the branch integrates broader government directions and objectives into infrastructure planning. 

The function of the branch clearly delineates the role of the department as an infrastructure client and steward of the health infrastructure asset management system. Under this model: 

The Victorian Infrastructure Delivery Authority (VIDA) is responsible for delivering health infrastructure at the direction of the department to meet its current and future service needs, and 

Health service providers are the day-to-day operators and maintainers of assets and infrastructure. They are operational experts who are accountable for the asset’s reliability and availability to support the delivery of high-quality health outcomes.

Asset Planning Unit

The Asset Planning Unit is responsible for the asset management function within the department under a stewardship model, establishing and implementing strategic asset management capabilities across Victoria’s health sector. The unit is responsible for monitoring and facilitating compliance of the department and health agencies with the Asset Management Accountability Framework (AMAF). The Asset Planning unit provides guidance of sector practice and the setting of asset management standards. This includes policy and reform initiatives that have a strong focus on health infrastructure planning, data capability, risk management, and performance monitoring. The unit aims to equip Victorian health services with the skills and knowledge to optimise their asset lifecycle and enable delivery of quality care.

BACKGROUND

The Department of Health, as the steward of Victoria’s public health infrastructure, is committed to ensuring that all health facilities operate at optimal standards of safety, reliability, and efficiency. Across the state, 77 public health services manage a diverse portfolio of assets, each employing a range of engineering maintenance models—from fully in-house teams to fully outsourced service arrangements. This diversity, while reflecting local needs and capacities, presents challenges in achieving consistent asset performance and maintenance outcomes.

To address these challenges and drive sector-wide improvement, the Department has developed comprehensive Engineering Standard Maintenance Guidance. Central to this initiative is the implementation of 12 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), which have been carefully designed to:

Establish clear, measurable standards for asset maintenance across all Victorian public health services.

Enable consistent and transparent monitoring of maintenance performance, supporting benchmarking and continuous improvement.

Provide an evidence-based foundation for strategic asset planning and investment decisions ensuring resources (particularly capital funds) are directed to areas of the greatest impact.

Support compliance with best practice maintenance standards and inform actions that reduce risk and sustain the reliability and availability of critical health infrastructure., reducing risk and enhancing the reliability and availability of critical health infrastructure.

Tailor technical support and guidance to the unique needs of individual health services, fostering capability development and knowledge sharing across the sector.

By supporting health services to implement these KPIs, the Department enables informed, risk-based decision-making that strengthens operational and financial planning. This approach provides a consistent evidence base for prioritising maintenance and renewal, ensuring investment decisions deliver maximum value and align with government objectives for sustainability, resilience, and accountability.

PURPOSE

The purpose of this project is to engage a qualified and experienced contractor who will collaborate closely with relevant stakeholders. The contractor will work alongside stakeholders, utilising their existing systems and data capturing processes, to implement the defined Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) through a phased approach. This collaborative effort will ensure that the implementation is tailored to the operational realities of each stakeholder group, supports effective data-driven decision-making, and enables continuous improvement in engineering maintenance standards across the sector.

REQUIREMENTS AND DELVERABLES

The contractor will deliver a phased engagement, structured as follows:

Phase 1: Discovery & Assessment

Comprehensive Review: Undertake a systematic assessment of maintenance models, systems, and processes across pilot health services.

Stakeholder Mapping: The Department of Health (DH) will systematically identify and provide comprehensive details of all key stakeholders, including operational leads and asset managers, to enable targeted and effective engagement throughout the program lifecycle.

Readiness Evaluation: Assess organizational and operational readiness for KPI implementation, including data capability and resource availability.

Phase 2: Data Analysis & Benchmarking

Data Collection: Gather and validate maintenance data from participating health services.

Benchmarking: Analyse data to identify sector-wide strengths, weaknesses, and gaps; compare against best practice standards.

Maturity Segmentation: In collaboration with the Department of Health, health services will be systematically classified into distinct cohorts according to their asset management maturity and operational capability. Based on the data analysis, we will define the appropriate size of the pilot health services (HS) group and identify the targeted health services for participation in the pilot phase.

Phase 3: Engagement & Co-Design

Stakeholder Consultation: Facilitate workshops, interviews, and surveys to capture insights, challenges, and local innovations.

Collaborative Design: Co-develop tailored support options and action plans with stakeholders, ensuring alignment with operational realities.

Phase 4: Pilot Implementation

Pilot Planning: Design and execute a pilot phase with selected health services, including direct consultation and workshops.

Monitoring & Feedback: Collect pilot data, evaluate effectiveness, and document lessons learned.

Phase 5: Full Implementation & Continuous Improvement

Refinement: Incorporate pilot feedback to finalize implementation and action plans.

Change Management: Develop and deliver communication and engagement strategies to support sector-wide adoption.

Sustainability: Establish mechanisms for ongoing monitoring, evaluation, and continuous improvement.

The contractor will provide the following, structured for clarity and accountability.

ESTIMATED ENGAGEMENT TIMELINE

PROPOSED PROJECT MANAGEMENT / OUTLINE OF KEY STAKEHOLDERS AND OTHER PROJECT ADVISERS: 

The successful bidder will be required to work directly with the Asset Planning unit within the System Planning Division. In addition, engagement will be required across other departments and Health Services.

EVALUATION CRITERIA

The Service Provider will be required to address the criteria in the table below which will be included in the evaluation. Criteria should be designed to specifically reflect the unique requirements of your project. 

Weightings are optional and a decision concerning the use of these must be made by the Engagement Manager. If weightings are used, they must be disclosed in the RFP so that Service Providers are aware of the relative importance of the criteria in preparing their response. The importance or weighting of each criterion may be assigned with a percentage weighting to indicate the relative importance of the criteria.

If Local Job First policy applies, then weightings must be used. It is mandatory to give LJF a 20% weighting. Please discuss with your Procurement Officer for further advice of Local Job First.

EVALUATION PROCESS

Bidders will be scored against the following scale:

An initial evaluation may be used to shortlist proposals.  Following shortlisting, one or more bidders may be interviewed to provide clarification or further information.  Shortlisted bidders may be invited, as part of the evaluation process, to submit a best and final offer in relation to all or certain aspects of their respective proposals.

All bidders will be advised of the final outcome of the selection process.

OTHER CONDITIONS/REQUIREMENTS

Communications

All communications, questions or requests for clarification or further information relating to this RFP and/or the RFP process must be directed to the Buyer in the Digital Marketplace during the allotted time period.  

Unauthorized or inappropriate communications may lead to the disqualification of the Service Provider from participating in the RFP. 

Service Providers must ensure that they and their representatives do not make or provide any inducement, payment or promise to the Buyer or any of its personnel in connection with this RFP or the Services requested under it. 

A breach of this clause will constitute a material breach of the Master Supply Agreement (MSA) not capable of being remediate and may result in the State terminating the MSA or a State Purchasing Entity terminating a Purchase Order Contract in accordance with clause 22.2 of the MSA.

Information Security

[If the supplier is accessing, generating, storing, or modifying departmental information data or systems as a part of the proposed services, an Information Security Classification (ISC) must be completed to determine the Protective Marking of the information.

Bidders are required to complete the department's Information Security Standards Requirements Assessment (ISSRA) at Attachment 1, to assess their information security maturity against the department's Third-Party Information Security Standards. The completed ISSRA must be included as an attachment to the bidder’s proposal.

The Third-Party Standard 8b for Externals attached as Attachment 2 to this RFQ outlines the department’s expectations for third parties engaged to work with our public sector for information, data, or IT systems.
